questions.prompt: What is `rustup`?

options.A: The Rust compiler that turns `.rs` files into executables
options.B: A command-line tool for managing Rust versions and associated tools
options.C: Rust's package manager for downloading third-party crates
options.D: A code formatter that enforces the standard Rust style

questions.explanation: `rustup` is the installer and toolchain manager: it installs Rust and lets you update it with `rustup update` or remove it with `rustup self uninstall`. The compiler is `rustc`, the package manager is Cargo, and the formatter is `rustfmt`.

questions.prompt: After installing Rust, which command verifies that the compiler is installed correctly and prints its version?

options.A: rustc --version
options.B: rust --version
options.C: rustup verify
options.D: cargo --install-check

questions.explanation: The book verifies the installation with `rustc --version`, which prints the version, commit hash, and commit date of the latest stable release. There is no `rust` binary or `rustup verify` command.

questions.prompt: You get "linker not found" errors when compiling a fresh Rust installation on macOS or Linux. According to the book, what is the fix?

options.A: Reinstall Rust, because the download was corrupted
options.B: Install a C compiler, which typically ships with the linker Rust needs
options.C: Run `rustup doc` to download the missing linker component
options.D: Switch from `rustc` to Cargo, which bundles its own linker

questions.explanation: Rust needs a linker to join its compiled outputs into one file, and it does not ship one. Installing a C compiler (e.g. `xcode-select --install` on macOS, `build-essential` on Ubuntu) provides a linker; Cargo does not bundle its own.

questions.prompt: In the Hello, World! program, what does the `!` in `println!` signify?

options.A: It marks the call as one that can never fail
options.B: It calls a macro instead of a normal function
options.C: It forces the output to flush immediately to the terminal
options.D: It indicates the call returns no value

questions.explanation: The `!` means `println!` is a macro, not a function; macros generate code and don't always follow the same rules as functions. A plain function would be called as `println` without the `!`.

questions.prompt: You run `rustc main.rs` on Linux and it succeeds, but nothing prints to the screen. Why?

options.A: `rustc` only checks the code; you must use Cargo to produce a binary
options.B: Compiling and running are separate steps: `rustc` only produces an executable, which you then run with `./main`
options.C: The program printed to a log file because `println!` doesn't write to the terminal
options.D: The binary was placed in `target/debug/`, so it must be run from there

questions.explanation: Rust is an ahead-of-time compiled language: `rustc main.rs` outputs an executable (`main` on Linux/macOS, `main.exe` on Windows) without running it. `target/debug/` is where Cargo, not `rustc`, puts binaries.

questions.prompt: This program fails to compile. What is the problem?

options.A: `main` must declare a return type before its body
options.B: String literals must use single quotes in Rust
options.C: `println` is missing the `!` — the macro is named `println!`, and no function `println` is in scope
options.D: The call is missing a format argument such as `{}`

questions.explanation: Printing to the screen in this program uses the `println!` macro; writing `println` without `!` makes the compiler look for an ordinary function of that name, which doesn't exist in scope, so compilation fails.

questions.prompt: What does `cargo new hello_cargo` create?

options.A: Only an empty `Cargo.toml` file in the current directory
options.B: A `hello_cargo` directory containing `Cargo.toml`, a `src` directory with `main.rs`, and a new Git repository
options.C: A compiled `hello_cargo` executable in `target/debug/`
options.D: A `hello_cargo` directory with `main.rs` at its top level and a `Cargo.lock` file

questions.explanation: `cargo new` generates the project directory with a `Cargo.toml` manifest, `src/main.rs` containing a Hello, World! program, and initializes a Git repo (with `.gitignore`) by default. `Cargo.lock` and `target/` only appear after the first `cargo build`.

questions.prompt: Which statements about Cargo commands are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `cargo check` verifies the code compiles without producing an executable
options.B: `cargo run` always recompiles the project, even if the source hasn't changed
options.C: `cargo build` places the resulting binary in `target/debug/` by default
options.D: `cargo build` produces an optimized release binary by default
options.E: `cargo run` compiles the code if needed and then runs the resulting executable in one command

questions.explanation: `cargo check` skips producing a binary, making it faster for iterating; the default profile is debug, so `cargo build` outputs to `target/debug/`. `cargo run` builds only when files have changed — if nothing changed it just runs the existing binary — and optimized builds require `cargo build --release`.

questions.prompt: A teammate says: "Before benchmarking, just run `cargo build` and time the binary in `target/debug/`." Why is this advice wrong?

options.A: Benchmarks must be run with `cargo check`, which produces faster code
options.B: Debug builds compile without optimizations; benchmarks should use `cargo build --release` and the binary in `target/release/`
options.C: `target/debug/` binaries cannot be executed directly; they can only be launched via `cargo run`
options.D: The debug binary panics when run outside of Cargo, invalidating any timing

questions.explanation: The book notes that `cargo build --release` compiles with optimizations into `target/release/`, and that you should benchmark the release build because the debug profile trades runtime speed for faster compilation. Debug binaries are ordinary executables and run fine directly.

questions.prompt: Which statements about a project's `Cargo.toml` and `Cargo.lock` files are true? (Select all that apply.)
questions.code:
[package]
name = "hello_cargo"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"

[dependencies]

options.A: `Cargo.toml` is written in the TOML format
options.B: The `[dependencies]` section lists the crates the project depends on
options.C: `Cargo.lock` is created by `cargo new` alongside `Cargo.toml`
options.D: You should hand-edit `Cargo.lock` to pin dependency versions
options.E: The `[package]` section must list every source file so Cargo knows what to compile

questions.explanation: `Cargo.toml` uses TOML, with `[package]` configuring the package and `[dependencies]` listing crates. `Cargo.lock` first appears when you run `cargo build` and is managed by Cargo — you never need to edit it manually — and Cargo finds source files by convention in `src/` rather than from a file list.

questions.prompt: In `let mut guess = String::new();`, why is the `mut` keyword required?

options.A: Variables in Rust are immutable by default, and `read_line` needs to modify `guess` later
options.B: All `String` values must be declared with `mut` because strings are growable
options.C: `mut` tells the compiler to allocate the string on the heap instead of the stack
options.D: Without `mut`, the variable would be a constant that must be known at compile time

questions.explanation: Rust variables are immutable by default; `mut` makes `guess` mutable so `read_line` can append the user's input to it. `String` values do not require `mut` in general, and `mut` has nothing to do with heap allocation.

questions.prompt: What does the `::` syntax in `String::new()` indicate?

options.A: `new` is a macro defined inside the `String` module
options.B: `new` is an associated function implemented on the `String` type
options.C: `new` is a method being called on an existing `String` instance
options.D: `new` is a keyword that constructs any type, similar to `new` in other languages

questions.explanation: The `::` syntax indicates that `new` is an associated function of the `String` type — a function implemented on a type rather than on an instance. Rust has no `new` keyword; `new` is just a conventional constructor name.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about `io::stdin().read_line(&mut guess)` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: It appends the user's input to the string rather than overwriting it
options.B: It requires a mutable reference because it needs to change the string's contents
options.C: It returns a `Result` value that should be handled
options.D: It automatically strips the trailing newline from the input
options.E: It converts the input to a number if the string is annotated with a numeric type

questions.explanation: `read_line` appends input to the provided `String` (it does not overwrite), takes `&mut` because it mutates the string, and returns a `Result` (`Ok`/`Err`). It keeps the trailing newline — that's why the tutorial calls `trim()` — and it never performs numeric conversion.

questions.prompt: This code fails to compile. Why?

options.A: `read_line` expects `&mut String`, but `&guess` is an immutable reference
options.B: `guess` must be declared without `mut` when passing a reference to it
options.C: `expect` cannot be called on the return value of `read_line`
options.D: `io::stdin()` must be stored in a variable before calling `read_line`

questions.explanation: References are immutable by default, so `&guess` is a `&String` while `read_line` needs `&mut String` to modify the string; the fix is `&mut guess`. Declaring the variable with `mut` alone is not enough — the reference itself must also be mutable.

questions.prompt: What does calling `.expect("Failed to read line")` on a `Result` value do?

options.A: If the `Result` is `Err`, it crashes the program and displays the given message; if `Ok`, it returns the value inside
options.B: It prints the message to the screen every time the line is executed
options.C: It retries the failed operation until it succeeds, logging the message on each failure
options.D: It silently discards any error and returns a default value

questions.explanation: `expect` unwraps the `Ok` value on success; if the `Result` is an `Err`, it causes the program to crash (panic) and displays the message passed to it. It neither retries nor substitutes a default.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about Cargo dependencies and `Cargo.lock` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Adding `rand = "0.8.5"` under `[dependencies]` makes Cargo download the crate from crates.io
options.B: `Cargo.lock` records the exact dependency versions used, so future builds are reproducible
options.C: You must edit `Cargo.lock` by hand whenever you want to upgrade a dependency
options.D: `cargo update` upgrades dependencies to newer versions allowed by the constraints in `Cargo.toml`
options.E: `Cargo.lock` is regenerated from scratch on every `cargo build`, so versions may change each build

questions.explanation: Cargo fetches dependencies listed in `Cargo.toml` from crates.io, and `Cargo.lock` (created on the first build) pins exact versions for reproducible builds. You never edit `Cargo.lock` manually — `cargo update` moves versions forward within the `Cargo.toml` constraints — and Cargo reuses the lock file rather than regenerating it each build.

questions.prompt: In the guessing game, what range of numbers can `rand::thread_rng().gen_range(1..=100)` produce?

options.A: 1 through 100, inclusive of both endpoints
options.B: 1 through 99, because the upper bound is exclusive
options.C: 0 through 100, because ranges start at zero by default
options.D: 2 through 100, because the lower bound is exclusive

questions.explanation: The `1..=100` syntax is a range inclusive on both ends, so `gen_range` returns a number between 1 and 100 including both. The exclusive-upper-bound form would be `1..101`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: It prints `You guessed: 42`
options.B: It fails to compile because `guess` is declared twice
options.C: It panics with `Please type a number!` because the string contains whitespace
options.D: It prints `You guessed:  42` including the leading space

questions.explanation: Shadowing lets the second `let guess` reuse the name with a new type (`u32`), and `trim()` removes the surrounding whitespace and newline before `parse()`, so parsing succeeds and `42` is printed. Redeclaring with `let` is legal shadowing, not an error.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `Too small!`
options.B: `Too big!`
options.C: `You win!`
options.D: It fails to compile because `cmp` needs two references

questions.explanation: `guess.cmp(&secret_number)` compares `guess` (100) to `secret_number` (50); since 100 is greater, the result is `Ordering::Greater` and the `Too big!` arm runs. `cmp` is called on the value with one reference argument, which is fine.

questions.prompt: This code fails to compile. Why?

options.A: The compiler cannot infer which numeric type `parse` should produce, so a type annotation like `let guess: u32` is needed
options.B: `parse` cannot be called on the result of `trim` because `trim` returns a `&str`
options.C: Shadowing a `String` with a number is not allowed; the new variable needs a different name
options.D: `expect` cannot be chained directly after `parse`; the `Result` must be matched first

questions.explanation: `parse` can convert a string into many number types, so without a target type such as `let guess: u32` (or a turbofish like `parse::<u32>()`), the compiler reports that type annotations are needed. Shadowing across types is allowed, and `parse` is normally called on the `&str` returned by `trim`.

questions.prompt: What happens when this program runs?

options.A: It panics with the message `Please type a number!`
options.B: It prints `You guessed: 5`
options.C: It fails to compile because `parse` cannot return a `u32`
options.D: It prints `You guessed: 5` followed by a blank line

questions.explanation: The string still contains the trailing newline, and parsing `"5\n"` as a `u32` fails, so `parse` returns `Err` and `expect` panics with the given message. This is exactly why the tutorial calls `trim()` before `parse()`.

questions.prompt: A learner says: "To convert `guess` from a `String` to a `u32`, we must mark it `mut`, because its type is changing." What is wrong with this claim?

options.A: Nothing — a variable's type can only change if it is mutable
options.B: The tutorial uses shadowing: `let guess: u32 = ...` creates a brand-new variable that reuses the name, so no `mut` is needed for the conversion
options.C: The claim is wrong because `mut` is never allowed on `String` variables
options.D: The claim is wrong because Rust converts the type automatically when you compare a `String` with a number

questions.explanation: Mutability never allows a variable's type to change; instead, the tutorial shadows `guess` with a new `let` binding of type `u32`, reusing the name rather than mutating the old variable. Rust also performs no implicit `String`-to-number conversions.

questions.prompt: By default, what is the mutability of a variable declared with `let` in Rust?

options.A: Mutable, unless marked with `const`
options.B: Immutable, unless marked with `mut`
options.C: Mutable inside functions, immutable at module level
options.D: It depends on whether the type implements `Copy`

questions.explanation: Variables are immutable by default in Rust; you must opt in to mutation by writing `let mut`. This default makes code easier to reason about and is one of Rust's nudges toward safety.

questions.prompt: What is wrong with this program?

options.A: Nothing; it prints 5 and then 6
options.B: It compiles but panics at runtime on the reassignment
options.C: It fails to compile: you cannot assign twice to the immutable variable `x`
options.D: It fails to compile because `x` has no type annotation

questions.explanation: `x` is bound with plain `let`, so it is immutable, and `x = 6` produces error E0384: cannot assign twice to immutable variable. Adding `mut` (`let mut x = 5;`) would fix it.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: inner: 12, then outer: 12
options.B: inner: 12, then outer: 6
options.C: inner: 10, then outer: 6
options.D: It fails to compile because `x` is declared twice without `mut`

questions.explanation: Each `let x` shadows the previous binding, which is allowed even without `mut`. The inner-scope shadow computes 6 * 2 = 12, but it ends with the block, so the outer `x` is still 6.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about `const` in Rust are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: The type of a constant must always be annotated
options.B: You can make a constant mutable by writing `const mut`
options.C: A constant must be set to a constant expression that can be evaluated at compile time
options.D: The naming convention for constants is all uppercase with underscores between words
options.E: Constants can only be declared inside function bodies

questions.explanation: Constants require a type annotation, must be initialized with a compile-time constant expression, and are conventionally named like `THREE_HOURS_IN_SECONDS`. They are always immutable (`mut` is not allowed) and can be declared in any scope, including the global scope.

questions.prompt: The shadowing version `let spaces = "   "; let spaces = spaces.len();` compiles, but this version does not. Why?

options.A: `len()` cannot be called on string slices
options.B: Assignment through `mut` must keep the same type, while shadowing with `let` creates a brand-new binding that may have a different type
options.C: `mut` variables cannot be assigned the result of a method call
options.D: Shadowing only works with numbers, so the first version does not actually compile either

questions.explanation: Mutating a binding cannot change its type, so assigning a `usize` to a `&str` variable is a mismatched-types error (E0308). Shadowing with a new `let` creates a new variable, so it is free to change the type.

questions.prompt: If you write `let x = 42;` with no type annotation and no other constraints, what integer type does Rust use for `x`?

options.C: `isize`

questions.explanation: Integer literals default to `i32` when no annotation or inference constraint says otherwise. Floating-point literals similarly default to `f64`.

questions.prompt: The value 255 is parsed at runtime, so the compiler cannot see the overflow ahead of time. What happens on the line `let y = x + 1;`?

options.A: In both debug and release builds it panics with an overflow error
options.B: In debug builds it panics; in release builds it wraps via two's complement and prints 0
options.C: In both debug and release builds it wraps and prints 0
options.D: It automatically promotes `y` to `u16` and prints 256

questions.explanation: Debug builds include integer-overflow checks that panic at runtime, while `--release` builds omit them and perform two's-complement wrapping, so 255 + 1 becomes 0. To handle overflow explicitly, use the `wrapping_*`, `checked_*`, `overflowing_*`, or `saturating_*` methods.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.D: It fails to compile because a tuple cannot mix integer and float types

questions.explanation: Tuples can hold mixed types; `tup.0` accesses the first element (500) and destructuring binds `y` to the second element (6.4). So it prints "500 6.4".

questions.prompt: A program indexes a 5-element array with an index of 10 that was read from user input at runtime. What happens?

options.A: It reads whatever memory happens to sit past the array (undefined behavior)
options.B: The program panics at runtime with an "index out of bounds" error
options.C: The indexing expression evaluates to `None`
options.D: It returns the default value of the element type, such as 0

questions.explanation: Rust checks array indexing at runtime: if the index is not less than the length, the program panics (e.g., "index out of bounds: the len is 5 but the index is 10") instead of allowing invalid memory access. The `Option`-returning behavior belongs to the `get` method, not the `[]` operator.

questions.prompt: Why does this program fail to compile?

options.A: `char` cannot hold ASCII letters, only Unicode symbols
options.B: The variable name `letter` is a reserved keyword
options.C: `"z"` with double quotes is a string literal (`&str`); `char` literals use single quotes, like `'z'`
options.D: A `char` must be initialized with its numeric Unicode code point

questions.explanation: Double quotes create a string literal of type `&str`, which does not match the annotated type `char`, so the compiler reports mismatched types. `char` literals use single quotes and hold a 4-byte Unicode scalar value.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about Rust's compound types are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Arrays have a fixed length that is part of their type, e.g. `[i32; 5]`
options.B: Every element of an array must have the same type
options.C: A tuple can group values of different types together
options.D: Arrays automatically grow when you add elements to them
options.E: Tuple elements are accessed with square brackets, like `tup[0]`

questions.explanation: Arrays are fixed-length, homogeneous, stack-allocated sequences whose length is part of the type, while tuples can mix types. Growable collections are `Vec<T>`, not arrays, and tuple elements are accessed with a period and index (`tup.0`), not brackets.

questions.prompt: In a Rust function signature such as `fn print_measurement(value: i32, unit: char)`, are the parameter type annotations optional?

options.A: Yes, the compiler infers parameter types from call sites
options.B: No, every parameter's type must be declared in the signature
options.C: Only the first parameter needs a type; the rest are inferred
options.D: Types are only required when the function is public

questions.explanation: Rust requires type annotations for every function parameter; this deliberate design choice means the compiler rarely needs annotations elsewhere and can give better error messages. Type inference applies to local `let` bindings, not to function signatures.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.C: It fails to compile: a block cannot be used on the right side of `let`

questions.explanation: A block is an expression whose value is its final expression; `x + 1` has no trailing semicolon, so the block evaluates to 4, which is bound to `y`.

questions.prompt: Why does this function fail to compile?

options.A: The function is missing an explicit `return` keyword, which is always required
options.B: The trailing semicolon turns `x + 1` into a statement, so the body evaluates to `()` instead of the declared `i32`
options.C: `x` is immutable, so `x + 1` is not allowed
options.D: The return type must be written as `-> int`, not `-> i32`

questions.explanation: Adding a semicolon converts the expression into a statement, and statements evaluate to the unit type `()`. The compiler reports mismatched types (expected `i32`, found `()`) and even suggests removing the semicolon.

questions.prompt: Coming from C, a programmer expects `x = y = 6` style code to work and writes `let x = (let y = 6);`. Why does Rust reject this?

options.A: Parentheses are not allowed around `let` for stylistic reasons only
options.B: `y` must be declared `mut` before it can appear on the right-hand side
options.C: A `let` binding is a statement, not an expression, so it does not evaluate to a value that `x` could be bound to
options.D: It works, but only when both variables have explicit type annotations

questions.explanation: Statements perform an action but do not return a value; since `let y = 6` is a statement, there is nothing for `x` to bind to, unlike in C where assignment is an expression. This is a core piece of Rust's statement-vs-expression distinction.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: The value of x is: 5
options.B: The value of x is: 6
options.C: It fails to compile because there is no `return` statement
options.D: The value of x is: ()

questions.explanation: The last expression in a function body is its implicit return value, so `plus_one(5)` returns 6. An explicit `return` keyword is only needed for early returns.

questions.prompt: What is the idiomatic way to write an ordinary (non-documentation) comment in Rust?

options.A: Start the comment with `//`; everything until the end of the line is ignored
options.B: Start the comment with `#`, as in Python and shell scripts
options.C: Wrap the comment in `<!--` and `-->`
options.D: Comments are not supported; use `println!` with a note instead

questions.explanation: Rust's ordinary comments start with two slashes and run to the end of the line; multi-line comments simply repeat `//` on each line. (Rust also has `///` documentation comments, covered later in the book.)

questions.prompt: Why does this program fail to compile?

options.A: `if` requires parentheses around its condition
options.B: The condition must be a `bool`; Rust will not automatically convert an integer to a boolean
options.C: `number` must be declared `mut` to be used in a condition
options.D: An `if` without an `else` branch is not allowed

questions.explanation: Unlike languages such as C or JavaScript, Rust never coerces non-boolean values to `bool`, so the compiler reports "expected `bool`, found integer". You must write an explicit test like `if number != 0`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: The result is 10
options.B: The result is 20
options.C: It fails to compile: `break` cannot carry a value
options.D: It loops forever and prints nothing

questions.explanation: A `loop` is an expression, and `break` can pass a value out of it; when `counter` reaches 10, `break counter * 2` ends the loop with the value 20, which is bound to `result`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.B: 1! 2! 3! LIFTOFF!!!
options.C: 3! 2! 1! LIFTOFF!!!

questions.explanation: The range `1..4` is end-exclusive, so it yields 1, 2, 3, and `.rev()` reverses that to 3, 2, 1. After the loop finishes, LIFTOFF!!! is printed.

questions.prompt: What is the final line this program prints?

options.A: End count = 0
options.B: End count = 1
options.C: End count = 2
options.D: End count = 3

questions.explanation: The unlabeled `break` exits only the inner loop, letting `count` increment to 1 and then 2; on the pass where `count == 2`, `break 'counting_up` exits the labeled outer loop before `count += 1` runs. Loop labels let `break` and `continue` target an outer loop instead of the innermost one.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about Rust control flow are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `if` is an expression, so it can appear on the right side of a `let` statement
options.B: When `if`/`else` is used as an expression, every arm must evaluate to the same type
options.C: `break` can return a value from a `loop` expression
options.D: `while` loops can also return a value with `break value`, just like `loop`
options.E: In an `if` condition, any non-zero integer is treated as true

questions.explanation: `if` is an expression whose arms must all have one type (`let x = if cond { 5 } else { "six" };` is a compile error), and only the infinite `loop` form can yield a value through `break value`. `while` and `for` cannot break with a value, and conditions must be genuine `bool`s — integers are never coerced.

questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly states Rust's three ownership rules?

options.A: Each value has an owner; there can be multiple owners at a time; values are freed by the garbage collector.
options.B: Each value has an owner; there can only be one owner at a time; when the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped.
options.C: Each variable has a reference count; values are dropped when the count reaches zero; ownership can be shared freely.
options.D: Each value lives on the heap; the programmer must call `free` manually; the compiler warns about leaks.

questions.explanation: The three ownership rules are: each value has an owner, there is exactly one owner at a time, and the value is dropped when its owner goes out of scope. There is no garbage collector or manual free.

questions.prompt: What happens when this code is compiled and run?

options.A: It prints "hello, world!" because `s2` is a shallow copy and both variables stay valid.
options.B: It compiles but panics at runtime with a use-after-free error.
options.C: It fails to compile with error E0382: borrow of moved value `s1`.
options.D: It prints an empty string because `s1` was reset when it was copied.

questions.explanation: `String` does not implement `Copy`, so `let s2 = s1;` moves the value and invalidates `s1`. Using `s1` afterward is a compile-time error (E0382), which is how Rust prevents a double free.

questions.prompt: What happens when this code is compiled and run?

options.A: Compile error: `x` was moved into `y` and can no longer be used.
options.B: It prints "x = 5, y = 5" because `i32` implements `Copy`, so `x` remains valid.
options.C: It prints "x = 5, y = 5" but only because the compiler silently calls `clone`.
options.D: Runtime panic: two variables cannot refer to the same stack value.

questions.explanation: Integers are simple, fixed-size stack values that implement the `Copy` trait, so assignment copies the bits and the original variable stays valid. No move occurs and no `clone` is needed.

questions.prompt: What does calling `s1.clone()` on a `String` do?

options.A: It copies only the stack data (pointer, length, capacity), so both strings share the same heap buffer.
options.B: It creates a second reference to `s1` that is tracked by a reference counter.
options.C: It performs a deep copy: the heap data is duplicated, giving two independent owners.
options.D: It moves the value and marks `s1` as invalid, exactly like plain assignment.

questions.explanation: `clone` explicitly duplicates the heap data, so `s1` and the clone each own their own buffer and both remain valid. It is explicit precisely because it may be expensive.

questions.prompt: This code does not compile. What is the bug, and what is the most idiomatic fix?

options.A: `s` was moved into `takes_ownership`, so the final `println!` uses a moved value; pass a reference (`&s`) and take `&String`/`&str` instead.
options.B: `takes_ownership` forgot to return, causing a memory leak; add `return some_string;`.
options.C: The code compiles fine; passing to a function never affects ownership.
options.D: `println!` cannot print a `String` directly; call `s.as_str()` first.

questions.explanation: Passing a non-`Copy` value to a function moves ownership into the parameter, so `s` is invalid afterward. Borrowing with a reference lets `main` keep ownership; returning the value back would also work but is clunkier.

questions.prompt: What happens when this code is compiled and run?

options.A: Compile error: a function cannot return a value it received as a parameter.
options.B: Runtime panic: `a_string` is dropped at the end of the function before it can be returned.
options.C: Compile error: `s1` is used after being moved into the function.
options.D: It prints "hello": ownership moves into the function and is moved back out through the return value.

questions.explanation: Returning a value moves ownership to the caller, so the `String` is not dropped inside the function; it now belongs to `s2`. Only `s1` is invalidated, and it is never used again.

questions.prompt: A developer coming from Java writes `let s2 = s1;` with a `String` and expects both variables to point at the same data and stay usable. What actually happens in Rust?

options.A: Rust behaves like Java: both variables alias the same heap object and a garbage collector cleans it up later.
options.B: Rust performs a move: the pointer/length/capacity are copied, `s1` is invalidated at compile time, and only `s2` will be dropped.
options.C: Rust performs an automatic deep copy, so both variables own separate heap buffers.
options.D: Both stay valid, but using `s1` afterward causes a runtime double-free error.

questions.explanation: Assignment of a non-`Copy` type copies only the stack metadata and invalidates the source — a move. Invalidating `s1` is exactly what lets Rust free the memory once (when `s2` goes out of scope) without a garbage collector.

questions.prompt: For `let s = String::from("hello");`, where do the parts of the `String` live?

options.A: Everything, including the character data, lives on the stack because `s` is a local variable.
options.B: Everything lives on the heap; the stack holds nothing for `s`.
options.C: The pointer, length, and capacity live on the stack; the character contents live on the heap.
options.D: The contents live in the read-only data section of the binary, like a string literal.

questions.explanation: A `String` is a stack value of three parts — pointer, length, capacity — pointing at a growable heap buffer holding the contents. String literals, by contrast, are baked into the binary.

questions.prompt: In this code, when is the memory holding "hello" freed?

options.A: At the end of the enclosing scope, when `s` is dropped.
options.B: Immediately at the reassignment: Rust drops the old value "hello" as soon as `s` is bound to the new `String`.
options.C: Never — reassignment leaks the old buffer unless you call `drop` manually.
options.D: At the next call to `println!`, when the allocator runs a cleanup pass.

questions.explanation: When you assign a new value to an existing owner, Rust calls `drop` on the old value immediately; only the new value "ahoy" survives to the end of the scope. There is no deferred cleanup pass.

questions.prompt: Which of these types implement the `Copy` trait, so that assignment leaves the original variable valid? (Select all that apply.)

options.B: `bool`
options.C: `String`
options.D: `(i32, bool)`
options.E: `Vec<i32>`

questions.explanation: Simple fixed-size stack types like integers, `bool`, floats, `char`, and tuples made only of `Copy` types implement `Copy`. `String` and `Vec` manage heap memory (they implement `Drop`), so they cannot be `Copy` and are moved instead.

questions.prompt: What happens when this code is compiled and run?

options.A: It prints "hello" twice: `s` becomes valid again once `t` goes out of scope.
options.B: It prints "hello" once, then panics at runtime on the second `println!`.
options.C: It fails to compile: `s` was moved into `t`, and a move is permanent even after `t`'s scope ends.
options.D: It fails to compile because you cannot move a value into an inner scope.

questions.explanation: The move into `t` permanently invalidates `s`; when the inner scope ends, the `String` is dropped by `t`, not returned to `s`. The second `println!` is a compile-time borrow-of-moved-value error.

questions.prompt: What does creating a reference with `&s1` and passing it to a function accomplish?

options.A: It transfers ownership to the function, which must return it back.
options.B: It lets the function refer to the value without taking ownership, so the value is not dropped when the reference goes out of scope.
options.C: It makes a cheap copy of the heap data for the function to use.
options.D: It increments a runtime reference count that keeps the value alive.

questions.explanation: Passing `&s1` borrows the value: the function can read it, but ownership stays with the caller, so nothing is dropped when the reference's scope ends. This avoids moving values into and back out of every function.

questions.prompt: This code fails to compile. What is wrong, and what changes fix it?

options.A: `push_str` does not exist on `String`; use the `+` operator instead.
options.B: References cannot be passed across function boundaries; pass `s` by value.
options.C: References are immutable by default; declare `let mut s`, pass `&mut s`, and take `some_string: &mut String`.
options.D: The code compiles; the mutation just silently does nothing through a shared reference.

questions.explanation: You cannot mutate through an immutable reference. The fix needs all three pieces: a mutable owner (`let mut s`), a mutable borrow at the call site (`&mut s`), and a `&mut String` parameter.

questions.prompt: What happens when this code is compiled and run?

options.A: It prints "hello, hello": both mutable references read the same value.
options.B: It fails to compile with E0499: cannot borrow `s` as mutable more than once at a time.
options.C: It compiles but the behavior is undefined because of the aliasing pointers.
options.D: It panics at runtime when the second mutable borrow is created.

questions.explanation: Rust allows at most one active mutable reference to a value; since `r1` is still used in the `println!`, creating `r2` is a compile-time error. This restriction rules out data races at compile time.

questions.prompt: What happens when this code is compiled and run?

options.A: Compile error: `r1` and `r2` are immutable borrows still in scope when `r3` is created.
options.B: Compile error: you can never mix `&` and `&mut` borrows of the same variable in one function.
options.C: It compiles and prints "hello and hello" then "hello world": the immutable borrows end at their last use, so the mutable borrow is allowed.
options.D: It compiles but panics at runtime because `r3` invalidates `r1` and `r2`.

questions.explanation: A reference's borrow lasts only until its last use (non-lexical lifetimes), not to the end of the scope. `r1` and `r2` are last used in the first `println!`, so `&mut s` afterward is perfectly legal.

questions.prompt: This code fails to compile with E0502. Which exact detail makes it an error, given that non-lexical lifetimes exist?

options.A: Creating `&mut s` after any `&s` is always an error, no matter how the references are used.
options.B: `r1` is used in the `println!` after `r3` is created, so the immutable borrow is still live at the point of the mutable borrow.
options.C: `println!` can only take one reference argument at a time.
options.D: `r3` must be declared `mut` itself, as in `let mut r3 = &mut s;`.

questions.explanation: With NLL the immutable borrow would end at its last use — but here `r1`'s last use is after `&mut s` is created, so both borrows are simultaneously live, violating the one-`&mut`-XOR-many-`&` rule. Deleting `r1` from the `println!` would make it compile.

questions.prompt: Which statement correctly summarizes Rust's rules of references?

options.A: At any given time you can have either one mutable reference or any number of immutable references, and references must always be valid.
options.B: You can have at most one reference of any kind to a value at a time.
options.C: You can have unlimited mutable references as long as only one thread is running.
options.D: You can mix any number of mutable and immutable references as long as you do not write through two of them on the same line.

questions.explanation: The two rules: one mutable reference XOR any number of immutable references at a time, and references must always point to valid data. Together they prevent data races and dangling pointers at compile time.

questions.prompt: Why does this function fail to compile, and what is the fix?

options.A: `String::from` cannot be used inside a function that returns a reference.
options.B: It compiles, but calling it returns a null reference at runtime.
options.C: It returns a reference to `s`, which is dropped when the function ends — a dangling reference; fix it by returning the owned `String` (`s`) directly.
options.D: The return type should be `&str` instead of `&String`; that alone makes it compile.

questions.explanation: `s` is dropped at the end of `dangle`, so the returned reference would point to freed memory; the compiler rejects it ("there is no value for it to be borrowed from"). Returning the `String` itself moves ownership out, so nothing dangles. Changing to `&str` would not help — the local is still dropped.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about Rust's borrowing rules are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: You may hold any number of simultaneous immutable references to a value.
options.B: You may hold one mutable and one immutable reference at the same time, as long as both are used afterwards.
options.C: A reference's borrow ends at the reference's last use, not necessarily at the end of its lexical scope.
options.D: Violating the borrowing rules causes a panic the first time the offending reference is dereferenced at runtime.
options.E: You may create a mutable reference after earlier immutable references have already had their last use.

questions.explanation: Many readers are fine (A); borrows end at last use under NLL (C), which is exactly why E is allowed. Mixing a live `&mut` with a live `&` is forbidden (B), and all of this is enforced at compile time, never as a runtime panic (D).

questions.prompt: Does this code compile, and why?

options.A: No: once a value has been mutably borrowed, it can never be mutably borrowed again.
options.B: Yes: `r1` goes out of scope before `r2` is created, so the two mutable borrows are sequential, not simultaneous — it prints "hello world!".
options.C: No: `s` must be re-declared `mut` before each new mutable borrow.
options.D: Yes, but only because the inner block copies `s` rather than borrowing it.

questions.explanation: The one-mutable-reference rule forbids simultaneous mutable borrows, not repeated ones. `r1` ends with the inner scope, so `r2` is the only live mutable reference when it is created.

questions.prompt: "Rust's one-mutable-reference rule must be enforced with runtime locks, which slows programs down." What is actually true?

options.A: Correct: every `&mut` acquires a hidden mutex at runtime.
options.B: Partially correct: the rule is checked at runtime, but only in debug builds.
options.C: Wrong: the rule is enforced entirely by the borrow checker at compile time, preventing data races with zero runtime cost.
options.D: Wrong: the rule is merely a lint; ignoring the warning gives undefined behavior.

questions.explanation: The borrow checker statically proves that no value has a writer aliased with other readers or writers, so compiled code needs no locks or counters for this guarantee. Data races become compile errors, not runtime failures.

questions.prompt: What is the type of `s` in `let s = "Hello, world!";`, and where does the data live?

options.A: `String` — the text is allocated on the heap at startup.
options.B: `&str` — a string slice pointing at data stored directly in the compiled binary, which is why literals are immutable.
options.C: `char` array — each character is stored separately on the stack.
options.D: `&String` — a reference to a global `String` created by the compiler.

questions.explanation: String literals are `&str`: slices pointing at bytes baked into the binary. That storage is read-only, which is exactly why literals cannot be mutated.

questions.prompt: Given `fn first_word(s: &str) -> &str` that returns a slice of the input, what happens here?

options.A: It prints "the first word is: hello" because `word` was computed before `clear`.
options.B: It prints an empty first word because `word` re-reads `s` lazily.
options.C: It panics at runtime: `word` points into a buffer that `clear` emptied.
options.D: It fails to compile with E0502: `clear` needs a mutable borrow of `s`, but `word` is an immutable borrow of `s` that is still used in the `println!`.

questions.explanation: The returned slice borrows from `s`, and `clear` requires `&mut self`. Since `word` is used after the `clear` call, the immutable borrow is still live at the mutation point — a compile error, which is exactly the bug class slices are designed to catch.

questions.prompt: For `let s = String::from("hello");`, which expression is equivalent to `&s[..]`?

options.C: `s.clone()`

questions.explanation: In range slice syntax, omitting the start defaults it to 0 and omitting the end defaults it to the length, so `&s[..]` is a slice of the entire string — the same as `&s[0..s.len()]`.

questions.prompt: This program compiles and runs without errors. What is the bug the book warns about?

options.A: `first_word` reads past the end of the string when there is no space.
options.B: `word` is a plain `usize` detached from `s`: after `s.clear()` it still holds 5, an index that no longer means anything, and the compiler cannot catch the desynchronization.
options.C: `s.clear()` fails to compile because `word` still borrows `s`.
options.D: Iterating with `as_bytes` breaks on any non-ASCII input, causing a panic.

questions.explanation: Because the returned index carries no connection to the `String`, nothing stops the data from changing underneath it — a silent logic bug. Returning a `&str` slice instead ties the result to the data, turning this into a compile error (E0502).

questions.prompt: Given `fn first_word(s: &str) -> &str` and `let my_string = String::from("hello world");`, which calls compile? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `first_word(&my_string)` — a `&String` argument
options.B: `first_word(my_string)` — passing the `String` by value
options.C: `first_word("hello world")` — a string literal
options.D: `first_word(&my_string[0..6])` — a slice of the `String`
options.E: `first_word(5)` — an integer index

questions.explanation: Taking `&str` as the parameter type is the flexible choice: it accepts slices, literals (which are already `&str`), and `&String` via deref coercion. Passing an owned `String` by value or an integer is a type mismatch.

questions.prompt: What does this code print, and what is the type of `slice`?

options.A: It prints "[2, 3]"; `slice` has type `&[i32]`.
options.B: It prints "[1, 2, 3]"; `slice` has type `&[i32; 3]`.
options.C: It prints "[2, 3, 4]"; ranges in Rust are inclusive of the end index.
options.D: Compile error: slices only work on `String`, not arrays.

questions.explanation: Slices work on arrays too: `&a[1..3]` covers indices 1 and 2 (the end is exclusive), giving `[2, 3]` with type `&[i32]` — a pointer plus a length, just like a string slice.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about slices are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: A string slice `&str` stores a pointer to the starting byte and a length; it does not own the data.
options.B: A string slice owns its data and frees it when the slice goes out of scope.
options.C: String literals such as `"hello"` have type `&str`.
options.D: Slicing a `String` in the middle of a multibyte UTF-8 character compiles, but the program will exit with an error at runtime.
options.E: A slice of an `[i32; 5]` array has type `&[i32; 5]`.

questions.explanation: A slice is a borrowed view (pointer + length), never an owner, and literals are `&str` into the binary. String slice boundaries are checked at runtime, so slicing mid-character panics; an array slice erases the length from the type, giving `&[i32]`, not `&[i32; 5]`.

title: Using Structs to Structure Related Data

questions.prompt: When creating an instance of a struct with `StructName { ... }`, which statement is true?

options.A: Fields must be listed in the same order as in the struct definition
options.B: You must specify a value for every field, but in any order you like
options.C: Fields you omit are automatically zero-initialized
options.D: You can only create instances inside an `impl` block

questions.explanation: A struct literal must provide a value for every field (unless struct update syntax fills the rest), but the fields do not have to appear in the same order as the definition.

questions.prompt: This program fails to compile. What is the bug?

options.A: `user1` is not declared with `mut`, so no field of it can be assigned
options.B: Only the `email` field needs to be marked `mut` in the struct definition
options.C: `String` fields can never be reassigned after construction
options.D: Nothing is wrong; the program compiles and runs fine

questions.explanation: Rust requires the entire instance to be mutable (`let mut user1 = ...`) to assign to any field. Rust does not allow marking only certain fields as mutable.

questions.prompt: Inside `build_user(email: String, username: String)`, when is it valid to write just `email` instead of `email: email` in the `User { ... }` literal?

options.A: Only when the field type implements `Copy`
options.B: Whenever a variable or parameter in scope has exactly the same name as the field
options.C: Only when the struct derives `Default`
options.D: Never; the `field: value` form is always required

questions.explanation: Field init shorthand applies when a variable with the same name as the field is in scope, so `email` is shorthand for `email: email`. It has nothing to do with `Copy` or `Default`.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile and run this program?

options.A: It prints `alice`, because `..user1` copies the remaining fields
options.B: Compile error: `user1.username` was moved into `user2` by the struct update syntax
options.C: It compiles but panics at runtime with a use-after-move error
options.D: Compile error: `..user1` is not allowed when any field is overridden

questions.explanation: Struct update syntax uses move semantics like assignment: the non-Copy `String` in `username` is moved into `user2`, so `user1.username` can no longer be used. Move errors are caught at compile time, never at runtime.

questions.prompt: Which statements about struct update syntax (`..other`) are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `..other` must come last in the struct literal
options.B: Fields whose types implement `Copy` (like `bool` and `u64`) are copied, so they remain usable on the original instance
options.C: `..other` performs a deep clone, so the original instance is always fully usable afterward
options.D: It moves non-Copy fields, behaving like `=` assignment for each filled-in field
options.E: `..other` can appear before explicitly listed fields

questions.explanation: `..other` must be the last element of the literal and moves or copies each remaining field exactly like assignment: Copy types are copied, non-Copy types like `String` are moved. It never clones.

questions.prompt: Given `struct Color(i32, i32, i32);` and `struct Point(i32, i32, i32);`, can a function `fn show(c: Color)` be called with a `Point` value?

options.A: Yes, because both are tuple structs with identical field types
options.B: Yes, but only if `Point` derives `Into<Color>`
options.C: No; each tuple struct is its own distinct type even when the field types match
options.D: No, because tuple structs cannot be passed to functions at all

questions.explanation: Tuple structs give a name to a tuple shape and create a brand-new type; `Color` and `Point` are different types despite having the same `(i32, i32, i32)` layout, so the call is a type error.

questions.prompt: What is the primary use of a unit-like struct such as `struct AlwaysEqual;`?

options.A: To implement traits on a type that has no data to store
options.B: To create a struct whose fields are filled in later at runtime
options.C: To define a compile-time constant with zero value
options.D: To mark a struct as private to the current module

questions.explanation: Unit-like structs have no fields and are instantiated as just `AlwaysEqual`. They are useful when you need a type to implement a trait on but have no data to carry.

questions.prompt: This struct definition fails to compile. Why?

options.A: Struct fields cannot be references under any circumstances
options.B: `&str` fields require a lifetime parameter, e.g. `struct User<'a> { username: &'a str, ... }`
options.C: `&str` must be replaced with `str` because references are implicit in structs
options.D: The `bool` field must come before the reference field

questions.explanation: Storing a reference in a struct requires a lifetime specifier (error E0106) so the compiler can ensure the referenced data outlives the struct. Using an owned `String` instead avoids the annotation.

questions.prompt: In the rectangles example, the function is defined as `fn area(rectangle: &Rectangle) -> u32`. Why does it take `&Rectangle` instead of `Rectangle`?

options.A: References are required for all struct parameters
options.B: So `main` keeps ownership of `rect1` and can continue to use it after the call
options.C: Because `u32` cannot be returned from a function that owns its argument
options.D: To make the multiplication faster at runtime

questions.explanation: Borrowing with `&` lets `area` read the struct's fields without taking ownership, so the caller can keep using the instance afterward. Accessing fields through a reference does not move them.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.A: It prints `Rectangle { width: 30, height: 50 }`
options.B: Compile error: `Rectangle` doesn't implement `std::fmt::Display`, which `{}` requires
options.C: It compiles but panics at runtime because `{}` cannot format structs
options.D: It prints `(30, 50)` using the derived formatter

questions.explanation: `{}` uses the `Display` trait, which is not implemented automatically for structs and is not provided by `#[derive(Debug)]`. Deriving `Debug` only enables `{:?}` and `{:#?}` formatting.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between printing a struct with `{:?}` and with `{:#?}`?

options.A: `{:?}` requires `Debug` but `{:#?}` requires `Display`
options.B: Both use the `Debug` trait; `{:?}` prints on one line while `{:#?}` pretty-prints across multiple lines
options.C: `{:#?}` also prints the memory address of the struct
options.D: `{:?}` works on any type without deriving anything

questions.explanation: Both specifiers use the `Debug` trait (typically enabled via `#[derive(Debug)]`); `{:#?}` produces the pretty, multi-line output that is easier to read for larger structs.

questions.prompt: Which statements about the `dbg!` macro are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: It prints to standard error, not standard output
options.B: It returns ownership of the expression's value, so it can be used inline like `width: dbg!(30 * scale)`
options.C: Its output includes the source file, line, and column of the call
options.D: It prints to standard output, exactly like `println!`
options.E: It formats values using the `Display` trait

questions.explanation: `dbg!` prints `[file:line:col] expr = value` to stderr using `Debug` formatting, takes ownership of its argument, and returns that ownership, which is why passing `&rect1` is used to avoid moving a value you still need.

questions.prompt: In a method signature inside `impl Rectangle`, what is `&self` shorthand for?

options.A: `self: &Self`
options.B: `self: Self`
options.C: `self: &mut Self`
options.D: `self: Box<Self>`

questions.explanation: `&self` is shorthand for `self: &Self`, where `Self` is an alias for the type the `impl` block is for. It borrows the instance immutably; `self` takes ownership and `&mut self` borrows mutably.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `10 true`
options.B: Compile error: a method cannot have the same name as a field
options.C: `true true`

questions.explanation: A method may share a name with a field: `rect.width` (no parentheses) accesses the field, while `rect.width()` calls the method, which returns `self.width > 0`, i.e. `true`.

questions.prompt: A C++ programmer asks where Rust's `->` operator is for calling methods through a reference. What is the correct answer?

options.A: Rust uses `->` only for methods that take `&mut self`
options.B: Rust has automatic referencing and dereferencing: `p1.distance(&p2)` and `(&p1).distance(&p2)` are equivalent, so no `->` is needed
options.C: You must always dereference manually with `(*ptr).method()` in Rust
options.D: Rust methods can only be called on owned values, never through references

questions.explanation: When you call a method, Rust automatically adds `&`, `&mut`, or `*` to the receiver so it matches the method's signature. This works because the receiver type `Self` makes the needed borrow unambiguous.

questions.prompt: This program fails to compile. What is the bug?

options.A: `grow` must take `self` by value to modify fields
options.B: `rect` is not declared `mut`, so the automatic `&mut` borrow required by `grow(&mut self, ...)` is not allowed
options.C: Methods with parameters besides `self` are not allowed
options.D: `+=` cannot be used on struct fields inside methods

questions.explanation: Calling `rect.grow(5)` auto-borrows the receiver as `&mut rect` to match `&mut self`, but a mutable borrow of an immutable binding is rejected. Declaring `let mut rect = ...` fixes it.

questions.prompt: Which statements about associated functions and `impl` blocks are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: A function in an `impl` block without a `self` parameter is an associated function, not a method
options.B: Associated functions are called with `::` syntax, like `Rectangle::square(3)`
options.C: `new` is a built-in keyword that Rust reserves for constructors
options.D: A single type may have multiple separate `impl` blocks
options.E: All methods of a type must live in exactly one `impl` block

questions.explanation: Functions without a `self` receiver are associated functions (often constructors like `square` or `new`) called via `Type::name(...)`. `new` is just a conventional name, not a keyword, and a type can have as many `impl` blocks as you like.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile and run this program?

options.A: It prints `3 4`
options.B: Compile error: `into_width(self)` takes ownership, so `rect` is moved and `rect.height` cannot be used afterward
options.C: It compiles but panics at runtime when accessing `rect.height`
options.D: It prints `3 0` because `rect` was reset to defaults

questions.explanation: A method taking plain `self` consumes the instance; after `rect.into_width()`, `rect` has been moved and any later use is a compile-time borrow-of-moved-value error. Taking `self` is deliberate when the caller should not use the old instance again.

questions.prompt: What does defining an enum like `enum IpAddrKind { V4, V6 }` express in Rust's type system?

options.A: A value of type `IpAddrKind` is exactly one of a fixed set of possible variants
options.B: A value of type `IpAddrKind` contains both a `V4` and a `V6` field at the same time
options.C: `V4` and `V6` are two independent types with no relationship to each other
options.D: `IpAddrKind` is an alias for an integer that can hold any value

questions.explanation: An enum says a value is one of a possible set of variants — it can be `V4` or `V6`, but never both at once. Both variants are values of the same type, `IpAddrKind`.

questions.prompt: Given `enum IpAddrKind { V4, V6 }`, how do you create an instance of the `V4` variant?

options.A: `let four = V4;` — variants are global names
options.B: `let four = IpAddrKind::V4;` — variants are namespaced under the enum with `::`
options.C: `let four = IpAddrKind.V4;` — variants are accessed with a dot
options.D: `let four = new IpAddrKind::V4();` — variants must be constructed with `new`

questions.explanation: Enum variants are namespaced under the enum's identifier, so you use the double colon: `IpAddrKind::V4`. (Only `Option`'s `Some`/`None` are usable without a prefix, because the prelude imports them.)

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about enum variants in Rust are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Different variants of the same enum can hold different types and amounts of data
options.B: Every variant of an enum must hold the same type of data
options.C: A variant can have struct-like named fields, as `Move { x: i32, y: i32 }` does
options.D: Variants that hold data must first be defined as separate structs
options.E: You can define methods on an enum using an `impl` block, just like on a struct

questions.explanation: Variants may carry no data (`Quit`), named fields (`Move`), or tuple-like data of any types (`Write`, `ChangeColor`), and each variant can differ. Enums also support `impl` blocks for methods; no separate struct definitions are required.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: It prints `write: hello`
options.B: It prints `quit`
options.C: It fails to compile because enums cannot have `impl` blocks
options.D: It fails to compile because `describe` does not handle the `Write` case

questions.explanation: Enums can have methods defined in `impl` blocks just like structs. `m` is the `Write` variant, so the match binds `text` to the inner `String` and the method returns `write: hello`.

questions.prompt: Why does Rust use `Option<T>` instead of a null value like many other languages?

options.A: Because `Option<T>` and `T` are different types, so the compiler forces you to handle the absent case before using the value
options.B: Because `Option<T>` is faster at runtime than checking for null
options.C: Because Rust has null, but only inside `unsafe` blocks, and `Option<T>` wraps that mechanism
options.D: Because `Option<T>` automatically converts to `T` whenever a value is present

questions.explanation: Rust has no null. Since `Option<T>` is a distinct type from `T`, the compiler will not let you use an optional value as if it were definitely present — you must explicitly handle both `Some` and `None`, eliminating the classic null-dereference bug.

questions.prompt: What is the result of running this program?

options.A: It prints `10` — `Some(5)` is unwrapped automatically
options.B: It compiles but panics at runtime when adding the `Option`
options.C: It fails to compile: there is no implementation for `i8 + Option<i8>`
options.D: It prints `5` — `None`-like values are treated as zero

questions.explanation: `Option<i8>` and `i8` are different types, so `x + y` is rejected at compile time (error E0277: cannot add `Option<i8>` to `i8`). You must convert the `Option<i8>` to an `i8` — for example with `match` — before adding.

questions.prompt: Why does this program fail to compile?

options.A: `None` cannot be assigned to a variable; it can only appear in `match` arms
options.B: The compiler cannot infer the `T` in `Option<T>` from `None` alone, so a type annotation like `Option<i32>` is required
options.C: `None` is not in scope unless you write `use std::option::Option::None;`
options.D: Variables holding `None` must be declared `mut` so they can later hold `Some`

questions.explanation: With `Some(5)` the compiler infers `Option<i32>`, but `None` carries no value to infer `T` from, so you must annotate, e.g. `let absent_number: Option<i32> = None;`. `None` itself is in scope via the prelude.

questions.prompt: A colleague says: "`Some(5)` has type `i32`, since it just wraps the number 5." What is the correct picture?

options.A: They are right — `Some(5)` and `5` are interchangeable
options.B: `Some(5)` has type `Option<i32>`, a different type from `i32`, so you must extract the inner value before using it as an `i32`
options.C: `Some(5)` has type `Some<i32>`, which coerces to `i32` on demand
options.D: `Some(5)` has no type until it is matched

questions.explanation: `Some` is a variant of `enum Option<T> { None, Some(T) }`, so `Some(5)` is an `Option<i32>`. The deliberate separation between `Option<T>` and `T` is what forces code to handle the possibly-absent case.

questions.prompt: In a `match` expression, which arm's code runs, and what does the whole `match` evaluate to?

options.A: Every arm whose pattern matches runs, and the `match` evaluates to the last one
options.B: The first arm whose pattern matches runs, and the `match` evaluates to that arm's expression
options.C: The most specific matching arm runs regardless of order, and the `match` evaluates to `()`
options.D: Arms are tried in random order, so a catch-all must always be present

questions.explanation: Patterns are compared in order, like coins falling through a sorting machine: the first pattern the value fits wins. The resulting value of the chosen arm's expression becomes the value of the entire `match` expression.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: Only `25`
options.B: `State quarter from Alaska!` followed by `25`
options.C: It fails to compile because `state` is not declared anywhere
options.D: `State quarter from UsState!` followed by `25`

questions.explanation: The pattern `Coin::Quarter(state)` binds the variable `state` to the data inside the variant, here `UsState::Alaska`. The arm prints the debug form of the state and then evaluates to `25`, which `main` prints.

questions.prompt: What is wrong with this function?

options.A: Nothing — if `x` is `None`, the match simply returns `None` by default
options.B: It compiles but panics at runtime when called with `None`
options.C: It fails to compile: matches are exhaustive, and the `None` case is not covered
options.D: It fails to compile because `i` shadows the function parameter

questions.explanation: Rust `match` arms must cover every possible value; here the compiler rejects the code with a `non-exhaustive patterns: None not covered` error. Adding a `None => None` arm (or a catch-all) fixes it — a bug caught at compile time, not runtime.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `Some(6)` then `None`
options.B: `6` then `0`
options.C: `Some(6)` then a panic on the second call
options.D: `Some(5)` then `None` — `i + 1` modifies a copy that is discarded

questions.explanation: For `Some(5)` the pattern `Some(i)` binds `i` to `5` and the arm produces `Some(6)`; for `None` the first arm matches and returns `None`. `{:?}` prints the `Option` values in debug form.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about catch-all patterns in `match` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Using `_` as the final arm makes the match non-exhaustive
options.B: A catch-all arm should be placed last, because patterns are evaluated in order
options.C: A named pattern like `other` matches any remaining value and binds it so the arm can use it
options.D: The `_` pattern matches any remaining value but does not bind it
options.E: Putting an arm after a catch-all is a hard compile error

questions.explanation: `other` and `_` both satisfy exhaustiveness; the difference is that `other` binds the value while `_` tells Rust you won't use it. Order matters because arms are tried top to bottom — an arm after a catch-all is unreachable, which produces a compiler warning, not an error.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.A: It prints `got: hello` and then `Some("hello")`
options.B: It fails to compile: matching on `opt` by value moves the `String` into `s`, so `opt` is partially moved and cannot be used afterward
options.C: It compiles but panics at runtime when printing the moved value
options.D: It fails to compile because `match` never takes ownership of the value it inspects

questions.explanation: `String` is not `Copy`, so the pattern `Some(s)` moves the inner `String` out of `opt`, leaving it partially moved; the later use of `opt` is a compile error (E0382). Matching on a reference instead — `match &opt` — borrows the inner value and leaves `opt` usable.

questions.prompt: In the arm `_ => ()`, a beginner claims `()` is "an empty function call that does nothing". What does `()` actually mean here?

options.A: `()` is the unit value; the arm matches every other value and explicitly does nothing
options.B: `()` calls the default handler registered for the enum
options.C: `()` is a compile error — an arm must contain at least one statement
options.D: `()` returns `None`, since a `match` must produce an `Option`

questions.explanation: `()` is the unit value, the value of the empty tuple type. Using it as an arm's expression says: this pattern matches anything else, and we run no code for it.

questions.prompt: Why does this program fail to compile?

options.A: The `None` arm is unreachable because `x` is known to be `Some`
options.B: A `match` used as an expression requires all arms to produce the same type, but one arm yields `i32` and the other `&str`
options.C: String literals are not allowed inside `match` arms
options.D: `result` must be annotated with a type before the `match` can compile

questions.explanation: Because the whole `match` is an expression assigned to `result`, every arm must evaluate to a single common type. Here the arms produce `i32` and `&str`, so the compiler reports mismatched types (E0308).

questions.section: 6.3 Concise Control Flow with if let and let else
questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about `if let` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `if let` is syntactic sugar for a `match` that runs code for one pattern and ignores all other values
options.B: If the pattern does not match, the program panics at runtime
options.C: Using `if let` means giving up the exhaustiveness checking that `match` enforces
options.D: An `else` block attached to `if let` behaves like the `_` catch-all arm of the equivalent `match`
options.E: `if let` must always be followed by an `else` block

questions.explanation: `if let` handles exactly one pattern and simply skips the block (or runs the optional `else`) when the value doesn't match — no panic, but also no compiler check that every case is handled. The `else` corresponds to the `_ =>` arm of the equivalent `match`.

questions.section: 6.3 Concise Control Flow with if let and let else
questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `State quarter from Alaska!` then `count = 2`
options.B: `State quarter from Alaska!` then `count = 3`
options.C: `count = 2` only — `if let` cannot bind `state`
options.D: It fails to compile because `if let` with `else` is not valid syntax

questions.explanation: The quarter matches the `if let` pattern, binding `state` and printing the message; the two pennies fall into the `else` branch, incrementing `count` to 2. `if let ... else` is exactly like a two-arm `match` with a `_` arm.

questions.section: 6.3 Concise Control Flow with if let and let else
questions.prompt: What is wrong with this use of `let...else`?

options.A: Nothing — after printing, `n` defaults to `0` and the function returns `0`
options.B: It fails to compile: the `else` block of `let...else` must diverge (e.g. `return` or `panic!`), because `n` would otherwise be unbound
options.C: It fails to compile because `let...else` only works with the `Result` type
options.D: It compiles but panics at runtime whenever `x` is `None`

questions.explanation: With `let PATTERN = EXPR else { ... }`, the `else` block runs when the pattern doesn't match, and it must exit the enclosing scope (return, panic, break, etc.) since no binding for `n` exists on that path. Adding `return 0;` after the `println!` would fix it.

questions.section: 6.3 Concise Control Flow with if let and let else
questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of `let...else`, as in the `describe_state_quarter` example?

options.A: To retry the pattern match repeatedly until it succeeds
options.B: To extract a binding into the surrounding scope on a match, while handling the non-matching case with an early exit — keeping the happy path unindented
options.C: To make the compiler treat the pattern as exhaustive without listing other variants
options.D: To shadow `coin` with a new variable of a different type

questions.explanation: `let...else` puts the successful binding (`state`) directly in the outer scope and forces the non-matching case into a diverging `else` (here `return None;`). This keeps the main logic on the "happy path" without nesting it inside a `match` or `if let` block.

title: Managing Growing Projects with Packages, Crates, and Modules

questions.prompt: By Cargo's conventions, which files does the compiler treat as crate roots in a package?

options.A: `Cargo.toml` for the library crate and `Cargo.lock` for the binary crate
options.B: `src/main.rs` for a binary crate and `src/lib.rs` for a library crate
options.C: Any file in `src/` that contains a `mod` declaration
options.D: `src/root.rs`, which must be created manually for every crate

questions.explanation: Cargo passes the crate root files to `rustc`: `src/main.rs` is the crate root of a binary crate and `src/lib.rs` is the crate root of a library crate, both named after the package. The crate root is the source file the compiler starts from and forms the root module of the crate.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between a binary crate and a library crate?

options.A: Library crates may not contain modules; binary crates may
options.B: Binary crates cannot depend on library crates in the same package
options.C: The word `crate` in Rust always refers to a library; binaries are called `targets`
options.D: A binary crate compiles to an executable and must have a `fn main`; a library crate has no `main` and does not produce an executable

questions.explanation: Binary crates are programs you can compile to an executable and each must define `fn main`. Library crates define functionality to be shared (like `rand`) and have no `main`; when Rustaceans casually say "crate" they often mean a library crate, but both kinds exist.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about a Cargo package are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: A package can contain at most one library crate
options.B: A package must contain at least one crate, either a library or a binary
options.C: A package can contain at most one binary crate
options.D: Each file placed in `src/bin/` becomes a separate binary crate
options.E: A library crate in a package must contain a `fn main` function

questions.explanation: A package can hold at most one library crate, any number of binary crates, and must contain at least one crate of some kind. Extra binaries go in `src/bin/`, where each file is its own binary crate; library crates never need `fn main`.

questions.section: 7.2 Defining Modules to Control Scope and Privacy
questions.prompt: In the crate root you write `mod garden;` with a semicolon instead of a body. Where does the compiler look for the module's code?

options.A: In `src/garden.rs` or, in the older style, `src/garden/mod.rs`
options.B: In `garden.rs` in the package root, next to `Cargo.toml`
options.C: Nowhere; `mod garden;` is only a forward declaration and the code must appear later in the same file
options.D: In whatever file is listed under `[modules]` in `Cargo.toml`

questions.explanation: For a module declared in the crate root, the compiler checks (in order) for an inline body, then `src/garden.rs`, then the older-style `src/garden/mod.rs`. Modules are never registered in `Cargo.toml`.

questions.section: 7.2 Defining Modules to Control Scope and Privacy
questions.prompt: A library crate defines `mod front_of_house` at the top level of `src/lib.rs`. In the module tree, what is the parent of `front_of_house`?

options.A: It has no parent; top-level modules are roots of their own trees
options.B: A module named `lib`, created from the file name `lib.rs`
options.C: An implicit module named `crate`, formed by the contents of the crate root
options.D: The package itself, referred to by the package name from `Cargo.toml`

questions.explanation: The crate root file forms an implicit module named `crate` at the root of the module tree, so `front_of_house` is a child of `crate`. That is why absolute paths such as `crate::front_of_house::hosting` start with the `crate` keyword.

questions.section: 7.2 Defining Modules to Control Scope and Privacy
questions.prompt: What happens when this program in `src/main.rs` is compiled and run?

options.A: Compile error: `garden` is private, so the `use` statement cannot name it
options.B: It compiles and prints `Growing Asparagus!`
options.C: Compile error: `use` paths must always begin with `crate::`
options.D: Compile error: `grow` cannot be called through the `vegetables` shortcut

questions.explanation: Both the `use` and `mod garden` live directly in the crate root, so the private `garden` module is visible from that same scope; `vegetables` and `grow` are `pub`, so the path resolves. Relative `use` paths starting from the current module are allowed, so it prints `Growing Asparagus!`.

questions.section: 7.3 Paths for Referring to an Item in the Module Tree
questions.prompt: Which statement correctly describes absolute and relative paths in the module tree?

options.A: Absolute paths begin with the package name from `Cargo.toml`, even inside the same crate
options.B: Relative paths must always begin with the `self` keyword
options.C: Absolute paths use `.` as a separator, relative paths use `::`
options.D: An absolute path for code in the current crate starts with the literal `crate`; a relative path starts from the current module using an identifier, `self`, or `super`

questions.explanation: Absolute paths begin at the crate root: with `crate::` for the current crate, or with the crate's name for an external crate. Relative paths start from the current module and may begin with an identifier, `self`, or `super`; both styles separate identifiers with `::`.

questions.section: 7.3 Paths for Referring to an Item in the Module Tree
questions.prompt: This library crate fails to compile. What is the bug?

options.A: `front_of_house` must be marked `pub` for `eat_at_restaurant` to name it
options.B: Absolute paths starting with `crate::` cannot be used inside the crate that defines the item
options.C: `add_to_waitlist` is private: making `hosting` public does not make its contents public, so the function also needs `pub`
options.D: A `pub mod` is not allowed inside a private module

questions.explanation: Making a module public with `pub mod` only lets ancestor code refer to the module; the items inside stay private until each is marked `pub`, so the call fails with E0603. `front_of_house` itself is fine because `eat_at_restaurant` is defined in the same module (the crate root) and siblings can see each other.

questions.section: 7.3 Paths for Referring to an Item in the Module Tree
questions.prompt: What is the result of compiling and running this program?

options.A: Compile error: `deliver_order` is private, so it cannot be reached via `super::`
options.B: Compile error: `cook_order` is private and cannot be called
options.C: It prints `delivered` then `cooked`
options.D: It prints `cooked` then `delivered`

questions.explanation: Child modules can use items in their ancestor modules even when those items are not `pub`, so `super::deliver_order()` (like `..` in a filesystem) is legal, and `cook_order` is called from within its own module. The calls run in order: `cooked`, then `delivered`.

questions.section: 7.3 Paths for Referring to an Item in the Module Tree
questions.prompt: Inside a module you declare `pub struct Breakfast { toast: String }` and `pub enum Appetizer { Soup, Salad }`. What can code outside the module access?

options.A: The `Breakfast` type but not its `toast` field, and the `Appetizer` enum with both variants: struct fields stay private by default while `pub enum` makes all variants public
options.B: Everything: `pub` on a type always exposes all of its contents
options.C: Neither field nor variants: `pub` on a type only exposes its name for use in signatures
options.D: The `toast` field but not the enum variants: variants each need their own `pub`
