questions.explanation: A `pub struct` is public but each field is still private unless individually marked `pub`, which is why the book's `Breakfast` needs a public constructor like `summer`. In contrast, marking an enum `pub` makes all of its variants public, since an enum is not very useful otherwise.

questions.section: 7.4 Bringing Paths into Scope with the use Keyword
questions.prompt: According to the book, what is the idiomatic way to bring the function `add_to_waitlist` (in `crate::front_of_house::hosting`) into scope and call it?

options.A: `use crate::front_of_house::hosting::add_to_waitlist;` and call `add_to_waitlist()`
options.B: `use crate::front_of_house::*;` and call `hosting::add_to_waitlist()`
options.C: `use crate::front_of_house::hosting;` and call `hosting::add_to_waitlist()`
options.D: Functions cannot be brought into scope with `use`; only types can

questions.explanation: For functions, the idiom is to bring the parent module into scope so the call `hosting::add_to_waitlist()` makes clear the function is not locally defined. For structs, enums, and other items, the idiom is the opposite: spell out the full path, as in `use std::collections::HashMap;`.

questions.section: 7.4 Bringing Paths into Scope with the use Keyword
questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about `use` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: A `use` statement only creates the shortcut in the particular scope where it appears
options.B: `use std::io::Result as IoResult;` brings `std::io::Result` into scope under the new name `IoResult`
options.C: With `pub use`, external code can refer to the re-exported name as if it were defined in the re-exporting module
options.D: Bringing a private item into scope with `use` makes it accessible from anywhere
options.E: The glob operator `*` also imports the private items of a module

questions.explanation: A `use` shortcut is limited to the scope it is declared in, `as` renames an import, and `pub use` re-exports the name so callers can use the new path (useful for shaping a public API). `use` still obeys privacy rules, and the glob operator brings in only public items.

questions.section: 7.4 Bringing Paths into Scope with the use Keyword
questions.prompt: This library crate fails to compile with `failed to resolve: use of undeclared crate or module hosting`. Why?

options.A: The `use` shortcut applies only in the crate root scope, not inside the child module `customer`; the `use` must be moved into `customer` or the call changed to `super::hosting::add_to_waitlist()`
options.B: `use` statements must appear before all `mod` declarations in a file
options.C: `add_to_waitlist` is private to `hosting`, so the shortcut cannot see it
options.D: Relative paths are forbidden inside nested modules, so `hosting::` can never work there

questions.explanation: A `use` only creates a shortcut in the scope where it occurs; module `customer` is a different scope, so the crate-root shortcut does not apply there (and the compiler also warns the `use` is unused). Moving the `use` inside `customer` or writing `super::hosting::add_to_waitlist()` fixes it.

questions.section: 7.4 Bringing Paths into Scope with the use Keyword
questions.prompt: Which single statement brings exactly the same items into scope as the two lines `use std::cmp::Ordering;` and `use std::io;`?

options.A: `use std::cmp::{Ordering, io};`
options.B: `use std::{cmp::Ordering, io};`
options.C: `use std::*;`
options.D: `use std::io::{self, cmp::Ordering};`

questions.explanation: Nested paths merge imports that share a prefix: `use std::{cmp::Ordering, io};` brings in both items in one line. Writing `use std::cmp::{Ordering, io};` wrongly nests `io` under `cmp`, and the glob `use std::*;` imports every public item of `std`, not just those two.

questions.prompt: A crate is split into files as shown in the comments below, but compilation fails with `file not found for module hosting`. What is wrong?

options.A: `pub mod hosting;` is illegal in a non-root file; submodules must be declared in `src/lib.rs`
options.B: `src/front_of_house.rs` must be renamed `src/front_of_house/mod.rs` before it may declare submodules
options.C: Because `hosting` is declared inside `front_of_house`, its file must live in the parent's directory: `src/front_of_house/hosting.rs` (or `src/front_of_house/hosting/mod.rs`), not `src/hosting.rs`
options.D: The `hosting` module also needs an entry in `Cargo.toml` to be compiled

questions.explanation: The directory structure must mirror the module tree: a submodule declared in `src/front_of_house.rs` is looked up at `src/front_of_house/hosting.rs` or the older-style `src/front_of_house/hosting/mod.rs`. A file at `src/hosting.rs` would only match a `mod hosting;` declared in the crate root.

questions.prompt: `src/lib.rs` already contains `mod front_of_house;`, and the module's code lives in `src/front_of_house.rs`. Another part of the crate now needs to call code in that module. What should you do?

options.A: Add another `mod front_of_house;` declaration in each file that needs it, like `#include` in C
options.B: Use the `include!` macro to paste `src/front_of_house.rs` into the calling file
options.C: List `front_of_house.rs` under a `[modules]` section in `Cargo.toml`
options.D: Nothing extra: `mod` is declared only once in the module tree, and other code refers to the module's items with absolute or relative paths (optionally shortened with `use`)

questions.explanation: `mod` is not an include operation: you declare a module once, the compiler loads its file, and from then on it is part of the module tree. All other code reaches it through paths such as `crate::front_of_house::hosting`, possibly shortened with `use`.

questions.prompt: Why does `let v: Vec<i32> = Vec::new();` need a type annotation, while `let v = vec![1, 2, 3];` does not?

options.A: The `vec!` macro can only ever create vectors of `i32`, so no annotation is possible
options.B: `Vec::new()` provides no values, so the compiler cannot infer the element type; `vec![1, 2, 3]` lets it infer `i32` from the initial values
options.C: `Vec::new()` requires an annotation because vectors store heterogeneous types by default
options.D: The annotation is never actually required; it is purely a style convention

questions.explanation: `Vec<T>` is generic, and with an empty `Vec::new()` there are no elements from which to infer `T`. With `vec![1, 2, 3]`, the compiler infers `Vec<i32>` (the default integer type) from the values.

questions.prompt: Given `let v = vec![1, 2, 3, 4, 5];`, what is the difference between evaluating `&v[100]` and `v.get(100)`?

options.A: Both cause a panic at runtime
options.B: `&v[100]` panics at runtime; `v.get(100)` returns `None`
options.C: `&v[100]` is a compile-time error because the compiler can see the index is out of range
options.D: `v.get(100)` panics at runtime; `&v[100]` returns `None`

questions.explanation: Indexing with `[]` panics on an out-of-bounds index, while `get` returns an `Option<&T>` (`None` when out of range), letting you handle the missing element gracefully.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile and run this program?

options.A: It prints `The first element is: 1`
options.B: It compiles but panics at runtime when `push` reallocates the vector
options.C: It fails to compile: `v` cannot be borrowed as mutable because it is also borrowed as immutable (E0502)
options.D: It prints `The first element is: 6`

questions.explanation: `first` is an immutable borrow of `v` that is still used after `v.push(6)`, which needs a mutable borrow. The borrow checker rejects this because `push` might reallocate the vector's memory and invalidate `first`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.C: It fails to compile: cannot modify `v` while iterating over it

questions.explanation: Iterating with `&mut v` yields mutable references to each element; the `*` dereference operator lets the loop add 50 to each value in place. Modifying element values through the loop's own mutable borrow is allowed.

questions.prompt: A vector can only store elements of a single type. How does the book suggest storing an `i32`, an `f64`, and a `String` in the same vector?

options.A: Declare it as `Vec<i32, f64, String>` with multiple type parameters
options.B: Define an enum whose variants hold each type and store enum values, since all variants are the same enum type
options.C: Vectors automatically box mixed values, so you can push different types directly
options.D: It is impossible to represent values of different kinds in one vector in any form

questions.explanation: An enum like `SpreadsheetCell { Int(i32), Float(f64), Text(String) }` gives every element the same type — the enum — while each variant carries different data. Trait objects cover cases where the set of types isn't known at compile time.

questions.prompt: What happens to a vector's elements when the vector goes out of scope?

options.A: Only the vector itself is freed; heap-allocated elements like `String`s must be freed manually
options.B: The vector is freed and all of its contents are dropped as well
options.C: The elements leak until the program exits
options.D: The elements are dropped only if they implement the `Copy` trait

questions.explanation: Like any struct, a vector is freed when it goes out of scope, and dropping it also drops all of its elements. The borrow checker guarantees no references to elements outlive the vector.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.C: It fails to compile: `v[2]` is out of bounds
options.D: It panics at runtime: index out of bounds

questions.explanation: After `push(3)` the vector is `[1, 2, 3]`, so `v[1]` is 2 and `v[2]` is 3, printing 5. Out-of-bounds indexing is a runtime check, not a compile-time one — and here both indices are valid.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about `Vec<T>` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Elements are stored next to each other in memory, and `push` may reallocate and move them
options.B: `v.get(i)` returns an `Option<&T>` and never panics for an out-of-range index
options.C: A vector can directly store elements of several different types without any enum or trait-object wrapper
options.D: When a vector is dropped, all of its elements are dropped too
options.E: Indexing with `&v[i]` returns `None` when `i` is out of bounds

questions.explanation: Vectors store same-typed values contiguously on the heap, which is why growing may reallocate (and why holding a reference across a `push` is rejected). `get` returns an `Option`; `[]` indexing panics rather than returning `None`; mixed types require an enum or trait objects.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between `String` and `&str` in Rust?

options.A: `String` is an owned, growable, UTF-8 encoded string; `&str` is a borrowed string slice, which is what string literals are
options.B: `String` is stored on the stack, while `&str` is always stored on the heap
options.C: `String` can hold arbitrary bytes in any encoding, while `&str` must be ASCII
options.D: They are interchangeable aliases for the same type

questions.explanation: `String` is the standard library's owned, growable string type (a wrapper around `Vec<u8>` holding UTF-8), while `str`/`&str` is a borrowed slice view into string data. Both must be valid UTF-8.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile and run this program?

options.A: It fails to compile: `s2` was moved into `push_str` and can no longer be used
options.B: It prints `foobar bar` — `push_str` takes a `&str`, so it does not take ownership of `s2`
options.C: It fails to compile: `push_str` only accepts a `String`, not a string literal
options.D: It prints `foo bar` because `push_str` returns a new string instead of modifying `s1`

questions.explanation: `push_str` has the signature `fn push_str(&mut self, s: &str)`: it borrows its argument rather than taking ownership, so `s2` remains usable afterward, and it appends in place to `s1`.

questions.prompt: What is wrong with this program?

options.A: Nothing; it prints `Hello, Hello, world!`
options.B: `&s2` is the wrong type: `+` requires a `String` on the right-hand side, not a reference
options.C: It fails to compile: `s1` was moved by the `+` operator (whose `add` method takes `self`) and cannot be used afterward
options.D: It panics at runtime because `s1` was freed by the concatenation

questions.explanation: The `+` operator calls a method with a signature like `fn add(self, s: &str) -> String`, so it takes ownership of the left operand `s1`; using `s1` afterward is a compile-time "borrow of moved value" error. `&s2` is fine: `&String` deref-coerces to `&str`.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.A: It prints `h`
options.B: It fails to compile: `String` cannot be indexed by an integer because it does not implement `Index<usize>`
options.C: It compiles but panics at runtime with an index-out-of-bounds error
options.D: It prints `104`, the byte value of `h`

questions.explanation: Rust rejects integer indexing of strings at compile time. Because `String` stores variable-width UTF-8 bytes, `s[0]` would be ambiguous (byte? char?) and could return an invalid fragment of a character, so the `Index<usize>` operation simply isn't implemented.

questions.prompt: Each character in this Cyrillic string takes 2 bytes in UTF-8. What happens when you run this program?

options.A: It prints the first letter, `З`
options.B: It fails to compile: ranges cannot be used to slice strings
options.C: It panics at runtime: byte index 1 is not a char boundary
options.D: It prints the first byte of `З` as a number

questions.explanation: Slicing a string by a byte range compiles, but if the range ends in the middle of a multi-byte character, the program panics at runtime — exactly as if an invalid vector index were accessed. `&hello[0..4]` would work and yield `Зд`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?
questions.explanation: `len` returns the number of bytes, and each of these Cyrillic letters takes 2 bytes in UTF-8, so `len` is 4. `.chars()` iterates over Unicode scalar values, of which there are 2.

questions.prompt: Which statements about building and concatenating strings are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `push_str` takes a `&str` parameter and does not take ownership of its argument
options.B: `push` appends a single `char` to a `String`
options.C: The `+` operator takes ownership of its left-hand `String` operand
options.D: `format!` takes ownership of all of its arguments
options.E: `push_str` takes ownership of the string passed to it, so it cannot be used afterward

questions.explanation: `push_str(&mut self, s: &str)` borrows its argument, `push` takes a single `char`, and `+` calls `add(self, s: &str)`, consuming the left operand. `format!` uses references, so none of its arguments are moved — which is one reason the book recommends it for combining multiple strings.

questions.prompt: Which statements about inspecting UTF-8 encoded strings are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: The standard library includes an iterator over grapheme clusters (user-perceived letters)
options.B: `len()` on a `String` returns the number of bytes, not the number of characters
options.C: `.chars()` yields Unicode scalar values, so combining diacritics appear as separate items rather than as part of one letter
options.D: Slicing `&s[0..n]` always succeeds as long as `n <= s.len()`
options.E: `.bytes()` yields the raw `u8` values of the string's UTF-8 encoding

questions.explanation: Strings are `Vec<u8>` wrappers, so `len` counts bytes and `.bytes()` exposes them; `.chars()` gives Unicode scalar values, which don't always match user-perceived letters (e.g., Devanagari diacritics). Grapheme-cluster iteration requires an external crate, and byte-range slicing panics if a boundary falls mid-character.

questions.prompt: What must you do before you can use `HashMap` in your code?

options.A: Nothing — `HashMap` is included in the prelude like `Vec` and `String`
options.B: Bring it into scope with `use std::collections::HashMap;`, since it is not in the prelude
options.C: Add the external `hashmap` crate to `Cargo.toml`
options.D: Import it with `use std::hash::HashMap;`

questions.explanation: Of the three common collections, `HashMap` is the least often used, so it's not brought into scope automatically by the prelude. It lives in `std::collections` and requires a `use` statement (no external crate needed).

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.C: It panics at runtime because the key `Red` is missing
options.D: It fails to compile: `get` returns an `i32`, so `copied` cannot be called on it

questions.explanation: `get` returns `Option<&V>` — here `None` because `"Red"` was never inserted. `.copied()` turns `Option<&i32>` into `Option<i32>`, and `.unwrap_or(0)` supplies the default 0 instead of panicking.

questions.prompt: When you iterate over a hash map with `for (key, value) in &scores`, in what order are the pairs visited?

options.A: In the order the keys were inserted
options.B: Sorted alphabetically by key
options.C: In an arbitrary order that may differ from run to run
options.D: Sorted by value, ascending

questions.explanation: A `HashMap` stores entries according to its hashing function, so iteration order is arbitrary and not guaranteed to be stable. If you need ordering, you must sort the entries yourself or use a different structure.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.A: It prints `Favorite color`
options.B: It fails to compile: `field_name` was moved into the map by `insert` and cannot be used afterward
options.C: It fails to compile: hash map keys must be `&str`, not `String`
options.D: It compiles but prints an empty string because the map now owns the data

questions.explanation: For owned types like `String`, `insert` moves both the key and the value into the hash map, which becomes their owner. Using `field_name` afterward is a compile-time "borrow of moved value" error; `Copy` types like `i32` would be copied instead.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.D: It panics at runtime: duplicate key inserted

questions.explanation: Each key in a hash map can have only one value at a time. Calling `insert` with a key that already exists replaces the old value, so only `"Blue": 25` remains.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.C: It fails to compile: `count` is a `&mut i32`, which cannot be incremented

questions.explanation: `entry(word).or_insert(0)` returns a mutable reference (`&mut i32`) to the word's counter, inserting 0 first if the word is new. Dereferencing with `*count += 1` increments it, and `"world"` appears twice in the text.

questions.prompt: After this code runs, what value is associated with the key `Blue`?

options.A: 50, because `or_insert` always sets the value
options.B: 10, because `or_insert` only inserts when the key has no value; `Blue` already has one
options.C: 60, because `or_insert` adds to the existing value
options.D: The program fails to compile because `entry` cannot be called on a key that already exists

questions.explanation: The `entry` API's `or_insert` inserts the given value only if the key is vacant and otherwise leaves the existing value untouched, returning a mutable reference to whichever value is there. So `Blue` stays 10 while `Yellow` gets 50.

questions.prompt: Which statements about `HashMap<K, V>` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Calling `insert` with a key that already exists keeps the old value unchanged
options.B: `get` returns an `Option<&V>` rather than the value itself
options.C: Iterating over `&map` visits key/value pairs in an arbitrary order
options.D: Owned `String` keys and values are moved into the map when inserted
options.E: The default SipHash hasher was chosen because it is the fastest hashing algorithm available

questions.explanation: `get` yields `Option<&V>`, iteration order is arbitrary, and the map takes ownership of owned keys and values. `insert` on an existing key overwrites the old value, and SipHash was chosen for resistance to hash-flooding DoS attacks, trading away some speed (you can swap hashers via `BuildHasher`).

questions.prompt: By default, what happens when a Rust program calls the `panic!` macro?

options.A: The program prints a failure message, unwinds the stack cleaning up each frame's data, and then quits
options.B: The program immediately aborts without any cleanup, leaving the OS to reclaim all memory
options.C: The current function returns an `Err` value to its caller so the error can be handled
options.D: The program continues running but logs the message to stderr

questions.explanation: By default a panic prints a failure message, unwinds the stack (walking back up and cleaning up data from each function), and quits. Aborting without cleanup only happens if you opt in with `panic = 'abort'`.

questions.prompt: What is the effect of adding `panic = 'abort'` under `[profile.release]` in `Cargo.toml`?

options.A: Panics in release builds are converted into `Result::Err` values automatically
options.B: On panic, the program ends immediately without unwinding, memory cleanup is left to the OS, and the resulting binary can be smaller
options.C: The compiler rejects any code path that could panic in release mode
options.D: Backtraces are printed automatically on every panic in release builds

questions.explanation: `panic = 'abort'` switches from unwinding to immediately aborting the process: no stack cleanup runs, the OS reclaims memory, and the binary shrinks because the unwinding machinery can be omitted.

questions.prompt: What happens when this program is compiled and run?

options.A: Compile error: the compiler detects the out-of-bounds index
options.B: It reads whatever memory happens to be at that location (a buffer overread), which is undefined behavior
options.C: It compiles, then panics at runtime with an "index out of bounds" message
options.D: It compiles and runs fine because the indexed value is never used

questions.explanation: Indexing a `Vec` past its end compiles but panics at runtime ("index out of bounds: the len is 3 but the index is 99"). Rust panics here precisely to prevent a C-style buffer overread of whatever memory lies past the end.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about panics and backtraces are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Setting the `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable makes Rust print a backtrace when a panic occurs
options.B: Getting file and line information in a backtrace requires debug symbols, which are enabled by default when building without `--release`
options.C: `RUST_BACKTRACE` is a key you set in `Cargo.toml`, not an environment variable
options.D: A full backtrace is printed by default on every panic, with no configuration needed
options.E: To find the source of a panic, read the backtrace from the top down until you reach the first frame that mentions code you wrote

questions.explanation: Backtraces are opt-in via the `RUST_BACKTRACE` environment variable (not `Cargo.toml`), need debug symbols for useful file/line info, and are read top-down to the first frame in your own code. By default only the panic message and a note about `RUST_BACKTRACE` are printed.

questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly describes the `Result<T, E>` enum?

options.A: It has variants `Ok(T)` for success and `Err(E)` for failure, where `T` and `E` are generic type parameters
options.B: It has variants `Some(T)` and `None`, where `None` represents an error
options.C: It has variants `Ok(T)` and `Panic(E)`, where `Panic` immediately aborts the program
options.D: It is a struct with a boolean `success` field and a payload field

questions.explanation: `Result<T, E>` is an enum with exactly two variants: `Ok(T)` holding the success value and `Err(E)` holding the error value. `Some`/`None` belong to `Option<T>`.

questions.prompt: Assuming `hello.txt` does not exist, what happens when this program runs?

options.A: Compile error: the `Result` returned by `File::open` must be handled with `match`
options.B: It compiles, then panics at runtime with a message that includes the `Err` value from `File::open`
options.C: `greeting_file` is bound to `None` and the program continues
options.D: `main` returns an `Err` value and the process exits with a nonzero code

questions.explanation: `unwrap` returns the value inside `Ok`, but if the `Result` is `Err` it calls `panic!` with a default message showing the error. There is no compile error, and `main` here returns `()`, not a `Result`.

questions.prompt: Suppose `hello.txt` does not exist and the current directory is writable. What does this program do?

options.A: It panics with "Problem opening the file" because `File::open` failed
options.B: It matches `ErrorKind::NotFound`, creates `hello.txt`, and binds the new file handle to `greeting_file`
options.C: Compile error: you cannot nest a `match` inside another `match` arm
options.D: It panics with "Problem creating the file" because the original open failed

questions.explanation: `io::Error` exposes `kind()` returning an `io::ErrorKind`; a missing file yields `ErrorKind::NotFound`, so the inner `match` creates the file and binds its handle. The catch-all `_` arm only runs for other error kinds, such as permission errors.

questions.prompt: Why do most Rustaceans prefer `expect` over `unwrap` in production-quality code?

options.A: `expect` returns a `Result` instead of panicking, so the error remains recoverable
options.B: `expect` lets you supply a message explaining why the operation should succeed, giving better context when it panics
options.C: `expect` is faster because it skips the runtime check that `unwrap` performs
options.D: `unwrap` is deprecated and will be removed in a future edition

questions.explanation: Both `unwrap` and `expect` panic on `Err`; the difference is that `expect` uses your message as the panic text, conveying your assumption and making debugging easier. Neither returns a `Result`, and `unwrap` is not deprecated.

questions.prompt: In this manual error-propagation pattern, what does the arm `Err(e) => return Err(e)` accomplish?

options.A: It ends the whole function early, passing the error value back to the caller so the caller decides how to handle it
options.B: It panics with the error `e` because errors in `match` arms are unrecoverable
options.C: It retries `File::open` once before giving up
options.D: It converts the `io::Error` into a `String` so it can be printed

questions.explanation: `return Err(e)` early-returns from `read_username_from_file`, propagating the error to the calling code. This is exactly the boilerplate the `?` operator condenses.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about the `?` operator are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Applied to an `Err(e)` value, it returns early from the enclosing function, converting the error to the function's error type via the `From` trait
options.B: Applied to an `Ok(v)` value, the expression evaluates to `v` and execution continues
options.C: It can be used in any function, regardless of the function's return type
options.D: It automatically converts between `Result` and `Option` so you can mix them freely in the same function
options.E: In a function returning `Option`, `?` applied to a `None` value returns `None` early

questions.explanation: `?` unwraps `Ok`/`Some` or early-returns `Err`/`None`, calling `From::from` on errors to convert them to the declared error type. It only works in functions whose return type is compatible (Result, Option, or another `FromResidual` type), and it never converts Result to Option or vice versa — you need `ok()` or `ok_or()` for that.

questions.prompt: This program fails to compile. What is the bug?

options.A: `File::open` does not return a `Result`, so `?` cannot be applied to it
options.B: `?` is used in a `main` that returns `()`; `?` only works in functions returning `Result`, `Option`, or another `FromResidual` type
options.C: The file name must be a `String`, not a string literal
options.D: Nothing is wrong at compile time; it panics at runtime if the file is missing

questions.explanation: The `?` operator early-returns an error, so the enclosing function's return type must support it; a `main` returning `()` does not, producing error E0277. Changing `main` to return `Result<(), Box<dyn Error>>` fixes it.

questions.prompt: This function fails to compile. Why?

options.A: `?` cannot convert the `std::io::Error` from `File::open` into `String`, because `String` has no `From<std::io::Error>` implementation
options.B: `?` cannot be used on the result of `File::open` because `File` is not `Copy`
options.C: The function must return `Result<File, std::io::Error>`; the error type of a `Result` returned with `?` can never differ from the original
options.D: `Ok(file)` moves `file` out of the function, which is not allowed after using `?`

questions.explanation: `?` calls `From::from` to convert the error into the function's declared error type; since there is no `impl From<std::io::Error> for String`, the conversion fails to compile. The error type may differ from the original as long as a suitable `From` impl exists, so C is too strong.

questions.prompt: Assuming `no-such-file.txt` does not exist, what happens when this program runs?

options.A: Compile error: `?` cannot convert an `std::io::Error` into `Box<dyn Error>`
options.B: It prints "opened" and exits with code 0 because `Ok(())` is always reached
options.C: It panics at the `?` operator with an "unwrap on Err" message
options.D: "opened" is never printed; `main` returns the error and the process exits with a nonzero exit code

questions.explanation: `Box<dyn Error>` accepts any error type via `From`, so `?` propagates the `io::Error` out of `main` before the `println!` runs. When `main` returns `Err`, the program exits with a nonzero code (returning `Ok(())` would exit with 0); `?` propagates rather than panics.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: Some('o') then None
options.B: Some('d') then None
options.C: Compile error: `?` cannot be used on an `Option` value
options.D: Some('o') then a panic, because calling `?` on `None` panics

questions.explanation: The first line of "hello\nworld" is "hello", whose last char is 'o'. For the empty string, `lines().next()` is `None`, so `?` makes the function return `None` early — `?` on `Option` propagates `None`; it never panics.

questions.section: 9.3 To panic! or Not to panic!
questions.prompt: According to the book, what is the good default choice for a library function whose failure is an expected possibility (e.g., a parser given malformed data)?

options.A: Call `panic!` so the bug is caught immediately
options.B: Return a `Result`, letting the calling code decide whether to recover or to treat the error as unrecoverable
options.C: Call `unwrap` internally and document the panic
options.D: Return a sentinel value such as `-1` or an empty string

questions.explanation: Returning `Result` is the good default because it preserves the caller's options: callers can recover, propagate, or still choose to panic. Panicking makes the decision on the caller's behalf and is unrecoverable.

questions.section: 9.3 To panic! or Not to panic!
questions.prompt: In which situations does the book consider it appropriate to call `unwrap` or `expect`? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: In examples and prototype code, where robust error handling would obscure the point
options.B: In tests, where a panic is exactly how the test should fail
options.C: When your own logic guarantees the value is `Ok`, but the compiler cannot see it — e.g., `"127.0.0.1".parse::<IpAddr>().expect(...)` on a hardcoded valid string
options.D: In library code that parses arbitrary user-supplied input
options.E: Whenever proper error handling would take more than a few extra lines of code

questions.explanation: Examples/prototypes, tests, and cases where you have logic ensuring the `Result` is `Ok` (like a hardcoded valid IP) justify `unwrap`/`expect`; the `expect` message should document the assumption. Expected failures such as malformed user input should be surfaced as `Result`, and mere convenience is not a justification.

questions.section: 9.3 To panic! or Not to panic!
questions.prompt: This validation type is meant to guarantee that every `Guess` holds a value between 1 and 100, but it fails to do so. What is the bug?

options.A: `new` should return `Result<Guess, String>` — panicking in a constructor never compiles
options.B: The `value` field is `pub`, so code outside the module can construct `Guess { value: 500 }` or overwrite the field, bypassing the validation in `new`
options.C: The range check is wrong: it should be `value <= 1 || value >= 100`
options.D: `panic!` cannot use the `{value}` inline-capture syntax in its format string

questions.explanation: The invariant only holds if the field is private, forcing all outside code through `Guess::new`; a `pub` field lets callers construct or mutate a `Guess` directly with an out-of-range value. The fix is a private field plus a public getter like `pub fn value(&self) -> i32`.

questions.section: 9.3 To panic! or Not to panic!
questions.prompt: A colleague says: "`Guess::new` panicking on an out-of-range value is bad design — every fallible constructor must return `Result` instead." Per the book's guidelines, why is panicking acceptable here?

options.A: Because panics are cheaper than allocating an `Err` value
options.B: Because `Result` cannot be returned from associated functions like `new`
options.C: Passing an out-of-range value violates the function's contract and indicates a bug in the calling code, which should be fixed during development rather than handled at runtime
options.D: Because the standard library never returns `Result` from constructors, and libraries should match it

questions.explanation: The book's guideline is to panic when a contract is violated, because that signals a caller-side bug that should be fixed, not an expected condition to recover from; such panic conditions should be documented in the API. Functions elsewhere can then accept `Guess` and rely on the 1..=100 invariant without rechecking.

questions.prompt: In the signature `fn largest<T>(list: &[T]) -> &T`, what does the `<T>` after the function name do?

options.A: It declares a generic type parameter named `T` so the rest of the signature can use it
options.B: It imports the type `T` from the standard library
options.C: It tells the compiler to accept only types whose name is exactly `T`
options.D: It marks the function as unsafe to call with more than one type

questions.explanation: Angle brackets after the function name declare generic type parameters. The declaration must appear before `T` can be used in the parameter list and return type.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile and run this program?

options.A: It compiles; `x` is inferred as `i32` and `y` as `f64`
options.B: Compile error: `x` and `y` must have the same type `T`, but an integer and a float were given
options.C: It compiles; the integer `5` is automatically converted to `5.0`
options.D: Runtime panic due to a type mismatch when constructing the struct

questions.explanation: `Point<T>` uses a single generic parameter for both fields, so `x` and `y` must be the same concrete type. Using `5` fixes `T` as an integer, so `4.0` causes a mismatched-types compile error.

questions.prompt: How can you define a `Point` struct so that `Point { x: 5, y: 4.0 }` compiles?

options.A: struct Point<T> { x: T, y: T } — a single parameter already allows different field types
options.B: struct Point<T, U> { x: T, y: U } — use two generic parameters, one per field
options.C: struct Point { x: auto, y: auto } — use the `auto` keyword for inference
options.D: It is impossible; struct fields can never have different generic types

questions.explanation: Declaring multiple generic parameters, `Point<T, U>`, lets each field take its own concrete type, so `x: 5` (integer) and `y: 4.0` (float) are both allowed.

questions.prompt: Why must you write `impl<T> Point<T>` (with `<T>` right after `impl`) when defining methods on the generic struct `Point<T>`?

options.A: It restricts the methods to the concrete type named `T` only
options.B: It declares `T` as a generic parameter so the compiler knows `Point<T>` is a generic type, not a concrete one
options.C: It is optional style; `impl Point<T>` alone means exactly the same thing
options.D: It makes the methods generic over lifetimes as well as types

questions.explanation: Declaring `T` just after `impl` tells Rust that the `T` in `Point<T>` is a generic parameter being implemented over, rather than a concrete type. Without it, `impl Point<T>` would refer to a (nonexistent) concrete type named `T`.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.A: It compiles and prints the distance, converting the integers to floats
options.B: Compile error: `distance_from_origin` exists only on `Point<f32>`, and `p` is a `Point<i32>`
options.C: Runtime panic because `powi` is not defined for integers
options.D: Compile error: an `impl` block may not name a concrete type like `f32`

questions.explanation: An `impl Point<f32>` block defines methods only for that concrete instantiation. `p` is inferred as `Point<i32>`, which has no `distance_from_origin` method, so the call is a compile error.

questions.prompt: A colleague claims: "Using generics makes Rust programs slower at runtime than writing duplicate concrete functions." Why is this wrong?

options.A: Rust performs monomorphization: at compile time it generates specialized code for each concrete type, so generics have zero runtime cost
options.B: It is actually correct: generics are resolved through vtables at runtime
options.C: Generics are faster because the compiler skips type checking for generic code
options.D: Generics are interpreted at runtime, but the JIT makes them as fast as concrete code

questions.explanation: The compiler monomorphizes generic code, producing a concrete version per type used (e.g., `Option<i32>` and `Option<f64>` become separate specialized definitions), so performance matches hand-written concrete code.

questions.prompt: This function fails to compile. What is the bug?

options.A: The `>` comparison requires `T` to implement `PartialOrd`, so the signature needs the bound `T: std::cmp::PartialOrd`
options.B: `&list[0]` is a dangling reference because `list` is borrowed
options.C: You cannot return `&T` from a generic function; it must return `T` by value
options.D: The loop variable `item` shadows `largest`, causing an infinite loop

questions.explanation: Not every possible `T` supports `>`. Comparison with `>` is defined by the `PartialOrd` trait, so the function must constrain `T` with `fn largest<T: PartialOrd>(list: &[T]) -> &T`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.B: p3.x = Hello, p3.y = 10.4
options.C: Compile error: `mixup` cannot mix two different `Point` instantiations

questions.explanation: `mixup` returns `Point<X1, Y2>`: `x` comes from `self` (`p1.x = 5`) and `y` from `other` (`p2.y = 'c'`). Method-level generics `X2, Y2` are independent of the struct's `X1, Y1`, so mixing instantiations is fine.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about Rust generics are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Generic code is turned into specialized concrete code for each type used, via monomorphization at compile time
options.B: Generic functions always use dynamic dispatch through a vtable at runtime
options.C: Enum variants can hold generic types, as in `Option<T>`'s `Some(T)` and `Result<T, E>`'s `Ok(T)` and `Err(E)`
options.D: A generic parameter declared after `fn` in a method signature belongs to that method only, not to the struct's `impl` block
options.E: In `struct Point<T> { x: T, y: T }`, the fields `x` and `y` may hold two different concrete types in one instance

questions.explanation: Monomorphization gives generics zero runtime cost (so B is false), enums like `Option<T>` are generic, and generics after `fn` are method-local. A single `T` forces both fields of `Point<T>` to be the same type (so E is false).

questions.prompt: What is a trait in Rust?

options.A: A collection of method signatures (optionally with default bodies) that defines behavior a type can implement and share
options.B: A concrete class that other types inherit fields and methods from
options.C: A compile-time macro that generates struct definitions
options.D: A runtime table mapping type names to their memory layouts

questions.explanation: A trait like `pub trait Summary { fn summarize(&self) -> String; }` groups method signatures describing shared behavior; types then provide implementations with `impl Summary for Type`.

questions.prompt: In your own crate (which defines neither `Display` nor `Vec`), this code fails to compile. Why?

options.A: The orphan rule: you can only implement a trait for a type if at least one of the trait or the type is local to your crate — here both are external
options.B: `Display` cannot be implemented for container types like `Vec`
options.C: The `fmt` method has the wrong signature for `Display`
options.D: You must write `impl<T> fmt::Display for Vec<T>` to cover all element types first

questions.explanation: Both `Display` and `Vec<String>` are defined outside your crate, so coherence (the orphan rule) forbids the impl. This prevents two crates from providing conflicting implementations for the same type.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: (Read more from @horse_ebooks...)
options.B: Compile error: `SocialPost` does not implement `summarize`
options.C: @horse_ebooks: of course, as you probably already know, people
options.D: (Read more from horse_ebooks...)

questions.explanation: The default `summarize` implementation calls the required method `summarize_author`, which the impl provides. Implementing only the required method is enough; the default fills in the rest.

questions.prompt: The parameter syntax `pub fn notify(item: &impl Summary)` is syntax sugar for which longer form?

options.A: pub fn notify<T: Summary>(item: &T)
options.B: pub fn notify(item: &dyn Summary)
options.C: pub fn notify(item: &Summary)
options.D: pub fn notify<T>(item: &T) — the bound is inferred automatically

questions.explanation: `impl Trait` in argument position is shorthand for a generic type parameter with a trait bound: `<T: Summary>(item: &T)`. It still uses static dispatch, unlike `&dyn Summary`.

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between these two signatures?
questions.code:
pub fn notify_a(item1: &impl Summary, item2: &impl Summary) { /* ... */ }

pub fn notify_b<T: Summary>(item1: &T, item2: &T) { /* ... */ }

options.A: `notify_a` allows `item1` and `item2` to be two different concrete types; `notify_b` forces both to be the same concrete type
options.B: They are fully equivalent in every call
options.C: `notify_a` uses dynamic dispatch while `notify_b` uses static dispatch
options.D: `notify_b` forces both arguments to be the same value, not just the same type

questions.explanation: Each `impl Summary` introduces its own independent generic parameter, so `notify_a` accepts, say, a `NewsArticle` and a `SocialPost` together. A single named `T` requires both arguments to have the same concrete type.

questions.prompt: Which of these are valid ways to require that a parameter's type implements BOTH `Summary` and `Display`? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: pub fn notify(item: &(impl Summary + Display))
options.B: pub fn notify<T: Summary + Display>(item: &T)
options.C: pub fn notify<T>(item: &T) where T: Summary + Display
options.D: pub fn notify(item: &impl Summary && Display)
options.E: pub fn notify<T: Summary, Display>(item: &T)

questions.explanation: Multiple trait bounds are combined with `+`, either inline on `impl Trait`, on a generic parameter, or in a `where` clause. `&&` is not bound syntax, and a bare `Display` after a comma declares a second type parameter, not a bound.

questions.prompt: Assuming both `NewsArticle` and `SocialPost` implement `Summary`, why does this fail to compile?

options.A: Returning `impl Summary` requires a single concrete return type, but the two branches return different types; a trait object like `Box<dyn Summary>` would be needed
options.B: `impl Trait` is only allowed in parameter position, never as a return type
options.C: The `if` and `else` branches must return references, not owned values
options.D: `Summary` must also implement `Sized` before it can be returned

questions.explanation: `-> impl Summary` means the function returns one specific concrete type that the compiler infers; branches returning `NewsArticle` versus `SocialPost` violate that. Heterogeneous returns require trait objects such as `Box<dyn Summary>`.

questions.prompt: Given the definitions below, what happens if you call `pair.cmp_display()` where `pair` is a `Pair<Vec<i32>>` (and `Vec<i32>` does not implement `Display`)?

options.A: Compile error: `cmp_display` is only defined when `T: Display + PartialOrd`, so it does not exist for `Pair<Vec<i32>>`
options.B: It compiles but panics at runtime when trying to format the vector
options.C: It compiles and prints the vector using `Debug` formatting as a fallback
options.D: Compile error: you cannot have two `impl` blocks for the same struct

questions.explanation: Trait bounds on an `impl` block conditionally implement methods: `cmp_display` exists only for `Pair<T>` where `T: Display + PartialOrd`. For other `T`, the method simply is not defined, which is a compile-time error, not a runtime one.

questions.prompt: Why can you call `3.to_string()` even though the integer types never explicitly implement `ToString`?

options.A: The standard library has a blanket implementation `impl<T: Display> ToString for T`, so every `Display` type gets `to_string` automatically
options.B: `to_string` is a compiler built-in available on every type
options.C: Integers implement `ToString` directly via `#[derive(ToString)]` in the standard library
options.D: The compiler auto-derives `ToString` for any type used with the `{}` format specifier

questions.explanation: A blanket implementation implements a trait for every type satisfying a bound. Because integers implement `Display`, the stdlib's `impl<T: Display> ToString for T` gives them `to_string` for free.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about traits are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: To call a trait method on a value, the trait must be in scope (e.g., `use aggregator::Summary;`)
options.B: An overriding implementation of a method can call that same method's default implementation
options.C: A default method body may call other methods of the same trait, including ones with no default that implementors must provide
options.D: You may implement an external trait for an external type as long as you add a `#[orphan]` attribute
options.E: Writing `impl Summary for NewsArticle {}` with an empty block makes `NewsArticle` use the trait's default implementations

questions.explanation: Traits must be in scope to call their methods, defaults can call required methods, and an empty impl block opts into all defaults. It is not possible to call the default implementation from an overriding implementation, and there is no attribute that bypasses the orphan rule.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.A: It prints `r: 5` because the value 5 is copied into `r`
options.B: Compile error: `x` does not live long enough — `r` would be a dangling reference after the inner scope ends
options.C: Runtime panic when `println!` dereferences freed memory
options.D: It prints an unspecified garbage value

questions.explanation: `x` is dropped at the end of the inner scope, but `r` (which borrows `x`) is used afterward. The borrow checker compares the two lifetimes and rejects the program at compile time (error E0597); Rust never lets this reach runtime.

questions.prompt: This function fails with "missing lifetime specifier". Why can't the compiler accept it as written?

options.A: The compiler cannot tell whether the returned reference borrows from `x` or `y`, so it needs a generic lifetime like `fn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str`
options.B: Functions can never return string slices; the return type must be `String`
options.C: The elision rules always fail for `&str` parameters, even with a single parameter
options.D: The bug is the comparison: `len()` cannot be called on borrowed strings

questions.explanation: With two reference parameters and no `&self`, the elision rules cannot determine the output lifetime, and the return could refer to either input. Annotating all three with `'a` expresses the required relationship.

questions.prompt: In `fn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str`, what does the lifetime `'a` actually mean for callers?

options.A: The returned reference is valid for the smaller (overlapping) region of the lifetimes of `x` and `y`
options.B: Both arguments are forced to live exactly as long as each other, or the call will not compile
options.C: The annotation extends the lifetimes of `x` and `y` until the return value is dropped
options.D: The returned reference always has the `'static` lifetime

questions.explanation: Lifetime annotations describe relationships; they never change how long values live. The concrete lifetime substituted for `'a` is the part where both inputs are valid — i.e., the shorter of the two — and the result is usable only within it.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.A: It prints `The longest string is long string is long` because `result` actually points into `string1`, which is still alive
options.B: Compile error: `string2` does not live long enough, because `'a` becomes the shorter lifetime and `result` is used after the inner scope
options.C: Runtime panic when `println!` reads the freed `string2`
options.D: Compile error: `result` must be initialized when it is declared

questions.explanation: The signature ties the return to the shorter of the two input lifetimes ('a = the overlap). Even though the returned reference would in fact point to `string1`, the compiler reasons from the signature, so using `result` after `string2` is dropped is rejected.

questions.prompt: The function `fn first_word(s: &str) -> &str` compiles without any lifetime annotations, while `fn longest(x: &str, y: &str) -> &str` does not. Which elision rule explains why `first_word` is accepted?

options.A: Rule 2: with exactly one input lifetime parameter, that lifetime is assigned to all output lifetimes, so the signature becomes `fn first_word<'a>(s: &'a str) -> &'a str`
options.B: Rule 1: every reference parameter gets its own lifetime, which also covers the return type automatically for any number of parameters
options.C: Rule 3: the lifetime of `&self` is assigned to the output — this applies to all free functions
options.D: String slices are always `'static`, so `first_word` needs no lifetime relationship

questions.explanation: After rule 1 gives `s` a lifetime `'a`, rule 2 (exactly one input lifetime) assigns `'a` to the output. `longest` has two distinct input lifetimes and no `&self`, so no rule determines its output lifetime and annotations are required.

questions.prompt: This implementation of `longest` fails to compile. What is the fundamental problem?

options.A: It returns a reference to `result`, a local `String` that is dropped when the function ends — no lifetime annotation can fix that; return an owned `String` instead
options.B: `'a` must also be applied to `x` and `y` for the code to compile; adding those annotations fixes it
options.C: `as_str()` is not allowed inside functions with lifetime parameters
options.D: It compiles fine but leaks the `String` at runtime

questions.explanation: The value `result` is owned by the function and dropped at its end, so any returned reference to it would dangle (error E0515). Lifetime syntax cannot extend a value's life; the fix is to return the owned `String` and move it out.

questions.prompt: Why does this struct definition require the lifetime parameter `'a`?

options.A: Any struct field that is a reference needs a lifetime annotation, so the compiler can ensure an `ImportantExcerpt` never outlives the data `part` refers to
options.B: All structs in Rust require a lifetime parameter, whether or not they hold references
options.C: `'a` makes the struct heap-allocated so the reference stays valid
options.D: It is only a documentation convention; the compiler ignores lifetime parameters on structs

questions.explanation: Structs can hold references, but each reference field needs a declared lifetime so the borrow checker can enforce that the struct instance does not outlive the borrowed data.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about lifetimes and the elision rules are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Elision rule 1: the compiler assigns a separate lifetime parameter to each parameter that is a reference
options.B: Elision rule 2: if there is exactly one input lifetime parameter, it is assigned to all output lifetime parameters
options.C: Lifetime annotations extend how long the referenced values actually live
options.D: Elision rule 3: in a method with `&self` or `&mut self`, the lifetime of `self` is assigned to all output lifetime parameters
options.E: Annotating a reference with `'static` is the recommended first fix for most lifetime errors

questions.explanation: A, B, and D restate the three elision rules. Annotations only describe relationships for the borrow checker — they never change actual lifetimes — and `'static` should be used only when data genuinely lives for the whole program, not as a blanket fix.

questions.prompt: What does this program (combining generics, trait bounds, and lifetimes) print?

options.A: Announcement! Today! — then — The longest string is abcd
options.B: Compile error: lifetime parameters and type parameters cannot be declared in the same angle-bracket list
options.C: Announcement! Today! — then — The longest string is xyz
options.D: Compile error: `T` needs a lifetime annotation as well

questions.explanation: Lifetime and type parameters share one generic list (`<'a, T>`), and `where T: Display` lets `ann` be printed. `"abcd"` (length 4) is longer than `"xyz"` (length 3), so the annotated-lifetime reference to `s1` is returned.

questions.prompt: How does the Rust test runner know which functions in a crate are tests?

options.A: It runs every function defined in a file named `test.rs`
options.B: It runs every function annotated with the `#[test]` attribute
options.C: It runs every function whose name starts with `test_`
options.D: It runs every `pub` function inside a module named `tests`

questions.explanation: A test in Rust is any function annotated with the `#[test]` attribute. When you run `cargo test`, Cargo builds a test runner binary that runs each annotated function and reports whether it passes or fails; names and file locations are just conventions.

questions.prompt: What happens when this test runs under `cargo test`?

options.A: The test passes
options.B: The test fails, printing `assertion \`left == right\` failed` with `left: 5` and `right: 4`
options.C: The test fails, printing `left: 4` and `right: 5` because the expected value is always shown as `left`
options.D: The code does not compile because `assert_eq!` cannot compare a variable to a literal

questions.explanation: `add_two(2)` returns `5` because of the `a + 3` bug, so `assert_eq!` panics. The failure output labels the first argument `left` (here `result`, which is `5`) and the second `right` (here `4`), printing both with `Debug` formatting.

questions.prompt: A teammate insists that `assert_eq!` must be called as `assert_eq!(expected, actual)` or the comparison will be wrong. What is the actual convention in Rust?

options.A: They are right: the first argument must be the expected value or the test misbehaves
options.B: The order is reversed: the actual value must come first or the macro panics
options.C: The order does not affect the result; Rust just labels the two arguments `left` and `right` in the failure message
options.D: The order only matters for `assert_ne!`, not `assert_eq!`

questions.explanation: Unlike some languages that call the arguments `expected` and `actual`, Rust names them `left` and `right`, and the order in which we specify the value we expect versus the value the code produces doesn't change whether the assertion passes. It only changes which side each value is printed on in a failure message.

questions.prompt: When this test runs, what does the failure output contain?

options.A: Only the generic message `assertion failed: result.contains("Carol")`
options.B: The custom message `Greeting did not contain name, value was `Hello!``
options.C: Nothing; the test passes because `greeting` returns a non-empty string
options.D: It does not compile: `assert!` accepts exactly one argument

questions.explanation: Arguments after the condition are passed along to `format!`, so the panic message is the custom string with `{result}` interpolated to `Hello!`. Custom messages replace the generic `assertion failed` text and make failures easier to debug.

questions.prompt: The two panic messages in `Guess::new` were accidentally swapped. What is the result of running this test?

options.A: The test passes because the code panicked, and that is all `#[should_panic]` requires
options.B: The test fails with a note that the panic message did not contain the expected string
options.C: The code does not compile because `should_panic` does not accept an `expected` parameter
options.D: The test is reported as ignored because the panic message did not match

questions.explanation: With `expected = "..."`, the test passes only if the panic message contains that substring. `Guess::new(200)` panics with `"...greater than or equal to 1..."`, which does not contain `"less than or equal to 100"`, so the harness fails the test and reports that the panic did not contain the expected string.

questions.prompt: This test is rejected by the compiler. Why?

options.A: Nothing is wrong; returning `Err` counts as a panic, so the test passes
options.B: `#[should_panic]` cannot be used on a test that returns `Result<T, E>`; the two are incompatible
options.C: Test functions are never allowed to have a return type
options.D: `Err` values must be created with the `?` operator inside tests

questions.explanation: You can't use the `#[should_panic]` annotation on tests that use `Result<T, E>`. Returning `Err` is a failure signal, not a panic, so the combination is meaningless and rejected. To assert that an operation returns an `Err`, use `assert!(value.is_err())` instead.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are true about test functions that return `Result<(), String>`? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: They can use the `?` operator to fail the test early if a fallible operation returns `Err`
options.B: They pass when they return `Ok(())` and fail when they return an `Err`
options.C: They can additionally be annotated with `#[should_panic]` to check panics
options.D: To assert that an operation returns `Err`, you should use `assert!(value.is_err())` rather than `?`
options.E: Returning an `Err` causes the test to be reported as ignored rather than failed

questions.explanation: `Result`-returning tests pass on `Ok(())`, fail on `Err`, and conveniently allow `?` in the body. They cannot be combined with `#[should_panic]`, and an `Err` is a failure, not an ignore; to check that something errors, assert `is_err()` instead of propagating with `?`.

questions.prompt: This test module fails to compile. What is the fix?

options.A: Add `#[derive(PartialEq, Debug)]` to `Point`, since `assert_eq!` needs to compare and print the values
options.B: Replace `assert_eq!` with `assert!`, because `assert_eq!` only works on primitive types
options.C: Mark `Point` and its fields `pub`, because tests cannot see private structs
options.D: Move the test out of the `#[cfg(test)]` module, which blocks trait resolution

questions.explanation: `assert_eq!` requires the compared values to implement `PartialEq` (for `==`) and `Debug` (to print `left`/`right` on failure). Both are derivable, so adding `#[derive(PartialEq, Debug)]` fixes the build. Visibility is not the issue: the child `tests` module can access private items.

questions.prompt: In the command `cargo test -- --test-threads=1`, what is the role of the bare `--`?

options.A: It marks the end of the command so the shell does not interpret the remaining flags
options.B: It separates arguments meant for `cargo test` itself from arguments passed to the compiled test binary
options.C: It tells Cargo to rebuild the tests from scratch before running them
options.D: It is required before every flag when running tests on Windows

questions.explanation: Arguments before `--` go to `cargo test`, and arguments after `--` go to the resulting test binary. That is why `cargo test --help` and `cargo test -- --help` show two different sets of options.

questions.prompt: These two tests each pass alone but fail intermittently when running `cargo test`. What is the root cause?

options.A: `fs::write` is asynchronous, so the file may not exist when `read_to_string` runs
options.B: Tests run in parallel by default, so both tests race on the same file; run them with `cargo test -- --test-threads=1` or use distinct files
options.C: `cargo test` runs each test twice, and the second run sees stale data
options.D: `assert_eq!` compares `String` to `&str`, which fails nondeterministically

questions.explanation: By default the test harness runs tests in parallel on multiple threads, so tests must not depend on shared mutable state such as a common file. Either make each test use its own file or serialize execution with `cargo test -- --test-threads=1`.

questions.prompt: Running `cargo test` (with no extra flags) on this code, which `println!` output appears in the terminal?

options.A: Both `I got the value 4` and `I got the value 8`
options.B: Only `I got the value 4`, from the passing test
options.C: Only `I got the value 8`, shown in the failing test's output
options.D: Neither line; `println!` never works inside tests

questions.explanation: The harness captures standard output of passing tests, so `I got the value 4` is swallowed, while output from the failing test is shown along with the failure message. To also see output from passing tests, run `cargo test -- --show-output`.

questions.prompt: A crate has tests named `add_two_and_two`, `add_three_and_two`, and `one_hundred`. What does `cargo test add` do?

options.A: Runs only a test named exactly `add`, and errors because none exists
options.B: Runs `add_two_and_two` and `add_three_and_two`; `one_hundred` is reported as filtered out
options.C: Runs all three tests, because the filter only affects display order
options.D: Runs nothing until you also pass `--filter` after `--`

questions.explanation: The first positional argument to `cargo test` is a filter: any test whose full name (including its module path) contains the substring runs. `one_hundred` doesn't match, so it is counted under `filtered out`; only one filter value can be passed this way.

questions.prompt: Which statements about `#[ignore]` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `cargo test` skips tests annotated with `#[ignore]` and lists them as ignored in the summary
options.B: `cargo test -- --ignored` runs only the ignored tests
options.C: `cargo test -- --include-ignored` runs all tests, whether ignored or not
options.D: `#[ignore]` excludes the test function from compilation entirely
options.E: Ignored tests are counted as failures in the final summary

questions.explanation: `#[ignore]` marks expensive tests to be skipped by default; they still compile and appear as `ignored` (not failed) in the summary. `-- --ignored` runs only those tests, and `-- --include-ignored` runs everything.

questions.prompt: Why are unit test modules in `src/` conventionally annotated with `#[cfg(test)]`?

options.A: It tells the compiler to build the module only when running `cargo test`, keeping test code out of `cargo build` artifacts
options.B: It grants the module access to private functions in the parent module
options.C: It is required for the `#[test]` attribute to be recognized at all
options.D: It makes the tests run in a separate process for isolation

questions.explanation: `#[cfg(test)]` is a conditional-compilation flag: the annotated module is compiled only for `cargo test`, which saves compile time and keeps test code and helpers out of the normal build. Private access comes from being a child module, not from `#[cfg(test)]`.

questions.prompt: A colleague claims that private functions in Rust can never be unit tested. Is that correct?

options.A: Yes; tests can only ever exercise a crate's public API
options.B: No; the `tests` module is a child of the module under test, so `use super::*;` brings private functions into scope for testing
options.C: No, but only if the private function is temporarily marked `pub(crate)` during testing
options.D: Yes, unless the function is annotated with `#[test_visible]`

questions.explanation: Rust's privacy rules let child modules use items from their ancestor modules. Because the unit test module lives in the same file as the code and typically starts with `use super::*;`, it can call private functions directly. Only integration tests in `tests/` are restricted to the public API.

questions.prompt: Which statements about integration tests in the `tests/` directory are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Each file directly under `tests/` is compiled as its own separate crate
options.B: Each test file must bring the library into scope, e.g. `use adder::add_two;`
options.C: Code in `tests/` must be wrapped in a `#[cfg(test)]` module to be compiled as tests
options.D: Integration tests can only call the library's public API, like any external user
options.E: Files in `tests/` are also compiled during a plain `cargo build`

questions.explanation: Cargo compiles every top-level file in `tests/` as an independent crate, so each one must `use` the library and can only reach its public API. No `#[cfg(test)]` is needed there because Cargo compiles the `tests/` directory only when you run `cargo test`, never during `cargo build`.

questions.prompt: A shared helper was placed in `tests/common.rs`. The tests compile and pass, but `cargo test` output now shows an unwanted section `Running tests/common.rs` with `running 0 tests`. What is the idiomatic fix?

options.A: Move the helper to `tests/common/mod.rs`; files in subdirectories of `tests/` are not compiled as separate test crates
options.B: Add `#[cfg(test)]` at the top of `tests/common.rs` so Cargo stops treating it as a test
options.C: Rename it to `tests/_common.rs`; a leading underscore hides files from the test runner
options.D: Add an empty `#[test]` function to `common.rs` so the section is no longer empty

questions.explanation: Every top-level file in `tests/` becomes its own integration test crate, so `common.rs` shows up as a `running 0 tests` section. The older naming convention `tests/common/mod.rs` avoids this: subdirectory files aren't compiled as separate crates, and test files still reach the helpers via `mod common;`.

questions.prompt: A project contains only `src/main.rs`. An integration test `tests/cli.rs` with `use myapp::run;` fails to compile because the crate cannot be found. Why, and what is the standard remedy?

options.A: Binary crates don't expose functions for other crates to `use`; move the logic into `src/lib.rs` and have `src/main.rs` call it, then test the library
options.B: The `tests/` directory only works for workspaces; add a `[workspace]` table to `Cargo.toml`
options.C: Integration tests must import binaries with `use main::run;` instead of the package name
options.D: Add `myapp` as a dev-dependency of itself in `Cargo.toml`

questions.explanation: Only library crates expose items that other crates can `use`; a binary crate's `src/main.rs` cannot be imported by integration tests. The standard structure is a thin `src/main.rs` that calls logic living in `src/lib.rs`, so integration tests can exercise the library with `use`, and the small remaining binary code doesn't need such tests.

title: An I/O Project: Building a Command Line Program

questions.prompt: What happens if a program iterates over `std::env::args()` and one of the command-line arguments contains invalid Unicode?

options.A: The invalid bytes are silently replaced with the Unicode replacement character.
options.B: The program panics as soon as the iterator reaches the invalid argument.
options.C: The iterator yields an `Err` value that can be handled with a `match`.
options.D: The invalid argument is skipped and iteration continues with the next one.

questions.explanation: `env::args()` panics if any argument contains invalid Unicode, because it yields `String` values. Programs that must accept arguments containing invalid Unicode should use `std::env::args_os()` instead, which yields platform-specific `OsString` values.

questions.prompt: This program is compiled to the binary `minigrep` and run with `cargo run -- needle haystack`. What does it print?

options.A: `needle`, because indexing starts at the first user-supplied argument
options.B: The path to the binary, such as `target/debug/minigrep`
options.C: `haystack`, because `collect` reverses the iterator
options.D: Nothing; the program panics because `args[0]` is out of bounds

questions.explanation: Like in C, the first element of the argument vector is the path used to invoke the program binary (e.g., `target/debug/minigrep`). The user's arguments start at `args[1]` (`needle`) and `args[2]` (`haystack`).

questions.prompt: This program is supposed to print the contents of `poem.txt`, but it does not work. What is wrong?

options.A: It compiles but prints the file path instead of the file contents.
options.B: It fails to compile: `read_to_string` returns `Result<String, io::Error>`, which does not implement `Display`; the code needs something like `.expect(...)` to get the `String` out.
options.C: It panics at runtime because `read_to_string` always requires an absolute path.
options.D: It compiles and runs, but prints an empty string because the `Result` is never awaited.

questions.explanation: `fs::read_to_string` returns a `Result<String, std::io::Error>`, not a `String`. `Result` does not implement `Display`, so `println!("{contents}")` is a compile error. The chapter first handles this with `.expect("Should have been able to read the file")`, and later propagates the error with `?` inside `run`.

questions.prompt: After the chapter's separation-of-concerns refactor splits the program into `main.rs` and `lib.rs`, which responsibilities remain in the `main` function? Select all that apply.

options.A: Calling the command-line parsing logic with the argument values
options.B: Calling a `run` function in `lib.rs` and handling the error if `run` returns one
options.C: Implementing the `search` logic that finds matching lines
options.D: Containing the unit tests for the program's core logic
options.E: Setting up any other configuration

questions.explanation: The book's guideline keeps `main` small: it calls the parsing logic, sets up any other configuration, calls `run` in `lib.rs`, and handles `run`'s error. All program logic like `search` moves to `lib.rs`, where it can be exercised by unit tests; `main` itself cannot be tested directly.

questions.prompt: Given this `main` and a `Config::build` that returns `Err("not enough arguments")` when `args.len() < 3`, what happens when the user runs `cargo run` with no extra arguments?

options.A: The program panics with `index out of bounds` and prints a backtrace.
options.B: It prints `Problem parsing arguments: not enough arguments` and exits with status code 1, without a panic backtrace.
options.C: It prints the message but continues running with a default `Config`.
options.D: It prints the message and exits with status code 0, signaling success.

questions.explanation: `Config::build` checks `args.len() < 3` and returns an `Err` instead of indexing out of bounds, so there is no panic. `unwrap_or_else` invokes the closure with the error value, which prints a friendly message and calls `process::exit(1)` — a nonzero exit code that signals failure to the calling shell.

questions.prompt: In `Config::build(&args).unwrap_or_else(|err| { ... })`, what does `unwrap_or_else` do when `Config::build` returns `Ok(config)`?

options.A: It calls the closure anyway, passing the `Config` as the `err` parameter.
options.B: It returns the inner `Config` value and never runs the closure.
options.C: It wraps the `Config` in another `Result` for later handling.
options.D: It clones the `Config` and passes the clone to the closure for validation.

questions.explanation: `unwrap_or_else` behaves like `unwrap` on the `Ok` variant: it returns the value inside `Ok`. Only when the value is an `Err` does it call the closure, passing the error value (here a `&'static str`) as the argument between the vertical pipes.

questions.prompt: Why does the chapter give `run` the return type `Result<(), Box<dyn Error>>` rather than a concrete error type like `std::io::Error`?

options.A: `Box<dyn Error>` is a trait object that lets `run` return values of any type that implements the `Error` trait, giving flexibility across different error cases.
options.B: `Box<dyn Error>` is required because the `?` operator only works with boxed errors.
options.C: Boxing the error stores it on the stack, which is faster than heap allocation.
options.D: Concrete error types cannot be returned from library functions in Rust.

questions.explanation: `Box<dyn Error>` is a trait object: it means the function returns some type implementing `Error` without specifying which one. This lets `run` propagate error values of different concrete types from different failure cases. The `?` operator works with any error type that can convert into the return's error type; boxing is not a requirement of `?` itself.

questions.prompt: This refactored `run` function does not compile. What is the problem?

options.A: `fs::read_to_string` cannot be used inside a function that takes ownership of `Config`.
options.B: The `?` operator can only be used in a function that returns `Result` (or `Option`), but `run` returns `()`; it should return `Result<(), Box<dyn Error>>` and end with `Ok(())`.
options.C: `config.file_path` must be borrowed with `&` before being passed to `read_to_string`.
options.D: `println!` cannot appear after a `?` expression in the same function body.

questions.explanation: `?` propagates an `Err` by returning it from the enclosing function, so that function's return type must be compatible — e.g., `Result<(), Box<dyn Error>>`. With a `()` return type, the compiler rejects the `?`. The fix is to change the signature and return `Ok(())` in the success case.

questions.prompt: A learner asks: "Why does `main` use `if let Err(e) = run(config)` instead of `unwrap_or_else`, like it does for `Config::build`?" What is the book's reasoning?

options.A: `unwrap_or_else` cannot be used on a `Result` whose error type is `Box<dyn Error>`.
options.B: `run` returns `()` in the success case, so there is no value to unwrap; the code only needs to detect an error, print it, and exit.
options.C: `if let` is faster than `unwrap_or_else` because it avoids a closure allocation.
options.D: `unwrap_or_else` would swallow the error message instead of printing it.

questions.explanation: `Config::build` produces a `Config` value we need on success, so `unwrap_or_else` extracts it. `run` returns `Ok(())` on success — nothing worth unwrapping — so `if let Err(e) = run(config)` expresses the intent exactly: act only when there is an error, print it, and `process::exit(1)`.

questions.prompt: The chapter develops `search` using test-driven development (TDD). Which sequence matches the process it follows?

options.A: Write the full implementation, then add tests to document its behavior, then optimize.
options.B: Write a test that fails, write just enough code to make it pass, then refactor while keeping tests passing.
options.C: Write tests and implementation simultaneously in the same commit, then delete tests that fail.
options.D: Benchmark a prototype first, then write tests only for the slow paths.

questions.explanation: The TDD flow in the book is: write a failing test and confirm it fails for the expected reason, write or modify just enough code to make it pass, then refactor while ensuring the tests continue to pass — and repeat. The `one_result` test is written before `search` has a real body.

questions.prompt: This first attempt at `search` fails to compile. Why?

options.A: `contents.lines()` consumes `contents`, so it cannot be borrowed again by `results`.
options.B: The compiler reports a missing lifetime specifier (E0106): with two `&str` parameters it cannot tell whether the returned slices borrow from `query` or `contents`; the signature needs `<'a>` tying the return to `contents`.
options.C: `Vec::new()` requires a type annotation such as `Vec::<&str>::new()` in function bodies.
options.D: `line.contains(query)` needs `&query` because `contains` takes its pattern by reference.

questions.explanation: The returned `Vec<&str>` holds slices borrowed from one of the inputs, but with two reference parameters the compiler cannot infer which. Rust rejects the signature with error E0106, "missing lifetime specifier." The fix is `pub fn search<'a>(query: &str, contents: &'a str) -> Vec<&'a str>`, declaring that the returned slices live as long as `contents`.

questions.prompt: Using the book's final `search` implementation (case-sensitive, matching lines that contain the query), what does this print?

options.A: `[]` — no line is exactly equal to `"duct"`
options.B: `["safe, fast, productive."]` — `"productive"` contains the substring `"duct"`
options.C: `["duct"]` — search returns the query for each match
options.D: `["Rust:", "safe, fast, productive.", "Pick three."]` — every line matches

questions.explanation: `search` iterates over `contents.lines()` and pushes each line for which `line.contains(query)` is true. Only `safe, fast, productive.` contains `duct` (inside the word `productive`), so the result is `["safe, fast, productive."]` — exactly what the chapter's `one_result` test asserts.

questions.prompt: A colleague looks at `pub fn search<'a>(query: &str, contents: &'a str) -> Vec<&'a str>` and claims: "The lifetime `'a` means `query` and `contents` must live equally long." Which statement is correct?

options.A: The colleague is right: all reference parameters in a generic function share one lifetime.
options.B: The colleague is wrong: `'a` connects only the returned slices to `contents`, telling the compiler the returned data lives as long as `contents`; `query` has its own independent (elided) lifetime and may be shorter-lived.
options.C: The colleague is wrong, but for a different reason: `'a` applies to `query`, not `contents`, because `query` appears first.
