options.D: The colleague is right in release builds only; in debug builds lifetimes are not checked.

questions.explanation: The returned `Vec` holds string slices that point into `contents`, so `'a` ties the return value's lifetime to `contents` alone. `query` is annotated with a separate elided lifetime and is unrelated to the return value. Lifetimes are a compile-time check in all build profiles.

questions.prompt: `Config::build` sets `let ignore_case = env::var("IGNORE_CASE").is_ok();`. A user runs the program with the environment variable set to `IGNORE_CASE=0`, intending to disable case-insensitive search. What actually happens?

options.A: The search is case-sensitive, because `"0"` is treated as false.
options.B: The search is case-insensitive, because `is_ok()` only checks whether the variable is set at all — its value (even `"0"` or `"false"`) is ignored.
options.C: `Config::build` returns an `Err` because `"0"` is not a valid boolean.
options.D: The program panics, because `env::var` cannot parse `"0"` into a `bool`.

questions.explanation: `env::var` returns `Ok(value)` whenever the variable is set and `Err` when it is unset. `is_ok()` therefore treats the variable as a presence flag: any value, including `0` or `false`, turns case-insensitive search on. The book notes that a real program might want to inspect the value instead.

questions.prompt: Using the book's `search_case_insensitive` (which lowercases the query and each line for comparison), what does this print?

options.A: `["rust:", "trust me."]` — matching lines are returned lowercased
options.B: `["Rust:"]` — only lines starting with the query match
options.C: `["Rust:", "Trust me."]` — comparison is done on lowercased copies, but the original lines are returned with their casing intact
options.D: `["Rust:", "Trust me.", "Duct tape."]` — every line contains at least one of the query's letters

questions.explanation: The function shadows `query` with `query.to_lowercase()` (yielding `"rust"`) and tests `line.to_lowercase().contains(&query)`. `"rust:"` and `"trust me."` both contain `"rust"`; `"duct tape."` does not. Because it pushes the original `line` slice, not the lowercased `String`, the output preserves original casing: `["Rust:", "Trust me."]`.

questions.section: 12.6 Writing Error Messages to Standard Error Instead of Standard Output
questions.prompt: After the final refactor, error messages are printed with `eprintln!` and `main` calls `process::exit(1)` on failure. The user runs `cargo run > output.txt` with no arguments, triggering the `not enough arguments` error. Which statements are true? Select all that apply.

options.A: The error message appears on the terminal screen.
options.B: `output.txt` is created but contains no error text, because `>` redirects only standard output.
options.C: The error message is written into `output.txt`.
options.D: `>` redirects both stdout and stderr, so nothing appears on the terminal.
options.E: The process exits with a nonzero status code.

questions.explanation: `eprintln!` writes to standard error, and the shell's `>` operator redirects only standard output. So the error stays visible on the terminal while `output.txt` ends up empty — exactly the behavior the chapter wants, so successful output can be piped to a file without losing error messages. `process::exit(1)` makes the process exit with status 1. Capturing stderr would require `2>` or `2>&1`.

title: Functional Language Features: Iterators and Closures

questions.prompt: What is the key capability that closures have but plain `fn` functions do not?

options.A: Closures can be called recursively, while functions cannot
options.B: Closures can capture values from the scope in which they are defined
options.C: Closures can return multiple values, while functions return only one
options.D: Closures run asynchronously by default

questions.explanation: Closures are anonymous functions that can capture values from their enclosing environment (by reference, mutable reference, or by value). Plain `fn` items cannot capture their environment. Recursion, return values, and async behavior are unrelated distinctions.

questions.prompt: This code fails to compile. Why?

options.A: Closures without type annotations can never be called
options.B: The first call locked the closure's parameter type to `String`, so calling it with an integer is a type mismatch
options.C: `example_closure` was moved by the first call, so it cannot be called a second time
options.D: A closure body consisting of just `x` is not a valid expression

questions.explanation: Closure parameter and return types are inferred once, from the first use. After `example_closure(String::from("hello"))`, the closure's type is locked to `String -> String`, so `example_closure(5)` produces `expected String, found integer`. The closure implements `Fn`, so calling it twice is fine; the problem is purely the type lock-in.

questions.prompt: How does the closure `only_borrows` capture `list`?

options.A: By immutable borrow, because the body only reads `list`
options.B: By mutable borrow, because closures always capture mutably
options.C: By value, because `Vec` is not `Copy`
options.D: It does not capture `list`; `println!` reads it directly from the outer scope

questions.explanation: The compiler infers the least restrictive capture mode the body needs: printing only requires reading, so `list` is captured by immutable borrow (`&Vec<i32>`). Ownership is only taken if the body requires it or `move` is used.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.D: It does not compile: `list` is still mutably borrowed at the `println!`

questions.explanation: The closure captures `list` by mutable borrow (its body calls `push`), so the binding must be `mut`. Each call pushes 7, giving `[1, 2, 3, 7, 7]`. The mutable borrow ends after the closure's last use, so the later `println!` is allowed by non-lexical lifetimes.

questions.prompt: Why is the `move` keyword required in this code?

options.A: `println!` always requires ownership of its arguments
options.B: The new thread might outlive `main`'s stack frame, so borrowing `list` would risk a dangling reference; `move` transfers ownership into the closure
options.C: `move` is required for every closure passed to another function
options.D: `Vec` cannot be borrowed across function boundaries

questions.explanation: Without `move`, the closure would capture `list` by immutable borrow, but the compiler cannot prove the spawned thread finishes before `list` is dropped. `move` forces the closure to take ownership so the data lives as long as the thread. The body itself only needs a read, which is why `move` must be explicit here.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about the `Fn`, `FnMut`, and `FnOnce` traits are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Every closure implements at least `FnOnce`
options.B: A closure that mutates a captured variable but does not move anything out of its body implements `FnMut` (and `FnOnce`)
options.C: A closure that only reads captured values (or captures nothing) implements `Fn`, `FnMut`, and `FnOnce`
options.D: Adding the `move` keyword means the closure can only implement `FnOnce`
options.E: Plain functions defined with `fn` can also be used where an `Fn` trait bound is required

questions.explanation: The traits are additive: all closures implement `FnOnce`; those that don't move captured values out also implement `FnMut`; those that additionally don't mutate implement `Fn`. D is false: `move` changes how values are captured (by value), not what the body does with them, so a `move` closure that only reads still implements `Fn`. E is true: `fn` items implement all three traits.

questions.prompt: The standard library defines `Option::unwrap_or_else` with the bound `F: FnOnce() -> T`. Why is `FnOnce` the right choice?

options.A: `FnOnce` closures are faster to call than `Fn` closures
options.B: `unwrap_or_else` calls the closure at most once, so requiring only `FnOnce` accepts the widest range of closures, including ones that move captured values out
options.C: `FnOnce` is required because the closure takes no arguments
options.D: Only `FnOnce` closures can return a value of type `T`

questions.explanation: `unwrap_or_else` invokes the closure at most once (only in the `None` case), so `FnOnce` is sufficient. Because `Fn` and `FnMut` closures also implement `FnOnce`, this bound is the most permissive: callers may even pass closures that consume captured values. The trait choice has nothing to do with speed, argument count, or return types.

questions.prompt: This code fails to compile. What is the actual problem?

options.A: The closure moves `value` out of its environment, so it implements only `FnOnce`, but `sort_by_key` requires `FnMut` because it calls the closure many times
options.B: `sort_by_key` requires `Fn`, and pushing to `sort_operations` mutates captured state, which is forbidden
options.C: `String` does not implement `Ord`, so it cannot be used inside a sort
options.D: Arrays cannot be sorted with `sort_by_key`; only `Vec` can

questions.explanation: `push(value)` moves the `String` out of the closure's environment, which can only happen once, so the closure implements only `FnOnce`. `sort_by_key` needs `FnMut` because it invokes the key closure once per comparison. B is wrong: `FnMut` explicitly allows mutating captured state (pushing to `sort_operations` alone would be fine, e.g. `sort_operations.push(value.clone())`). Slices/arrays do support `sort_by_key`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: It does not compile: `list` is borrowed by the closure and cannot be used by the first `println!`
options.B:
From closure: [1, 2, 3]
Before calling closure: [1, 2, 3]
options.C:
Before calling closure: [1, 2, 3]
From closure: [1, 2, 3]
options.D: Before calling closure: [1, 2, 3] only; defining a closure that is never stored does nothing

questions.explanation: Defining a closure does not run its body; it runs when called. The closure holds only an immutable borrow of `list`, and multiple immutable borrows may coexist, so reading `list` in the first `println!` is fine. Output order follows execution: the "Before" line, then the closure's line.

questions.prompt: A colleague claims: "If you write `move` in front of a closure, it becomes `FnOnce`-only, so you can't call it twice." Which statement is correct?

options.A: The colleague is right: `move` closures always implement only `FnOnce`
options.B: The colleague is wrong: which `Fn` traits a closure implements depends on what its body does with the captured values, not on how they were captured; a `move` closure that only reads its captures still implements `Fn` and can be called repeatedly
options.C: The colleague is wrong, but for another reason: `move` closures implement no `Fn` traits at all
options.D: The colleague is right only for closures that capture `Copy` types

questions.explanation: `move` controls capture (take ownership instead of borrowing); the trait a closure implements is determined by how the body uses the captures. `move || println!("{list:?}")` owns `list` but only reads it, so it implements `Fn` (and `FnMut`, `FnOnce`) and is freely callable multiple times. Only moving a captured value out of the body restricts a closure to `FnOnce`.

questions.prompt: What is the result of compiling and running this program?

options.A: It prints `4`
options.B: It prints `rust`
options.C: It does not compile: `s` is used after being moved into the closure
options.D: It does not compile: a closure body cannot move a captured value into a local binding

questions.explanation: The closure takes ownership of `s` and its body moves `s` into `owned`, so `consume` implements only `FnOnce`. Calling it exactly once is fine and returns `owned.len()`, which is 4. It would fail to compile only if `consume()` were called a second time (or if `s` were used in `main` after the closure definition).

questions.section: 13.2 Processing a Series of Items with Iterators
questions.prompt: This code compiles with a warning and has no visible effect. What went wrong?
questions.code:
let v1 = vec![1, 2, 3];

v1.iter().map(|x| x + 1);

options.A: `map` mutated `v1` in place, so the result was silently discarded
options.B: Iterator adapters are lazy: `map` just builds a new iterator and the closure never runs until a consuming method like `collect` is called
options.C: The closure should take `&x` instead of `x`
options.D: `iter()` returns owned values, so `x + 1` is a type error

questions.explanation: Adapters such as `map` are lazy; they return a new iterator and do nothing until it is consumed. The compiler warns `unused Map that must be used: iterators are lazy and do nothing unless consumed`. The fix is to consume it, e.g. `let v2: Vec<_> = v1.iter().map(|x| x + 1).collect();`.

questions.section: 13.2 Processing a Series of Items with Iterators
questions.prompt: For a `Vec<String>` named `v`, which method creates an iterator that yields owned `String` values, consuming the vector?

options.A: `v.iter()`
options.B: `v.iter_mut()`
options.C: `v.into_iter()`
options.D: `v.drain_owned()`

questions.explanation: `iter()` yields immutable references (`&String`), `iter_mut()` yields mutable references (`&mut String`), and `into_iter()` takes ownership of the collection and yields owned `String` values. `drain_owned` does not exist.

questions.section: 13.2 Processing a Series of Items with Iterators
questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A:
Some(1)
Some(2)
Some(3)
options.B:
Some(2)
Some(3)
None
options.C:
Some(&2)
Some(&3)
None

questions.explanation: Each `next()` call advances the iterator's internal state. The first (unprinted) call consumes `Some(&1)`, so the printed values are `Some(2)`, `Some(3)`, and `None`. Debug formatting of `Some(&2)` prints as `Some(2)` because references format transparently.

questions.section: 13.2 Processing a Series of Items with Iterators
questions.prompt: This code fails to compile. Why?

options.A: `next()` takes `&mut self` because it updates the iterator's internal state, so `iter` must be declared `mut`
options.B: `next()` returns `1`, not `Some(&1)`, so the assertion has a type mismatch
options.C: `v.iter()` cannot be stored in a variable; it must be consumed immediately
options.D: `assert_eq!` cannot compare `Option` values

questions.explanation: Calling `next()` mutates the iterator (it tracks where it is in the sequence), so its signature is `fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>`. The fix is `let mut iter = v.iter();`. Note that a `for` loop does not need `mut` because the loop takes ownership of the iterator and makes it mutable internally.

questions.section: 13.2 Processing a Series of Items with Iterators
questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.D: It does not compile: the `filter` closure cannot capture `threshold` from the surrounding scope

questions.explanation: Closures passed to adapters can freely capture their environment: `filter` captures `threshold` by immutable borrow. `into_iter` yields owned `i32` values; `filter` receives `&i32` (hence `*x`), keeping 3 and 4; `map` multiplies by 10; `collect` materializes `[30, 40]`.

questions.section: 13.2 Processing a Series of Items with Iterators
questions.prompt: Which of these iterator methods are consuming adapters, i.e. they call `next` repeatedly and use up the iterator? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `sum`
options.B: `map`
options.C: `collect`
options.D: `filter`
options.E: `count`

questions.explanation: `sum`, `collect`, and `count` take ownership of the iterator and drive it to completion, producing a final value; the iterator cannot be used afterward. `map` and `filter` are iterator adapters: they lazily wrap the iterator in a new one and do no work until something consumes it.

questions.section: 13.2 Processing a Series of Items with Iterators
questions.prompt: To implement the `Iterator` trait for your own type, what is the minimum you must provide?

options.A: The associated type `Item` and the `next` method; every other method has a default implementation
options.B: `next`, `size_hint`, and `collect`
options.C: Only `into_iter`, which the compiler uses to derive `next`
options.D: All adapter methods you intend to use, such as `map` and `filter`

questions.explanation: The trait is `pub trait Iterator { type Item; fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item>; ... }`. You define `Item` and `next`; the dozens of other methods (`map`, `zip`, `sum`, etc.) come for free as default implementations built on `next`.

questions.section: 13.2 Processing a Series of Items with Iterators
questions.prompt: What does this program print?
questions.explanation: `zip` pairs each element with its successor and stops when either side is exhausted: (1,2), (2,3), (3,4), (4,5). Products are 2, 6, 12, 20. `filter` keeps multiples of 3: 6 and 12. Their sum is 18. This mirrors the book's `Counter` example combining `zip`, `skip`, `map`, `filter`, and `sum`.

questions.section: 13.2 Processing a Series of Items with Iterators
questions.prompt: Calling `next()` directly requires a mutable iterator, yet this compiles even though `v1_iter` is not declared `mut`. Why?

options.A: `for` loops use a special immutable form of `next` that does not change iterator state
options.B: The `for` loop takes ownership of `v1_iter` and makes it mutable behind the scenes, so no `mut` is needed on the original binding
options.C: `v1.iter()` returns an iterator that is always implicitly mutable
options.D: The compiler silently clones the iterator on each loop pass

questions.explanation: A `for` loop desugars to code that moves the iterator into a new binding it controls and declares that binding mutable, then calls `next` until `None`. Ownership transfer is also why `v1_iter` cannot be used after the loop. There is no immutable `next` and no per-iteration cloning.

questions.prompt: In the minigrep project, `Config::build` was changed from taking `args: &[String]` to `mut args: impl Iterator<Item = String>`. What is the main benefit?

options.A: It makes argument parsing case-insensitive
options.B: Owning the iterator lets `build` move each `String` out with `args.next()`, eliminating the `clone()` calls (and their allocations) needed with the slice version
options.C: Iterators can be indexed faster than slices
options.D: It allows `Config` to store references into the original arguments

questions.explanation: With a borrowed slice, `Config` could not take ownership of the `String`s, forcing `args[1].clone()` and `args[2].clone()`. By taking any `Iterator<Item = String>` by value (e.g. `env::args()` passed directly), `build` calls `next()` to move the values into `Config` — no clones. The first `next()` call skips the program name.

questions.prompt:
The `search` function was rewritten from a `for` loop pushing into a `Vec` to:
`contents.lines().filter(|line| line.contains(query)).collect()`. Which statements about this version are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: It removes the mutable intermediate `results` vector
options.B: The `filter` closure captures `query` from the surrounding environment
options.C: It is significantly slower because each adapter adds a function-call layer at runtime
options.D: It returns the same matching lines as the loop version
options.E: `filter` scans all of `contents` eagerly the moment it is called, before `collect` runs

questions.explanation: The adapter chain expresses the filtering directly, with no mutable state (A) and the same behavior as the loop (D); the closure borrows `query` from the enclosing function (B). C is false: the book's benchmark showed the two versions perform about the same, since adapters compile down to equivalent code. E is false: `filter` is lazy and no lines are examined until `collect` consumes the iterator.

questions.prompt: The book calls iterators one of Rust's "zero-cost abstractions". What does that mean?

options.A: Using the abstraction imposes no additional runtime overhead compared to writing the lower-level code by hand
options.B: Iterators never allocate memory under any circumstances
options.C: Iterator code compiles instantly, costing zero compile time
options.D: Iterators are free to use only in release builds

questions.explanation: Zero-cost abstraction, echoing Stroustrup's "What you do use, you couldn't hand code any better", means the high-level iterator code compiles down to roughly the same machine code as an equivalent hand-written loop. It says nothing about allocation in general, compile times, or build profiles.

questions.prompt: Which statements about iterator performance from this section are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: In the book's `search` benchmark over the text of The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, the iterator version performed about the same as (even slightly faster than) the explicit loop
options.B: The compiler can unroll loops originating from iterator chains: in the audio decoder example, the loop over 12 coefficients compiled to repeated instructions with no loop at all
options.C: Because coefficient values ended up in registers and bounds checks were removed, accessing the array in that example was extremely fast
options.D: Closures passed to adapters are dispatched through vtables at runtime, adding indirect-call overhead by default
options.E: For hot code paths, you should routinely rewrite iterator chains as manual index-based loops

questions.explanation: The benchmark timings were nearly identical (about 19.2M ns/iter for the iterator vs 19.6M for the loop), and the audio decoder example showed full unrolling, registers holding coefficients, and eliminated bounds checks. D is false: adapters and closures are monomorphized and inlined, not dynamically dispatched by default. E is false: the book's advice is to prefer the clearer iterator style, since it compiles to comparable code.

questions.prompt: What are the default `opt-level` values for the `dev` and `release` profiles?

options.A: `dev` is 0 and `release` is 3
options.B: `dev` is 1 and `release` is 2
options.C: Both default to 0; you must opt in to optimizations explicitly
options.D: `dev` is 3 and `release` is 0

questions.explanation: The `dev` profile defaults to `opt-level = 0` (fast compiles, few optimizations) and the `release` profile defaults to `opt-level = 3` (slower compiles, faster runtime). `opt-level` ranges from 0 to 3.

questions.prompt: A developer wants release builds to use `opt-level = 2` and adds this to `Cargo.toml`, but release builds still use `opt-level = 3`. What is wrong?
questions.code:
[profiles.release]
opt-level = 2

options.A: The table name is wrong: profile customization must go under `[profile.release]`, not `[profiles.release]`
options.B: `opt-level` cannot be lowered below the profile default of 3
options.C: Profile settings belong in `Cargo.lock`, not `Cargo.toml`
options.D: The setting must be written as `opt_level = 2` with an underscore

questions.explanation: Cargo profiles are customized with `[profile.*]` sections (singular `profile`), e.g. `[profile.release]`. A `[profiles.release]` table is not recognized, so the default `opt-level = 3` remains in effect. Any subset of a profile's defaults can be overridden.

questions.prompt: With no `[profile.*]` sections in `Cargo.toml`, which profile does `cargo build` use, and which does `cargo build --release` use?

options.A: `cargo build` uses the `debug` profile; `--release` uses the `optimized` profile
options.B: `cargo build` uses the `dev` profile; `--release` uses the `release` profile — each with its own defaults
options.C: Both use the `dev` profile; `--release` only strips debug symbols
options.D: `cargo build` fails unless a profile is declared in `Cargo.toml`

questions.explanation: Cargo has two main built-in profiles with sensible defaults: `dev`, used by plain `cargo build` (shown as `Finished dev [unoptimized + debuginfo]`), and `release`, used by `cargo build --release` (shown as `Finished release [optimized]`). No configuration is required to use them.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between `///` and `//!` documentation comments?

options.A: `///` documents the item that follows it; `//!` documents the enclosing item, such as the crate root in `src/lib.rs`
options.B: `///` is for public items and `//!` is for private items
options.C: `//!` comments support Markdown, while `///` comments are plain text only
options.D: They are interchangeable; `//!` is simply the older syntax

questions.explanation: `///` doc comments describe the item immediately following them, while `//!` describes the item that contains the comment — typically used at the top of `src/lib.rs` to document the whole crate. Both support Markdown, and `cargo doc --open` builds and opens the generated HTML.

questions.prompt: This is the entire `src/lib.rs` of crate `my_crate`. What happens when you run `cargo test`?

options.A: All tests pass; code in doc comments is rendered but never executed
options.B: The doc-test compiles and runs, then fails because `assert_eq!(7, answer)` panics — 6 is not equal to 7
options.C: `cargo test` reports 0 tests since there is no `#[test]` function
options.D: Compilation of the doc-test fails because `my_crate` cannot reference itself

questions.explanation: Code blocks inside doc comments are run as documentation tests by `cargo test` (in a `Doc-tests my_crate` section). Here `add_one(5)` returns 6, so `assert_eq!(7, answer)` panics and the doc-test fails. This mechanism keeps examples in docs in sync with the actual code.

questions.prompt: The crate `art` defines `PrimaryColor` in module `kinds` and `mix` in module `utils`. What does adding these lines to the crate root accomplish?
questions.code:
pub use self::kinds::PrimaryColor;
pub use self::utils::mix;

options.A: It moves the items out of their modules, so `art::kinds::PrimaryColor` no longer compiles
options.B: It re-exports the items at the crate root, so users can write `use art::PrimaryColor;` without knowing the internal module structure
options.C: It makes the items visible only inside the crate; external users still need the full paths
options.D: It creates copies of the items, doubling the compiled crate size

questions.explanation: `pub use` re-exports an item at a new location without changing the internal organization. Users can then write `use art::PrimaryColor;` and `use art::mix;` instead of the deeper paths, and the re-exports are listed on the crate's docs front page. The original paths remain valid.

questions.prompt: Running `cargo publish` with this `Cargo.toml` fails with a 400 error from crates.io. Why?
questions.code:
[package]
name = "guessing_game"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"
description = "A fun game where you guess what number the computer has chosen."

options.A: The `edition` field must be "2021" for crates published to crates.io
options.B: The `description` field is not allowed in the `[package]` table
options.C: The manifest is missing a `license` field (an SPDX identifier such as `MIT OR Apache-2.0`) or a `license-file`
options.D: The version must start at "1.0.0" for a first publish

questions.explanation: Crates.io requires a unique `name`, a `version`, a `description`, and a `license` (an SPDX identifier like `MIT OR Apache-2.0`, or `license-file` pointing to custom license text). This manifest has everything except the license, so `cargo publish` is rejected with an error mentioning the missing metadata.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about publishing and yanking on crates.io are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: A publish is permanent: a published version can never be overwritten, and its code cannot be deleted
options.B: `cargo yank --vers 1.0.1` prevents new projects from depending on that version, but projects with it in `Cargo.lock` keep working
options.C: Yanking a version deletes its code from crates.io, so it is a safe way to remove accidentally uploaded secrets
options.D: To release a fix, you change `version` in `Cargo.toml` following Semantic Versioning rules and run `cargo publish` again
options.E: `cargo publish` succeeds even without a crates.io account, as long as the metadata is complete

questions.explanation: Publishing is permanent — versions cannot be overwritten or deleted (A). Yanking only stops new dependents; existing `Cargo.lock` files still resolve, and a yank can be undone with `--undo` (B). New releases require a semver version bump and another `cargo publish` (D). C is the classic misconception: yank does NOT delete code, so leaked secrets must be revoked. E is false — you need an account and an API token saved via `cargo login`.

questions.prompt: In a Cargo workspace containing crates `adder` and `add_one`, where do the `Cargo.lock` file and compiled artifacts live?

options.A: Each member crate has its own `Cargo.lock` and its own `target` directory
options.B: There is one `Cargo.lock` and one shared `target` directory at the workspace root
options.C: One shared `target` directory, but each member keeps its own `Cargo.lock`
options.D: One `Cargo.lock` at the root, but each member compiles into its own `target` directory

questions.explanation: A workspace has a single `Cargo.lock` at the top level, guaranteeing all members use the same versions of shared dependencies, and one shared `target` directory at the root so crates and dependencies are not recompiled redundantly for each member.

questions.prompt: In this workspace, `adder/src/main.rs` calls `add_one::add_one(10)` but the build fails with `can't find crate for add_one`. The workspace `Cargo.toml` lists `members = ["adder", "add_one"]`. What is missing?
questions.code:
# adder/Cargo.toml
[package]
name = "adder"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"

[dependencies]

options.A: Workspace membership does not create dependencies; `adder` needs `add_one = { path = "../add_one" }` under `[dependencies]`
options.B: `add_one` must be listed before `adder` in the `members` array so it builds first
options.C: The workspace `Cargo.toml` needs a `[dependencies]` section linking the two members
options.D: `adder` must declare `add_one` with a version from crates.io; path dependencies are not allowed inside workspaces

questions.explanation: Cargo does not assume crates in a workspace depend on each other. Each dependency between members must be declared explicitly as a path dependency, e.g. `add_one = { path = "../add_one" }` in `adder/Cargo.toml`. Member order in the `members` array is irrelevant.

questions.prompt: From the root of a workspace with several members, how do you run only the tests of the `add_one` crate?

options.A: `cargo test add_one` — the first positional argument selects the crate
options.B: `cargo test -p add_one` — the `-p` flag selects a specific package
options.C: `cd add_one && cargo test` is the only way; workspace roots cannot filter tests by crate
options.D: `cargo test --bin add_one`

questions.explanation: Running `cargo test` at the workspace root runs tests for all members. To test just one, use the `-p`/`--package` flag: `cargo test -p add_one`. (A positional argument to `cargo test` filters by test name, not by package, and `--bin` selects a binary target.) The same flag works for `cargo run -p adder`.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about Cargo workspaces are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: If one member depends on the external crate `rand`, every other member can `use rand` without declaring it, because dependencies are shared automatically
options.B: Each member must declare `rand` in its own `Cargo.toml` to use it, but the single root `Cargo.lock` ensures compatible members all get the same version
options.C: Crates in a workspace must be published to crates.io together with a single `cargo publish` at the root
options.D: Each workspace member is published separately; you can pick one with `cargo publish -p <crate>`
options.E: The top-level workspace `Cargo.toml` starts with a `[workspace]` section and has no `[package]` section of its own

questions.explanation: The shared root `Cargo.lock` keeps external dependency versions consistent, but each member must still list the dependency in its own `Cargo.toml` (B true, A false). Crates are published to crates.io individually — there is no whole-workspace publish — and `-p` selects which one (D true, C false). The root manifest is a virtual manifest: a `[workspace]` section with `members` (and e.g. `resolver = "3"`) but no `[package]` (E true).

questions.prompt: A colleague runs `cargo install serde` to add the `serde` library to their project. What is the misunderstanding?

options.A: `cargo install` only works for crates with binary targets and puts executables in `$HOME/.cargo/bin`; library dependencies go in `Cargo.toml` instead
options.B: Nothing — `cargo install` is the standard way to add any dependency to a project
options.C: The command should be `cargo install --lib serde` to install a library crate
options.D: `cargo install` works, but it installs libraries system-wide, which requires administrator rights

questions.explanation: `cargo install` is for installing binary crates (runnable tools like `ripgrep`, whose executable `rg` ends up in `$HOME/.cargo/bin`, which should be on your `$PATH`). It cannot install library-only crates, and it is not how you add dependencies — those are declared in the project's `Cargo.toml`.

questions.prompt: You install a tool whose executable is named `cargo-something`. How can it be used to extend Cargo?

options.A: It must be registered in `Cargo.toml` under `[commands]` before Cargo will recognize it
options.B: If a binary named `cargo-something` is on your `$PATH`, you can run it as the subcommand `cargo something`, and it appears in `cargo --list`
options.C: It can only be invoked directly as `cargo-something`; Cargo never dispatches to external binaries
options.D: It works as a subcommand only if it was compiled as part of the Cargo source tree

questions.explanation: Cargo is designed to be extended without modification: any binary on `$PATH` named `cargo-something` can be run as if it were a built-in subcommand via `cargo something`. Such custom commands are listed by `cargo --list`, and `cargo install` is a convenient way to get them.

questions.section: 15.1 Using Box<T> to Point to Data on the Heap
questions.prompt: Why does the definition `enum List { Cons(i32, List), Nil }` fail to compile, and how does changing it to `Cons(i32, Box<List>)` fix the problem?

options.A: Enums cannot contain other enum values at all; `Box` converts the inner value into a trait object, which enums are allowed to hold.
options.B: The compiler forbids recursion in type names; `Box` renames the inner type so the recursion is hidden.
options.C: The type would need infinite size because each `Cons` directly contains another `List`; `Box<List>` stores the next element on the heap, so the variant only holds a pointer of known, fixed size.
options.D: The original version compiles but overflows the stack at runtime; `Box` merely moves the overflow to the heap.

questions.explanation: Rust must know a type's size at compile time. A `Cons` that directly contains a `List` makes the size calculation recurse forever ("recursive type has infinite size"). `Box<T>` is a pointer with a fixed size, so `Cons(i32, Box<List>)` breaks the infinite chain: the variant stores an `i32` plus one pointer, and the rest of the list lives on the heap.

questions.section: 15.1 Using Box<T> to Point to Data on the Heap
questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.C: It fails to compile: a `Box<i32>` cannot be dereferenced with `*`.
options.D: It panics at runtime when dereferencing the box.

questions.explanation: `Box<T>` implements `Deref`, so `*b` yields the `i32` value `5` stored on the heap, and `*b + 1` is `6`. Dereferencing a `Box` is safe and always valid while the box exists — no panic is possible here.

questions.section: 15.1 Using Box<T> to Point to Data on the Heap
questions.prompt: A colleague says: "You should wrap values in `Box<T>` whenever possible because heap allocation makes access faster." Which response is accurate?

options.A: Correct — heap data is cached more aggressively by the CPU, so `Box` speeds up reads.
options.B: Incorrect — `Box` is faster only for integers; for structs it is slower.
options.C: Correct, but only in release builds where the allocator is optimized.
options.D: Incorrect — `Box` adds an allocation and a level of indirection with no access-speed benefit. Its real uses are: types whose size is unknown at compile time (e.g., recursive types), transferring ownership of large data without copying it, and owning trait objects.

questions.explanation: Boxes provide indirection and heap allocation, nothing more — dereferencing a pointer is never faster than direct access. The book lists the three motivating situations: unknown-size types (recursive types like the cons list), avoiding copies of large data when transferring ownership (only the pointer is copied), and owning a value that only needs to implement a trait.

questions.section: 15.1 Using Box<T> to Point to Data on the Heap
questions.prompt: What is wrong with this code?

options.A: Nothing — the list is finite (two elements), so it compiles and runs.
options.B: It fails to compile with "recursive type `List` has infinite size"; the fix is to add indirection, e.g. `Cons(i32, Box<List>)`.
options.C: It compiles but overflows the stack at runtime because each `Cons` copies the whole tail.
options.D: It fails to compile because enum variants may hold at most one field.

questions.explanation: The error is about the type definition, not any particular value: to lay out `List`, the compiler would need the size of `List` itself, recursively, which is infinite. The compiler explicitly suggests inserting indirection such as `Box`, `Rc`, or `&` to break the cycle. That a specific list is short does not help — sizes are computed per type at compile time.

questions.section: 15.1 Using Box<T> to Point to Data on the Heap
questions.prompt: Which statements about `Box<T>` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `Box::new(v)` moves `v` onto the heap; the box itself is a fixed-size pointer stored on the stack.
options.B: When a `Box<T>` goes out of scope, its `Drop` implementation frees the heap data as well as the pointer.
options.C: `Box<T>` implements `Deref`, so a box can be treated like a regular reference to `T`.
options.D: `Box<T>` maintains a reference count so several boxes can own the same heap value.
options.E: Beyond the heap allocation itself, `Box<T>` adds essentially no bookkeeping overhead.

questions.explanation: A box is just an owning pointer: the value lives on the heap, the pointer on the stack (A), and both are cleaned up when the box is dropped thanks to `Drop` (B). `Deref` lets it be used like `&T` (C), and it carries no extra metadata such as a count (E). Reference counting is what `Rc<T>` does, not `Box<T>` — a box always has exactly one owner (D is false).

questions.section: 15.2 Treating Smart Pointers Like Regular References with Deref
questions.prompt: In an implementation of `Deref`, why is the method signature `fn deref(&self) -> &Self::Target` — returning a reference rather than the inner value itself?

options.A: If `deref` returned the value directly, it would be moved out of the smart pointer; returning `&Self::Target` lets `*y` desugar to `*(y.deref())` without taking ownership of the inner value.
options.B: Returning by value is impossible in trait methods, so a reference is the only option.
options.C: References are always faster to return than values, and `Deref` is performance-critical.
options.D: Because `Self::Target` is an associated type, it can only be named behind a reference.

questions.explanation: When you write `*y`, Rust actually runs `*(y.deref())`. If `deref` returned `Self::Target` by value, the inner value would be moved out of `self`, which is almost never what you want when merely dereferencing. Returning a reference preserves ownership; the outer `*` then performs a plain dereference of that reference.

questions.section: 15.2 Treating Smart Pointers Like Regular References with Deref
questions.prompt: What is the result of running this program?

options.A: Compile error: expected `&str`, found `&MyBox<String>` — the cast must be written explicitly.
options.B: It compiles but panics because `String` cannot be viewed as `&str` implicitly.
options.C: It prints `Hello, Rust!` — deref coercion converts `&MyBox<String>` to `&String` and then to `&str`.
options.D: It prints `Hello, MyBox("Rust")!` because the wrapper is formatted directly.

questions.explanation: Because `MyBox<T>` implements `Deref<Target = T>`, deref coercion turns `&MyBox<String>` into `&String`, and since the standard library implements `Deref<Target = str>` for `String`, that becomes `&str`. Without coercion you would have to write the noisy equivalent `hello(&(*m)[..])`.

questions.section: 15.2 Treating Smart Pointers Like Regular References with Deref
questions.prompt: Which of the following are deref coercions Rust performs automatically when types don't match at a function or method call? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: From `&T` to `&U` when `T: Deref<Target = U>`.
options.B: From `&mut T` to `&mut U` when `T: DerefMut<Target = U>`.
options.C: From `&mut T` to `&U` when `T: Deref<Target = U>`.
options.D: From `&T` to `&mut U` when `T: Deref<Target = U>`.
options.E: All required `deref`/`deref_mut` insertions are resolved at compile time, so coercion has no runtime cost.

questions.explanation: Rust performs exactly three coercions: immutable to immutable (A), mutable to mutable via `DerefMut` (B), and mutable to immutable (C) — downgrading a mutable borrow is always safe. The reverse, `&T` to `&mut U` (D), is never allowed: it would create a mutable reference from an immutable one, violating the borrowing rules. The number of `deref` calls is determined at compile time (E).

questions.section: 15.2 Treating Smart Pointers Like Regular References with Deref
questions.prompt: Why does this code fail?

options.A: It compiles but the assertion fails because `*y` reads uninitialized memory.
options.B: It panics at runtime: dereferencing a struct is undefined until `new` finishes.
options.C: It fails because `assert_eq!` cannot compare an `i32` with a generic `T`.
options.D: It fails to compile (E0614): `MyBox<i32>` cannot be dereferenced because it does not implement `Deref`; adding `impl<T> Deref for MyBox<T>` with `fn deref(&self) -> &T { &self.0 }` fixes it.

questions.explanation: The `*` operator only works on references and on types implementing `Deref`. `MyBox` is a plain tuple struct with no `Deref` impl, so `*y` is a compile-time error (E0614: type cannot be dereferenced) — nothing ever runs. Implementing `Deref` with `Target = T` returning `&self.0` makes `*y` desugar to `*(y.deref())`.

questions.section: 15.2 Treating Smart Pointers Like Regular References with Deref
questions.prompt: A function `fn takes(s: &str)` is called as `takes(&b)` where `b: Box<String>`. When and how does the argument become a `&str`?

options.A: At runtime: each call walks the chain `Box<String>` → `String` → `str` through dynamic dispatch, adding a small per-call cost.
options.B: At compile time: the compiler inserts the exact sequence of `deref` calls (`&Box<String>` → `&String` → `&str`) into the generated code, so there is no runtime penalty compared with writing the conversions by hand.
options.C: It does not compile — only one level of deref coercion is applied per call, so `&Box<String>` can reach `&String` but not `&str`.
options.D: The allocator reallocates the `String` as a `str` slice the first time the coercion happens.

questions.explanation: Deref coercion chains as many times as needed: `Box<String>: Deref<Target = String>` and `String: Deref<Target = str>`, so `&Box<String>` coerces to `&String` and then `&str`. The number of `Deref::deref` insertions is resolved entirely at compile time, so using the coercion costs nothing at runtime — a "one level only" limit on deref coercion does not exist.

questions.section: 15.3 Running Code on Cleanup with the Drop Trait
questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `created`, then `Dropping second`, then `Dropping first`
options.B: `created`, then `Dropping first`, then `Dropping second`
options.C: `Dropping first`, `Dropping second`, then `created`
options.D: Only `created` — `drop` runs solely when `std::mem::drop` is called explicitly.

questions.explanation: Values are dropped at the end of scope in reverse order of declaration (LIFO): `_b` was created last, so it is dropped first. The `Drop::drop` method is called automatically when each value goes out of scope — no explicit call is needed.

questions.section: 15.3 Running Code on Cleanup with the Drop Trait
questions.prompt: This code tries to free a value early. What happens?

options.A: It works: `c.drop()` runs the destructor immediately, and `c` is not dropped again.
options.B: It compiles, but the destructor runs twice — once for `c.drop()` and once at end of scope.
options.C: It fails to compile with E0040 "explicit use of destructor method"; the fix is to call `drop(c)` (i.e., `std::mem::drop`, in the prelude) instead.
options.D: It panics at runtime with a double-free error.

questions.explanation: Rust forbids calling the `Drop` trait method directly, because it would still automatically drop the value at end of scope, causing a double free. The compile error E0040 even suggests the fix: `drop(c)`. `std::mem::drop` takes ownership of the value, so it is dropped exactly once, immediately.

questions.section: 15.3 Running Code on Cleanup with the Drop Trait
questions.prompt: What is the exact output of this program?

options.A: `created`, `done`, `Dropping \`some data\`!` — destructors always run last, at the end of `main`.
options.B: `created`, `Dropping \`some data\`!`, `done` — `drop(c)` takes ownership and destroys the value immediately, and it is not dropped again later.
options.C: `created`, `Dropping \`some data\`!`, `done`, `Dropping \`some data\`!` — dropped both early and at end of scope.
options.D: Compile error: `drop` cannot be called on a type that implements `Drop`.

questions.explanation: `std::mem::drop` (in the prelude) is a plain function that takes ownership of its argument; the value is dropped when that function's parameter goes out of scope — i.e., immediately. Because ownership moved into `drop`, `c` is not dropped a second time at the end of `main`, so the destructor line appears exactly once, between `created` and `done`.

questions.section: 15.4 Rc<T>, the Reference Counted Smart Pointer
questions.prompt: Which situation is `Rc<T>` designed for?

options.A: Sharing data between threads — each thread holds one `Rc` handle.
options.B: Allowing several handles to mutate the same value simultaneously.
options.C: Allocating any value on the heap when its size is unknown at compile time.
options.D: Letting multiple parts of a single-threaded program share read-only ownership of the same heap data, freeing it only when the last owner goes away.

questions.explanation: `Rc<T>` enables multiple ownership via a reference count: the value is dropped when the count reaches zero. It is strictly single-threaded (its count updates are not thread-safe — use `Arc<T>` across threads, so A is wrong) and it hands out only immutable access (pair it with `RefCell<T>` for mutation, so B is wrong). Plain heap allocation with one owner is `Box<T>`'s job (C).

questions.section: 15.4 Rc<T>, the Reference Counted Smart Pointer
questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.D: 1, 1, 1, 1 — `Rc::clone` copies the data, so the count of `a` never changes

questions.explanation: Creating the `Rc` sets the strong count to 1; each `Rc::clone(&a)` bumps it (2, then 3). When `_c` goes out of scope at the end of the inner block, its `Rc` is dropped and the count automatically decreases back to 2. `Rc::clone` does not copy the list data — it only increments the counter.

questions.section: 15.4 Rc<T>, the Reference Counted Smart Pointer
questions.prompt: Why does this code fail?

options.A: It compiles but panics: two owners may not exist while the string is being modified.
options.B: It fails to compile: `Rc<T>` only gives immutable access to its contents, so `push_str` (which needs `&mut self`) is rejected; use `Rc<RefCell<String>>` if shared mutation is needed.
options.C: It fails to compile because `Rc::clone` moved `a`, so `a` can no longer be used.
options.D: It prints `hi there` — `Rc` transparently forwards mutable calls to the inner value.

questions.explanation: If `Rc<T>` allowed mutation, multiple owners could hold mutable access to the same data at once, violating the borrowing rules — so `Rc` dereferences only to `&T`, and calling `push_str` fails at compile time (cannot borrow data in an `Rc` as mutable). The standard pattern for shared mutable data in single-threaded code is `Rc<RefCell<T>>`. `Rc::clone` takes `&a`, so `a` is not moved (C is wrong).

questions.section: 15.4 Rc<T>, the Reference Counted Smart Pointer
questions.prompt: "`Rc::clone(&a)` performs a deep copy of the data, so it should be avoided in performance-sensitive code." What is wrong with this claim?

options.A: Nothing — `Rc::clone` deep-copies, which is why `a.clone()` is preferred as the shallow variant.
options.B: `Rc::clone` is deep only for types that implement `Clone`; otherwise it is shallow.
options.C: `Rc::clone` never copies the data at all — it just increments the strong reference count, which is cheap. The `Rc::clone(&a)` spelling is a convention so readers can tell at a glance it is not a deep copy (unlike most `.clone()` calls).
options.D: The claim is right about the copy but wrong about performance: deep copies of reference-counted data are optimized away.

questions.explanation: Cloning an `Rc` only bumps an integer counter; the heap data is shared, not duplicated. `a.clone()` would compile and do exactly the same thing, but Rust convention prefers `Rc::clone(&a)` so that a visual scan for expensive `clone()` calls can skip reference-count bumps.

questions.section: 15.5 RefCell<T> and the Interior Mutability Pattern
questions.prompt: What is the interior mutability pattern, as implemented by `RefCell<T>`?

options.A: Mutating data even when there are only immutable references to it: `RefCell` uses `unsafe` code internally behind a safe API, and enforces the borrowing rules (many readers XOR one writer) at runtime instead of compile time.
options.B: A compiler flag that disables the borrow checker for a single type.
options.C: A way to get compile-time-checked mutation through shared references, with zero runtime bookkeeping.
options.D: A pattern where the inner value is copied on every mutation so the original stays immutable.

questions.explanation: `RefCell<T>` lets you mutate the value inside even when the `RefCell` itself is accessed through `&self` or another immutable handle. The borrowing rules still apply — they are just checked dynamically: `borrow()` returns a `Ref<T>`, `borrow_mut()` a `RefMut<T>`, and the cell tracks active borrows at runtime, panicking on violations rather than failing to compile.

questions.section: 15.5 RefCell<T> and the Interior Mutability Pattern
questions.prompt: What is the result of running this program?

options.A: It fails to compile: two mutable borrows of `cell` are active at once.
options.B: It compiles, but panics at runtime on the second `borrow_mut()` with a `BorrowMutError` (the message text varies by Rust version, e.g. `RefCell already borrowed`).
options.C: It prints `[1, 2, 3, 4]` — `RefCell` permits any number of mutable borrows.
options.D: It is undefined behavior: both handles write to the vector unsynchronized.

questions.explanation: This is the defining trade-off of `RefCell<T>`: the borrow checker sees only calls to safe methods, so the code compiles. At runtime the cell tracks active borrows; the second `borrow_mut()` while the first `RefMut` is still alive violates the one-mutable-borrow rule and panics with `BorrowMutError`. With `&mut` references instead, this would have been a compile error — `RefCell` defers the check to runtime, it never removes it.

questions.section: 15.5 RefCell<T> and the Interior Mutability Pattern
questions.prompt: This mock object for testing does not compile. The `Messenger` trait is fixed and cannot be changed. What is the problem and the idiomatic fix?

options.A: The problem is the missing `mut` on `message`; declare `fn send(&self, mut msg: &str)`.
options.B: `Vec<String>` cannot live inside a struct; store `Box<Vec<String>>` instead.
options.C: The trait itself is unimplementable; wrap `MockMessenger` in `Rc` so `send` can be called.
options.D: `send` takes `&self`, so `self.sent_messages` cannot be mutated; since the trait signature is fixed, store the field as `RefCell<Vec<String>>` and call `self.sent_messages.borrow_mut().push(...)` — interior mutability.

questions.explanation: `push` needs `&mut self.sent_messages`, but everything reachable through `&self` is immutable, so the compiler rejects the call. Changing `send` to `&mut self` would no longer match the trait. `RefCell<Vec<String>>` is exactly the escape hatch: the field can be mutated through `borrow_mut()` even behind `&self`, with the borrow rules checked at runtime; the test then reads it via `borrow()`.

questions.section: 15.5 RefCell<T> and the Interior Mutability Pattern
questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `5` — `shared` was cloned before the mutation, so it still sees the old value.
options.B: Compile error: the contents of an `Rc` cannot be mutated.
options.C: `15` — both `Rc` handles point to the same `RefCell`, so the mutation through `value` is visible through `shared`.
options.D: Runtime panic: `borrow()` after a `borrow_mut()` always fails.

questions.explanation: `Rc<RefCell<T>>` is the standard combo for shared mutable data: `Rc` gives multiple owners of the *same* `RefCell` (cloning the `Rc` does not copy the cell), and `RefCell` allows mutation through those shared handles. The `RefMut` returned by `borrow_mut()` is a temporary that is dropped at the end of its statement, so the later `borrow()` succeeds and prints `15`.

questions.section: 15.5 RefCell<T> and the Interior Mutability Pattern
questions.prompt: Which statements from the `Box<T>` / `Rc<T>` / `RefCell<T>` comparison are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `Box<T>` has a single owner and allows immutable or mutable borrows checked at compile time.
options.B: `Rc<T>` allows multiple owners of the same data but only immutable borrows, checked at compile time.
options.C: `RefCell<T>` has a single owner and allows immutable or mutable borrows checked at runtime.
options.D: Because `RefCell<T>` checks borrows at runtime, you can mutate the value inside it even when the `RefCell` itself is behind an immutable reference.
options.E: `Rc<T>` and `RefCell<T>` are both safe to share between threads.

questions.explanation: A, B, and C restate the book's comparison table exactly: the axes are number of owners (only `Rc` allows several) and when borrow rules are enforced (only `RefCell` defers to runtime). D is the essence of interior mutability. E is false — neither type is thread-safe; across threads you would reach for `Arc<T>` and `Mutex<T>` instead.

questions.section: 15.5 RefCell<T> and the Interior Mutability Pattern
questions.prompt: What happens when this program runs?

options.A: It panics with `BorrowError`: once a `RefCell` has been mutably borrowed, it can never be immutably borrowed again.
options.B: Compile error: `borrow` and `borrow_mut` may not be called on the same cell in one function.
options.C: It prints `hi there` — the `RefMut` is dropped at the end of the inner block, decrementing the runtime borrow counter, so the later `borrow()` succeeds.
options.D: It prints `hi` — mutations through a `RefMut` are discarded when it is dropped.

questions.explanation: `RefCell`'s runtime tracking is scoped just like compile-time borrows: the `RefMut` guard returned by `borrow_mut()` releases the mutable borrow when it is dropped at the closing brace. After that, the borrow counter is back to zero, so `borrow()` succeeds. A panic would only occur if the two borrows were *simultaneously* active. Mutations happen in place — nothing is rolled back (D is wrong).

questions.prompt: Two `Rc` values end up pointing at each other (`a`'s tail refers to `b`, and `b`'s tail is mutated via a `RefCell` to refer back to `a`). What happens when both variables go out of scope?

options.A: When each variable is dropped, the strong count of the value it points to falls from 2 to 1, never reaching 0, so neither value is freed: the memory is leaked. Safe Rust permits this — leaks are not undefined behavior, and the compiler does not detect cycles.
options.B: The compiler detects the cycle at compile time and refuses to build the program.
options.C: Rust's runtime includes a cycle-collecting garbage collector that eventually reclaims the pair.
options.D: The program panics with a `CycleError` as soon as the second link is created.

questions.explanation: `Rc` frees its value only when the strong count hits 0. In a cycle each value is kept alive by the other's strong reference, so after the stack variables drop, both counts sit at 1 forever — a silent memory leak (and trying to print the cyclic list would recurse until the stack overflows). Rust has no cycle detector or GC; preventing cycles is the programmer's job, typically using `Weak<T>` for back-links.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `true` then `2 0` — `branch` is kept alive by `leaf`'s parent pointer.
options.B: `false` then `1 0` — `branch` was dropped at the end of the block despite the weak reference to it, so `upgrade()` returns `None`; dropping `branch` also released its `Rc` to `leaf`, leaving `leaf` with strong count 1 and weak count 0.
options.C: Compile error: `branch` cannot be dropped while `leaf.parent` still refers to it.
options.D: It panics: calling `upgrade()` on a dangling `Weak` is a runtime error.

questions.explanation: A `Weak` reference does not keep its target alive: only the strong count controls dropping. At the end of the inner block `branch`'s strong count falls to 0 (the weak count of 1 from `leaf.parent` does not matter), so it is freed, its `children` vector drops the `Rc<Node>` to `leaf`, and `leaf`'s strong count returns to 1. `upgrade()` then safely returns `None` (printed as `false`) — no panic, no dangling pointer. This is exactly why parent links use `Weak`: it breaks the parent↔child cycle.

questions.prompt: Which statements about `Weak<T>` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `Rc::downgrade(&rc)` creates a `Weak<T>` and increments `weak_count`, leaving `strong_count` unchanged.
options.B: `Weak<T>::upgrade()` returns `Option<Rc<T>>`: `Some` if the value is still alive, `None` if it has been dropped.
options.C: A value cannot be dropped while its `weak_count` is greater than zero.
options.D: In a parent-child tree, children should hold their parent as `Weak<Node>` (while parents own children via `Rc<Node>`) so the parent↔child references do not form a leaking cycle.
options.E: Code using a `Weak<T>` must handle the possibility that the value no longer exists, which is what makes weak references memory-safe.

questions.explanation: `downgrade` only touches the weak count (A), and `upgrade` is the checked way in — the `Option` forces you to handle a dead target (B, E), so weak references can never dangle. C is exactly backwards: cleanup depends only on `strong_count` reaching zero; any number of outstanding `Weak`s will simply start upgrading to `None`. The tree pattern in D gives ownership one direction (`Rc` down) and a non-owning back-link (`Weak` up), so no strong cycle can form.

questions.prompt: How do you create a new thread in Rust's standard library?

options.A: Call `thread::spawn` and pass it a closure containing the code the new thread should run
options.B: Call `thread::new` and pass it a function pointer registered with the runtime
options.C: Annotate a function with `#[thread]` so the compiler runs it concurrently
options.D: Call `thread::spawn` with a string naming the function to execute

questions.explanation: `thread::spawn` takes a closure (or other `FnOnce`) containing the code to run in the new thread. The standard library uses a 1:1 model, so each call creates one OS thread.

questions.prompt: What is the most accurate description of this program's output?

options.A: It always prints all 9 `spawned` lines and all 4 `main` lines, in some interleaved order
options.B: The `main` lines print, but the spawned thread's output is most likely truncated, because the program exits when the main thread finishes
options.C: It fails to compile because the closure does not use the `move` keyword
options.D: It prints all 9 `spawned` lines first, then the 4 `main` lines

questions.explanation: When the main thread ends, the whole program exits and all spawned threads are shut down, whether or not they finished. Since nothing joins the spawned thread, it will most likely be cut off before printing all 9 lines. The interleaving of the lines that do print is nondeterministic.

questions.prompt: What does calling `join()` on the `JoinHandle` returned by `thread::spawn` do?

options.A: It merges the spawned thread's stack into the main thread's stack
options.B: It blocks the calling thread until the spawned thread finishes, returning a `Result`
options.C: It immediately terminates the spawned thread and reclaims its resources
options.D: It schedules the spawned thread to run at a higher priority

questions.explanation: `join()` blocks the thread that calls it until the thread the handle belongs to terminates. It returns a `Result` (an `Err` if the spawned thread panicked), which the book's examples handle with `unwrap()`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `spawned: 1`, `spawned: 2`, `spawned: 3`, then `main: 1`, `main: 2` — always in that order
options.B: The `spawned` and `main` lines interleave in a nondeterministic order
options.C: Only the `main` lines, because the spawned thread is killed when `main` continues
options.D: It deadlocks, because `join` cannot be called before the main thread's loop

questions.explanation: Because `join` is called before the main thread's loop, main blocks until the spawned thread completes all its iterations. The output is deterministic: all `spawned` lines, then all `main` lines. Moving `join` after the loop would allow nondeterministic interleaving — where you call `join` changes the behavior.

questions.prompt: Why does this code fail to compile?

options.A: `Vec<i32>` does not implement the `Send` trait, so it can never be used in another thread
options.B: The closure borrows `v`, but the spawned thread may outlive `main`'s ownership of `v`; the compiler suggests adding `move` (error E0373)
options.C: It compiles fine but panics at runtime when the spawned thread accesses freed memory
options.D: `println!` cannot be used inside a spawned thread

questions.explanation: The closure only borrows `v`, and Rust can't guarantee the spawned thread won't outlive `v` (e.g., if the handle were leaked or `v` were dropped first). The compiler rejects it with E0373 ("closure may outlive the current function, but it borrows `v`") and suggests `move` to transfer ownership into the closure. Rust turns this potential dangling reference into a compile error, not a runtime crash.

questions.prompt: Select ALL statements that are true about `move` closures used with `thread::spawn`.

options.A: `move` forces the closure to take ownership of the environment values it uses, rather than borrowing them
options.B: After moving `v` into the closure, `main` can still use `v`, because `move` only moves a reference
options.C: Without `move`, a closure that captures a local variable by reference cannot be passed to `thread::spawn`, because the new thread might outlive the borrowed value
options.D: `move` guarantees the spawned thread runs before any code after `thread::spawn` in the main thread
options.E: If both threads need the data, cloning it before the `move` closure captures it is a valid fix

questions.explanation: A and C are the core rules: `thread::spawn` requires the closure to own its captures (or capture only `'static` data), and `move` accomplishes that. E is the compiler's own suggested workaround for E0382. B is false — using `v` in `main` after the move is a compile error (use of moved value). D is false — `move` affects ownership, not scheduling.

questions.section: 16.2 Using Message Passing to Transfer Data Between Threads
questions.prompt: What does `mpsc::channel()` return, and what does `mpsc` stand for?

options.A: A single `Channel` object shared by all threads; `mpsc` means "multi-purpose synchronized channel"
options.B: A `(transmitter, receiver)` tuple, conventionally `(tx, rx)`; `mpsc` means "multiple producer, single consumer"
options.C: A `(receiver, transmitter)` tuple; `mpsc` means "multiple producer, single consumer"
options.D: A `(transmitter, receiver)` tuple; `mpsc` means "multiple producer, shared consumer", so `rx` can also be cloned

questions.explanation: `mpsc::channel()` returns a tuple whose first element is the transmitter (`tx`) and second is the receiver (`rx`). `mpsc` stands for multiple producer, single consumer: you can clone `tx` to get many senders, but there is only one receiver.

questions.section: 16.2 Using Message Passing to Transfer Data Between Threads
questions.prompt: What is wrong with this code?

options.A: Nothing — it prints `val is hi` and `Got: hi`
options.B: It compiles, but `println!("val is {val}")` prints garbage at runtime because the receiver may have modified the string
options.C: It fails to compile with E0382: `send` takes ownership of `val`, so using `val` afterwards is a use after move
options.D: It fails to compile because `tx` cannot be moved into the closure

questions.explanation: `send` moves its argument, transferring ownership down the channel. Using `val` after `tx.send(val)` is rejected at compile time with E0382 ("borrow of moved value: `val`"). This compile-time rule prevents a thread from accidentally using or mutating a value after another thread may have taken it.

questions.section: 16.2 Using Message Passing to Transfer Data Between Threads
questions.prompt: What is the difference between `rx.recv()` and `rx.try_recv()`?

options.A: `recv` blocks until a message arrives (or the channel closes), while `try_recv` returns immediately with `Ok` or an `Err` if no message is waiting
options.B: `recv` is non-blocking, while `try_recv` retries in a loop until a message arrives
options.C: They behave identically, but `try_recv` returns an `Option` instead of a `Result`
options.D: `recv` can only be called once, while `try_recv` can be called repeatedly

questions.explanation: `recv` blocks the calling thread until a value is available or all senders have been dropped, returning `Result<T, RecvError>`. `try_recv` never blocks: it returns immediately, with `Err` if no message is currently available — useful when the receiving thread has other work to do.

questions.section: 16.2 Using Message Passing to Transfer Data Between Threads
questions.prompt: What does this program do?

options.A: It prints the four `Got:` lines, then hangs forever waiting for more messages
options.B: It prints the four `Got:` lines, then the loop ends and the program exits, because iteration over `rx` stops when all senders are dropped
options.C: It fails to compile because a `Receiver` cannot be used in a `for` loop
options.D: It panics after the fourth message because `recv` returns an `Err` on a closed channel

questions.explanation: `Receiver` can be used as an iterator: each iteration waits for the next message, and the iteration ends cleanly when the channel closes — which happens when every transmitter (`tx`) has been dropped. Here `tx` was moved into the spawned thread and is dropped when that thread finishes, so the loop terminates after four messages. No panic occurs; the iterator just yields `None` internally.

questions.section: 16.2 Using Message Passing to Transfer Data Between Threads
questions.prompt: Two threads send over the same channel via a cloned transmitter. What is true of the output?

options.A: It fails to compile: a `Sender` cannot be cloned because the channel is single-producer
options.B: All 8 messages are printed; messages from each thread stay in their per-thread order, but how the two threads' messages interleave is nondeterministic
options.C: Only the first thread's 4 messages are printed, because cloning `tx` creates a second, separate channel
options.D: The loop never ends, because the original `tx` still exists in `main` after cloning

questions.explanation: Cloning the transmitter is exactly how mpsc supports multiple producers. All 8 messages reach the single receiver; each sender's own messages arrive in the order it sent them, but the interleaving between the two threads varies run to run. Both `tx` and `tx1` were moved into threads, so once both threads finish, all senders are dropped and the loop ends. (If `tx` had stayed usable in `main`, D's concern would be real.)

questions.section: 16.2 Using Message Passing to Transfer Data Between Threads
questions.prompt: Select ALL statements that are true about `std::sync::mpsc` channels.

options.A: `send` takes ownership of the value being sent, and the receiver takes ownership when the value arrives
options.B: `recv` blocks the calling thread until a value arrives or all senders have been dropped
options.C: `try_recv` blocks for a short built-in timeout before returning an error
options.D: A `for` loop over the receiver ends when the channel is closed, i.e. when all transmitters have been dropped
options.E: `send` panics automatically whenever the receiver has been dropped

questions.explanation: A, B, and D describe the actual API. C is false: `try_recv` returns immediately, never blocking. E is false: `send` returns `Result<(), SendError<T>>` — it returns an `Err` if the receiver was dropped, and only panics if you choose to `unwrap()` that error.

questions.prompt: What does `Mutex::lock()` return, and when is it an `Err`?

options.A: It returns the inner value `T` directly, and panics if the lock is held
options.B: It returns a `Result` containing a `MutexGuard`; it is an `Err` if another thread holding the lock panicked (the mutex is poisoned)
options.C: It returns `Option<MutexGuard>`; `None` means another thread currently holds the lock
options.D: It returns a `Result`; it is an `Err` whenever the lock is currently held by another thread

questions.explanation: `lock()` blocks until the lock is acquired and returns `LockResult<MutexGuard<T>>`. The `Err` case occurs when the mutex is poisoned — a thread panicked while holding the lock — not merely because the lock is busy. The book's examples call `unwrap()` to panic in that situation.

questions.prompt: How is a `Mutex` lock released in safe Rust?

options.A: You must call `mutex.unlock()` explicitly, or the lock is held forever
options.B: The lock is released when the `MutexGuard` is dropped — typically when it goes out of scope — because `MutexGuard` implements `Drop`
options.C: The lock is released as soon as you finish reading or writing through the guard, tracked automatically at runtime
options.D: The lock is released when the owning thread calls `thread::yield_now()`

questions.explanation: `lock()` returns a smart pointer, `MutexGuard`, which implements `Deref` to reach the inner data and `Drop` to release the lock automatically when the guard goes out of scope. This design means you can't forget to unlock — releasing happens deterministically at guard drop, and there is no `unlock` method to call in safe code.

questions.prompt: What does this single-threaded program print?

options.A: It prints the mutex with inner value `6`, e.g. `m = Mutex { data: 6, ... }`
options.B: It prints the mutex with inner value `5`, because `m` was not declared `mut`
options.C: It fails to compile: you cannot mutate through `m` because `m` is an immutable binding
options.D: It deadlocks at `println!` because the lock from the inner scope is still held

questions.explanation: `Mutex<T>` provides interior mutability, so the value can be changed through a shared binding once the lock is acquired. Dereferencing the `MutexGuard` sets the value to 6, and the guard is dropped at the end of the inner scope, releasing the lock — so the `println!` can safely observe the mutex containing 6. No `mut` on `m` is needed.

questions.prompt: Why does this code fail to compile?

options.A: E0382: `counter` is moved into the closure on the first loop iteration, so later iterations (and the final `println!`) use a moved value
options.B: `Mutex<i32>` does not implement `Send`, so it cannot be used from a spawned thread
options.C: It compiles, but the result is nondeterministic because the increments race
options.D: The closure needs `&mut counter`, which requires `counter` to be declared `mut`

questions.explanation: The `move` closure takes ownership of `counter`, and ownership can only be given away once — the second loop iteration already has no `counter` to move. The compiler reports E0382 ("borrow of moved value: `counter`"). The fix is shared ownership across threads: wrap the mutex in `Arc` and give each thread its own `Arc::clone`. Note the mutex itself would prevent a data race (C), but this code never gets past the compiler.

questions.prompt: To share the counter, a programmer wraps it in `Rc`. What happens?

options.A: It works: `Rc::clone` gives each thread shared ownership, and the mutex serializes the increments
options.B: It compiles but the reference count gets corrupted at runtime, causing a use-after-free
options.C: It fails to compile with E0277: `Rc<Mutex<i32>>` cannot be sent between threads safely, because `Rc` does not implement `Send`; the fix is to use `Arc` instead
options.D: It fails to compile because `Rc::clone` cannot be called inside a loop

questions.explanation: `Rc<T>` updates its reference count with non-atomic operations, so it is not thread-safe and does not implement `Send`. The compiler rejects moving it into `thread::spawn` with E0277. `Arc<T>` (atomically reference counted) has the same API but uses atomic count updates, making it safe to share across threads — the potential runtime corruption in B is exactly what Rust prevents at compile time.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `Result: 10`, every time — the mutex serializes the increments and every handle is joined before printing
options.B: A number between 0 and 10, varying by run, because the increments race
options.C: It fails to compile because `Arc` does not provide mutable access to its contents
options.D: `Result: 0`, because each thread increments its own clone of the counter

questions.explanation: Each thread gets an `Arc` clone pointing at the same `Mutex`; the lock makes each `+= 1` exclusive, and joining all 10 handles guarantees every increment happened before the final read. So the output is deterministically `Result: 10`. `Arc::clone` clones the pointer, not the data (ruling out D), and the `Mutex` inside supplies the mutability `Arc` alone lacks (ruling out C).

questions.prompt: Select ALL statements that are true about `Mutex<T>` in Rust.

options.A: `Mutex<T>` provides interior mutability: you can mutate the inner value through a shared reference, similar to `RefCell<T>`
options.B: The `MutexGuard` returned by `lock()` releases the lock automatically when it is dropped
options.C: Rust's compiler statically prevents deadlocks when using multiple mutexes
options.D: Using `Mutex<T>` carries a deadlock risk (e.g., two threads each holding one of two locks and waiting for the other), which Rust cannot catch at compile time
options.E: You must call an explicit `unlock()` method after mutating the data, or other threads block forever

questions.explanation: A: `Arc<Mutex<T>>` mirrors `Rc<RefCell<T>>` — the mutex supplies interior mutability. B and D: unlocking is automatic via `Drop`, but logic errors like deadlocks remain possible; Rust's type system prevents data races and invalid references, not deadlocks. C is therefore false, and E is false because there is no explicit unlock in safe Rust — dropping the guard releases the lock.

questions.section: 16.4 Extensible Concurrency with the Send and Sync Traits
questions.prompt: Which statement correctly describes the `Send` and `Sync` marker traits?

options.A: `Send` means ownership of the type can be transferred to another thread; `Sync` means the type can be referenced from multiple threads — formally, `T` is `Sync` if `&T` is `Send`
options.B: `Send` means the type can be serialized over a channel; `Sync` means the type is protected by an internal lock
options.C: `Send` means the type can be referenced from multiple threads; `Sync` means ownership can be transferred between threads
options.D: `Send` and `Sync` are equivalent; every `Send` type is automatically `Sync` and vice versa

questions.explanation: `Send` allows transferring ownership of a value across threads; `Sync` allows a type to be safely referenced from multiple threads, defined precisely as: `T` is `Sync` if and only if `&T` is `Send`. They are distinct properties — e.g., `RefCell<T>` can be `Send` yet is not `Sync`.

questions.section: 16.4 Extensible Concurrency with the Send and Sync Traits
questions.prompt: Which statement is true about `Rc<T>` and `RefCell<T>` in multithreaded code?

options.A: `Rc<T>` is `Send` but not `Sync`, so it can be moved to another thread but not shared
options.B: `Rc<T>` implements neither `Send` nor `Sync`, and `RefCell<T>` is not `Sync`; their thread-safe counterparts are `Arc<T>` and `Mutex<T>`
options.C: `RefCell<T>` is `Sync` because its borrow rules are checked at runtime
options.D: Wrapping an `Rc<T>` inside a `Mutex` makes it safe to send between threads

questions.explanation: `Rc<T>` uses non-atomic reference counting, so it is neither `Send` nor `Sync`. `RefCell<T>`'s runtime borrow tracking is not thread-safe, so it is not `Sync`. The thread-safe alternatives are `Arc<T>` and `Mutex<T>` (or `RwLock<T>`). D is false: a composite type is only `Send` if its components are, so `Mutex<Rc<T>>` still is not `Send`.

questions.section: 16.4 Extensible Concurrency with the Send and Sync Traits
questions.prompt: How do types come to implement `Send` and `Sync`, and what does implementing them manually involve?

options.A: You derive them with `#[derive(Send, Sync)]`, and the compiler generates the required methods
options.B: Every type must implement them explicitly in safe code by writing the traits' required methods
options.C: Types composed entirely of `Send`/`Sync` parts are automatically `Send`/`Sync`; since these marker traits have no methods, implementing them manually requires an `unsafe impl`, because you are asserting thread-safety guarantees the compiler cannot verify
options.D: Only standard library types can implement `Send` and `Sync`; user-defined types never do

questions.explanation: `Send` and `Sync` are marker traits with no methods.
