The compiler implements them automatically for any type made entirely of `Send`/`Sync` components — so user code almost never implements them by hand. Doing so (e.g., `unsafe impl Send for MyType {}`) requires the `unsafe` keyword because you, not the compiler, are guaranteeing the concurrency invariants; getting it wrong can cause data races and undefined behavior.

title: Fundamentals of Asynchronous Programming: Async, Await, Futures, and Streams

questions.prompt: The book shows that `async fn page_title(url: &str) -> Option<String>` is roughly equivalent to a non-async function. What is that function's return type?

options.A: `Option<String>` — the async keyword does not change the return type
options.B: `impl Future<Output = Option<String>>` — an anonymous compiler-generated type implementing `Future`
options.C: `Poll<Option<String>>` — the result of polling the body once
options.D: `Box<dyn Future<Output = Option<String>>>` — a heap-allocated trait object

questions.explanation: Rust compiles an `async fn` into a non-async function whose body is an `async move` block; the function returns an anonymous data type that implements `Future`, with the associated `Output` type equal to the original return type. So `async fn` is equivalent to a function returning `impl Future<Output = Option<String>>`.

questions.prompt: Inside an async block you write the following and never use `title_fut` again. What actually happens to the HTTP request?
questions.code:
let title_fut = page_title("https://www.rust-lang.org");
// title_fut is never awaited

options.A: The request starts running in the background as soon as `page_title` is called
options.B: Nothing happens — futures in Rust are lazy and do no work until awaited; the compiler even warns about the unused future
options.C: The request runs and blocks the current thread until it completes
options.D: The code fails to compile because every future must be awaited

questions.explanation: Futures in Rust are lazy: they don't do anything until you ask them to with `await`. This differs from `thread::spawn`, where the closure starts running immediately, and from async in many other languages. Rust shows a compiler warning (not an error) if you don't use a future.

questions.prompt: What is wrong with this program?

options.A: Nothing — it compiles and runs correctly
options.B: `main` is not allowed to be `async` (error E0752); use a normal `fn main` that calls `trpl::block_on` with an async block instead
options.C: `.await` cannot appear inside `main` even when a runtime is running
options.D: `match` cannot be used on the result of an awaited future

questions.explanation: Rust rejects `async fn main` with error E0752. Async code needs a runtime that executes futures; `main` can initialize a runtime but is not one itself. The book's fix is `trpl::block_on(async { ... })` inside a normal `fn main`, which sets up a runtime (Tokio under the hood) and blocks the current thread until the future completes. Some runtimes provide macros that rewrite `async fn main` into exactly this pattern.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about `async`/`await` in Rust are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `await` is a postfix keyword (`fut.await`), which makes chains of method calls nicer to work with
options.B: Every await point is a place where control can be handed back to the runtime
options.C: The Rust standard library bundles a default async runtime, like most other languages
options.D: Rust compiles async blocks into invisible state machines — anonymous types that implement `Future`
options.E: `.await` can be used in any function, including ordinary non-async functions

questions.explanation: A: the book emphasizes that `await` goes after the expression, unlike in many other languages. B: each await point represents where control is handed back to the runtime. D: the compiler creates and manages a state machine (like an enum with a variant per await point) implementing `Future`. C is false — Rust does not bundle a runtime; there are many runtime crates with different tradeoffs. E is false — `await` is only allowed in async functions or blocks.

questions.prompt: In Listing 17-5 the book races two page-title fetches with `trpl::select(title_fut_1, title_fut_2).await`. What does this call return?

options.A: A tuple containing the outputs of both futures once both complete
options.B: `Either::Left` with the first argument's output if it finishes first, or `Either::Right` with the second argument's output if that one wins
options.C: A `Result` that is `Ok` for the winning future and `Err` for the losing one
options.D: Always the output of the first argument; the second future is cancelled immediately

questions.explanation: `trpl::select` returns whichever future finishes first, wrapped in `trpl::Either`. Because either future can legitimately "win", there is no notion of success or failure, so `Either` uses `Left`/`Right` matching the argument order rather than `Result`'s `Ok`/`Err`. A tuple of both outputs is what `trpl::join` produces, not `select`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print (run inside `trpl::block_on` as the book does)?

options.A: `A1 B1 A2 B2` — and the exact same order every run, because `trpl::join` is fair and checks each future equally, alternating between them
options.B: An unpredictable interleaving that can differ between runs, just like OS threads
options.C: `A1 A2 B1 B2` — the first future runs to completion before the second starts
options.D: It does not compile: the two async blocks must be spawned with `trpl::spawn_task` before joining

questions.explanation: With `trpl::join` you see the exact same order every time, which is very different from threads or `spawn_task`. The book explains that `trpl::join` is fair: it checks each future equally often, alternating between them, and never lets one race ahead if the other is ready. With threads, the OS decides which thread runs; here the runtime decides deterministically.

questions.prompt: This message-passing program (Listing 17-11) sends and prints all four messages at 500 ms intervals — but there is still a bug. What is it?

options.A: Compile error: `tx` is moved into the first async block and cannot be used afterward
options.B: The program never exits: the sender block only borrows `tx`, so `tx` is never dropped, the channel never closes, `rx.recv()` never resolves to `None`, and the `while let` loop waits forever — fix it by making the sender block `async move`
options.C: The receiver misses messages because `rx.recv()` must be called exactly four times
options.D: `trpl::join` cannot be used with futures that capture channel endpoints

questions.explanation: Sending doesn't require ownership, so the plain `async` block only borrows `tx`. The channel closes only when the sender is dropped, `rx.recv()` returns `None` only when the channel closes, the `while let` loop ends only on `None`, and `trpl::join` completes only when both futures complete — a cycle that never resolves. Changing the sender block to `async move` moves `tx` in, so it is dropped when that block finishes, the channel closes, and the program shuts down gracefully (Listing 17-12).

questions.prompt: You start a long-running counting loop with `trpl::spawn_task(async { ... })` and never use the returned handle. The main async block finishes its own shorter loop first. What happens to the spawned task?

options.A: It keeps running in the background until it finishes, like a detached OS process
options.B: It is shut down when the main function ends; to run it to completion you must await the returned join handle, which is itself a future (its `Output` is a `Result`, so the book unwraps it)
options.C: The program does not compile: the handle returned by `spawn_task` must always be awaited
options.D: The runtime automatically blocks at the end of `main` until every spawned task finishes

questions.explanation: In Listing 17-6 the spawned task's loop is cut short because the task is shut down when the main function ends. Listing 17-7 fixes this with `handle.await.unwrap()`: the task handle is a future (analogous to a thread's `JoinHandle::join`), and awaiting it runs the task to completion. Not awaiting the handle is legal — the task just may not finish.

questions.prompt: How does the async channel from `trpl::channel` differ from the synchronous `std::mpsc::channel` used in Chapter 16?

options.A: The `recv` method produces a future to await: instead of blocking the thread, it hands control back to the runtime until a message arrives or the send side closes (and the receiver `rx` must be mutable)
options.B: The `send` method must also be awaited, because sending can block on a full buffer
options.C: The async channel allows only a single producer, unlike `std::mpsc`
options.D: The async `recv` returns the value directly but panics if no message is ready

questions.explanation: The book notes two API differences: the async version uses a mutable receiver `rx`, and its `recv` method produces a future to await rather than the value directly. The synchronous `Receiver::recv` blocks the thread; the async one hands control back to the runtime. `send` is not awaited because the channel is unbounded, so sending doesn't block — and it is still a multiple-producer channel (you can `clone` the sender, as in Listing 17-13).

questions.section: 17.3 Working With Any Number of Futures
questions.prompt: `slow` calls `std::thread::sleep` (blocking) and then prints. What output does this produce?

options.A: The `slow` calls from `a` and `b` interleave, because the runtime switches tasks whenever one sleeps
options.B: All of `a`'s slow calls run first, then all of `b`'s slow calls, then `'a' finished.` — there is no interleaving because `slow` blocks and control transfers only at await points
options.C: Only `a` runs; `b` is never started because `select` polls just the winner
options.D: It does not compile: `std::thread::sleep` cannot be called inside an async block

questions.explanation: Rust only pauses async blocks and hands control back to the runtime at an await point; everything between await points is synchronous. Each future here does all of its blocking `slow` work before reaching its first `.await` (the `trpl::sleep`), so `a` runs all its slow calls, then `b` runs all of its, and finally `a` completes and `select` finishes. This is the starvation problem — one future blocking others from making progress.

questions.section: 17.3 Working With Any Number of Futures
questions.prompt: According to the book, when can the runtime switch from one future to another?

options.A: At any moment — the runtime preempts futures the way an OS preempts threads
options.B: Only at await points — everything between await points runs synchronously, so a future doing long work without awaiting starves other futures; this is cooperative multitasking
options.C: At every function-call boundary inside an async block
options.D: Only when a future is spawned onto its own task with `trpl::spawn_task`

questions.explanation: Rust hands control back to the runtime only at an await point. This is a form of cooperative multitasking: each future has the power to decide when it yields control via await points, and therefore the responsibility not to block too long. To yield without actually sleeping, the book uses `trpl::yield_now().await`, which is clearer and can be faster than `trpl::sleep` with a tiny duration (the timer always sleeps at least a millisecond).

questions.section: 17.3 Working With Any Number of Futures
questions.prompt: Using the book's `timeout` built from `trpl::select` and `trpl::sleep`, what does this program print?

options.A: `Succeeded with 'Finally finished'`
options.B: `Failed after 5 seconds`
options.C: `Failed after 2 seconds`
options.D: Nothing — the program deadlocks waiting for both futures

questions.explanation: `timeout` races the future against a 2-second timer using `trpl::select`. The `slow` future needs 5 seconds, so the `trpl::sleep(max_time)` timer wins, `select` returns `Either::Right(())`, and `timeout` returns `Err(max_time)` — printing `Failed after 2 seconds`. This shows how futures compose: a new async building block made from `select` and `sleep`.

questions.section: 17.3 Working With Any Number of Futures
questions.prompt: In the `timeout` implementation, why does the book pass `future_to_try` as the *first* argument to `trpl::select`?

options.A: The type signature of `trpl::select` requires the generic future to come before the timer
options.B: Because `trpl::select` is not fair: it always polls its arguments in the order they are passed, so putting `future_to_try` first gives it a chance to complete even when `max_time` is a very short duration
options.C: So the first argument runs on a dedicated thread while the timer runs on the runtime
options.D: Because `Either::Left` is reserved for error values, which must come first

questions.explanation: The book states that `trpl::select`'s implementation is not fair — it always polls arguments in the order in which they are passed (other select implementations may randomize this). Passing `future_to_try` first means it is polled first and can win even against a very short timer. This contrasts with `trpl::join`, which is fair and alternates between futures.

questions.prompt: How does the book describe a stream?

options.A: An asynchronous form of iteration: it provides the next item the way `Iterator` does, but asynchronously
options.B: Another name for an async channel; every stream has a send half and a receive half
options.C: A pool of tasks that the runtime schedules in FIFO order
options.D: A future that resolves exactly once to a collection of items

questions.explanation: A stream is like an asynchronous form of iteration. The async channel receiver's `recv` method, which produces a sequence of items over time, is one instance of this general pattern. The differences from iterators are time (iterators are synchronous, streams asynchronous) and API (`next` vs the awaited methods). Because iterators and streams are so similar, you can create a stream from any iterator with `trpl::stream_from_iter`.

questions.prompt: This code fails to compile with error E0599: "no method named `next` found". Why?

options.A: `trpl::stream_from_iter` does not exist; iterators cannot be converted into streams
options.B: The `next` method comes from the `StreamExt` trait, which is not in scope — adding `use trpl::StreamExt;` fixes the error
options.C: `while let` loops cannot contain `.await` expressions
options.D: Streams must be consumed with `poll_next` directly; there is no `next` method on streams

questions.explanation: Items from traits can only be used if the trait is in scope. `next` is supplied not by `Stream` itself but by `StreamExt` ("Ext" for extension — a common Rust pattern): `Stream` defines the low-level interface combining `Iterator` and `Future`, while `StreamExt` supplies the higher-level utility APIs, including `next`. Adding `use trpl::StreamExt;` makes the code compile and run.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `The value was: 1`, `The value was: 2`, `The value was: 3`
options.B: `The value was: 2`, `The value was: 4`, `The value was: 6`
options.C: Nothing — the stream is lazy and is never started
options.D: It loops forever, because a stream created from an iterator never ends

questions.explanation: The iterator doubles each value with `map(|n| n * 2)`, and `trpl::stream_from_iter` turns it into a stream. The `while let` loop awaits `stream.next()` for each item, printing 2, 4, 6. When the underlying iterator is exhausted, `next().await` produces `None` and the loop ends — the awaiting *is* what drives the stream, so it does run.

questions.section: 17.5 A Closer Look at the Traits for Async
questions.prompt: The `Future` trait is defined as `fn poll(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Self::Output>`. Which statement about `poll` and the `Poll` type is correct?

options.A: `poll` blocks the calling thread until the future's value is ready, then returns it
options.B: `Poll::Pending` means the future still has work to do and the caller should check again later; `Poll::Ready(T)` means it has finished — and most futures should not be polled again after returning `Ready` (many will panic)
options.C: `Poll` is just a type alias for `Option`, so `Pending` and `None` are interchangeable
options.D: Each `.await` compiles into exactly one call to `poll`; if the future is not ready, the program crashes

questions.explanation: `Poll` is similar in shape to `Option` — `Ready(T)` has a value, `Pending` does not — but means something different: `Pending` signals that the future still has work to do. `await` compiles to code that polls repeatedly, but Rust ensures the loop hands control to the runtime between polls instead of blocking (that scheduling is one of the runtime's main jobs). The book also notes most futures panic if polled again after returning `Ready`, similar to `Iterator::next` conventions.

questions.section: 17.5 A Closer Look at the Traits for Async
questions.prompt: This attempt to await a runtime-sized collection of futures fails with error E0277: "`dyn Future<Output = ()>` cannot be unpinned". What is the problem, and the book's fix?

options.A: Trait objects can never be created for the `Future` trait; you must use an enum instead
options.B: `Box<T>` implements `Future` only when `T` implements `Unpin`, which `dyn Future` does not — fix it by pinning the futures (e.g., the `pin!` macro) and using `Vec<Pin<&mut dyn Future<Output = ()>>>`
options.C: `join_all` only accepts fixed-size arrays whose length is known at compile time
options.D: The async blocks must be declared `async move` before they can be boxed

questions.explanation: Directly awaiting a future pins it implicitly, but here a new `JoinAll` future is constructed from a collection, and its bound requires each item to implement `Future` — which `Box<T>` does only if the wrapped `T` implements `Unpin`. Compiler-generated futures may hold references to themselves (self-referential state machines), so they are not automatically `Unpin` and are unsafe to move. Pinning each future with `pin!` (or `Box::pin`, as the error suggests) and typing the vector as `Vec<Pin<&mut dyn Future<Output = ()>>>` makes the code compile and run (Listing 17-24).

questions.section: 17.5 A Closer Look at the Traits for Async
questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about `Pin` and `Unpin` are true, as the book presents them? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: The futures Rust creates for async blocks can end up with references to themselves, and a self-referential value is unsafe to move because its internal references would point to the old memory location
options.B: `Pin` wraps pointer-like types (`&`, `&mut`, `Box`, `Rc`) and guarantees the value ultimately pointed to will not move in memory — with `Pin<Box<SomeType>>` it is the `SomeType` value that is pinned, not the `Box`
options.C: `Unpin` is a marker trait, like `Send` and `Sync`, that the compiler implements automatically for all types where it can prove it is safe — the normal case for most types, such as `String`
options.D: Every `.await` in your code requires you to manually wrap the future with the `pin!` macro first
options.E: You need `Pin<Vec<String>>` before you are allowed to move a `Vec<String>` between functions

questions.explanation: A, B, and C match the book: async state machines may be self-referential, `Pin` pins the pointed-to data (the `Box` pointer itself may still move freely), and `Unpin` is an automatically implemented marker trait with `!Unpin` as the special case. D is false — directly awaiting a future pins it implicitly, which is why you don't need `pin!` everywhere; it only came up for `join_all`. E is false — most types like `Vec<String>` have no internal self-references, implement `Unpin`, and move freely; `Pin`/`Unpin` only matter when using a pinned pointer to a type.

questions.prompt: How does the book describe the relationship among runtimes, tasks, and futures?

options.A: The runtime's executor manages futures directly; tasks are just another name for OS threads
options.B: The runtime — specifically its executor — manages tasks, and tasks manage futures; futures are Rust's most granular unit of concurrency, and a task is like a lightweight thread managed by the runtime instead of the operating system
options.C: Each future is assigned its own operating system thread, and tasks group threads together
options.D: Tasks are managed by the operating system, while threads are managed by library-level code

questions.explanation: A task is similar to a thread but is managed by library-level code (the runtime) rather than the OS. Threads act as a boundary for sets of synchronous operations; tasks act as a boundary for sets of asynchronous operations — and concurrency is possible both between and within tasks, because a task can switch between the futures in its body. Each future may itself represent a tree of other futures.

questions.prompt: Which of the following match the book's guidance on choosing between threads and async? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: If the work is very parallelizable (CPU-bound), such as processing data where each part can be processed separately, threads are a better choice
options.B: If the work is very concurrent (I/O-bound), such as handling messages from many different sources arriving at different rates, async is a better choice
options.C: A program must commit to either threads or async; the two models cannot be combined
options.D: A task is managed by library-level code — the runtime — rather than by the operating system, making tasks similar to lightweight, runtime-managed threads
options.E: Threads have a native equivalent of futures and pause themselves cooperatively at await points

questions.explanation: The book's rules of thumb: CPU-bound, parallelizable work favors threads; I/O-bound, concurrent work favors async. C is false — the choice is often threads *and* async: you can freely combine them (e.g., video encoding on a dedicated thread, with completion notified over an async channel), and the runtime used in the chapter is itself multithreaded, using work stealing to move tasks between threads. E is false — threads are "fire and forget" with no native equivalent to a future; they run to completion, interrupted only by the OS.

questions.prompt: This program mixes a thread with async code (based on Listing 17-25). What happens when it runs?

options.A: It prints `1`, `2`, `3` roughly one second apart, then exits cleanly: when the thread finishes, `tx` is dropped, the channel closes, `rx.recv().await` resolves to `None`, and the loop ends
options.B: It prints `1`, `2`, `3` and then hangs forever waiting for more messages
options.C: It does not compile: a blocking `thread::sleep` cannot be used in a program that also calls `trpl::block_on`
options.D: It prints nothing — futures are lazy, so the async block never receives the messages the thread already sent

questions.explanation: Threads and async work well together. The spawned thread takes ownership of `tx` via `move`, sends 1 through 3 with a blocking sleep between each, and then finishes — dropping `tx`. That closes the channel, so the awaited `recv` future resolves to `None`, the `while let` loop ends, and `block_on` returns. Blocking calls are fine on a separate thread; the async block awaits the messages just as in the chapter's other message-passing examples.

questions.prompt: The book cites the Gang of Four definition: an object packages both data and the procedures that operate on that data. By this definition, why does Rust qualify as object-oriented?

options.A: Rust has a `class` keyword that bundles fields and methods together
options.B: Structs and enums hold data, and `impl` blocks provide methods on them, packaging data and behavior together
options.C: Rust requires every type to inherit from a common base object
options.D: Rust automatically generates getters and setters for every struct field

questions.explanation: Rust has no `class` keyword and no base object. Structs and enums carry the data, and `impl` blocks define the methods that operate on that data, which satisfies the Gang of Four definition even though the book doesn't call them "objects".

questions.prompt: This code uses the book's `AveragedCollection`. Why does it fail to compile?

options.A: `update_average` cannot be called from `add` because it takes `&mut self`
options.B: `AveragedCollection::new` must be marked `const` to be usable in `main`
options.C: `c.list.push(20)` accesses the private field `list` from outside its module; fields are private by default even when the struct is `pub`
options.D: `average` and the field `self.average` conflict, so the method shadows the field

questions.explanation: Marking the struct `pub` does not expose its fields; each field stays private unless individually marked `pub`. This is Rust's encapsulation: outside code must go through `add`/`remove`/`average`, so the cached `average` can never get out of sync with `list`. `c.list.push(20)` fails with E0616 (field is private). A method and a field may share a name, so D is not an error.

questions.prompt: A developer coming from Java asks how to make a `struct` inherit fields and methods from a parent `struct`. What does the book say?

options.A: Use the `#[derive(Inherit)]` attribute to copy the parent's fields and methods
options.B: Rust has no inheritance; for code reuse it offers default trait method implementations, and for polymorphism it offers generics and trait objects
options.C: Embed the parent struct as the first field and Rust will automatically delegate all method calls to it
options.D: Implement `Deref` to the parent type, which the book presents as Rust's official inheritance mechanism

questions.explanation: There is no way to define a struct that inherits another struct's fields and method implementations (without macros). The book's alternatives are default method implementations in traits (reuse of behavior) and generics with trait bounds or trait objects (polymorphism). Embedding a field does not auto-delegate, and using `Deref` to fake inheritance is an anti-pattern, not a book recommendation.

questions.prompt: Select ALL statements that correctly describe how Rust relates to classic object-oriented characteristics.

options.A: Structs and enums with `impl` blocks package data and behavior, satisfying the Gang of Four definition of objects
options.B: Struct fields are private by default, and the `pub` keyword opts specific items into the public API, which provides encapsulation
options.C: A struct can inherit the fields of another struct by listing it after a colon, as in `struct Child: Parent`
options.D: Default trait method implementations give a limited form of code sharing similar to a subclass inheriting a method it can also override
options.E: The only form of polymorphism Rust supports is dynamic dispatch through trait objects

questions.explanation: A, B, and D are exactly the book's points. C is not Rust syntax; there is no struct inheritance. E is wrong because Rust also has compile-time polymorphism: generics with trait bounds, which the book calls bounded parametric polymorphism.

questions.section: 18.2 Using Trait Objects That Allow for Values of Different Types
questions.prompt: In the type `Box<dyn Draw>`, what does the `dyn Draw` part denote?

options.A: A trait object: a value of any type that implements `Draw`, used behind a pointer, with the concrete method found at runtime
options.B: A generic type parameter that the compiler replaces with one concrete type at compile time
options.C: A special struct named `Draw` that the compiler generates automatically
options.D: A dynamically sized array of values that implement `Draw`

questions.explanation: A trait object points to an instance of some type implementing the trait plus a table used to look up that type's trait methods at runtime. Trait objects must live behind a pointer such as `Box<dyn Draw>` or `&dyn Draw`. Unlike a generic parameter (B), the concrete type is not fixed at compile time.

questions.section: 18.2 Using Trait Objects That Allow for Values of Different Types
questions.prompt: The book defines `Screen` with `components: Vec<Box<dyn Draw>>` instead of a generic `Screen<T: Draw>` with `components: Vec<T>`. What is the key capability the trait-object version gains?

options.A: It compiles faster because the compiler skips type checking of the components
options.B: One `Screen` can hold a mix of different concrete types (e.g., `Button` and `SelectBox`) in the same vector, whereas the generic version is substituted with a single concrete type, so its vector is homogeneous
options.C: It allows the components to be modified without `&mut`, since trait objects have interior mutability
options.D: It makes every `draw` call faster because the vtable caches results

questions.explanation: A generic parameter is monomorphized to exactly one concrete type per instantiation, so `Screen<Button>` could only ever hold `Button`s. `Vec<Box<dyn Draw>>` is a heterogeneous collection: each element can be a different concrete type as long as it implements `Draw`. The trade-off is runtime dispatch, not faster compilation or faster calls.

questions.section: 18.2 Using Trait Objects That Allow for Values of Different Types
questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `BSS`
options.B: `SBS`
options.C: It does not compile: a `Vec` cannot contain two different concrete types
options.D: It does not compile: `draw` must be called as `Draw::draw(c)` on trait objects

questions.explanation: Each `Box<dyn Draw>` erases the concrete type, so the vector legally mixes `Slider` and `Button`. At runtime, dynamic dispatch selects each element's own `draw` implementation in iteration order: `Slider`, `Button`, `Slider`, printing `SBS`. Method-call syntax works fine on trait objects.

questions.section: 18.2 Using Trait Objects That Allow for Values of Different Types
questions.prompt: Using the book's `Screen`, why does this code fail to compile?

options.A: `String` does not implement `Draw`, so `Box<String>` cannot be coerced to `Box<dyn Draw>` (error E0277)
options.B: `String` is dynamically sized, so it can never be placed in a `Box`
options.C: The `vec!` macro cannot construct a vector of boxed values
options.D: `components` is `pub`, so it can only be initialized from another module

questions.explanation: Trait objects accept any concrete type, but only types that actually implement the trait. `String` has no `Draw` impl, so the compiler rejects the coercion with "the trait bound `String: Draw` is not satisfied" (E0277). This check happens at compile time, so you never get a runtime "method not found" error. Boxing a `String` is otherwise perfectly legal.

questions.section: 18.2 Using Trait Objects That Allow for Values of Different Types
questions.prompt: Compare `fn show<T: Draw>(item: &T)` with `fn show(item: &dyn Draw)`. Which statement correctly describes how each call is dispatched?

options.A: Both use dynamic dispatch, because `Draw` is a trait in both signatures
options.B: The generic version is monomorphized: the compiler emits specialized code per concrete type and uses static dispatch; the `&dyn Draw` version looks up the method at runtime (dynamic dispatch), which has a runtime cost and prevents inlining
options.C: The generic version decides the method at runtime, while `&dyn Draw` is resolved at compile time
options.D: There is no difference: the compiler always optimizes both into direct calls

questions.explanation: Generics undergo monomorphization: nongeneric copies are generated for each concrete type used, so the exact method is known at compile time (static dispatch) and can be inlined. With `dyn Draw` the compiler cannot know the concrete type, so it emits a runtime lookup through the trait object's method table, which costs a lookup and blocks inlining and related optimizations.

questions.section: 18.2 Using Trait Objects That Allow for Values of Different Types
questions.prompt: This code fails to compile at the function signature. Why?

options.A: `name` returns an owned `String`, which trait objects cannot do
options.B: A trait used as a trait object may not have more than one method
options.C: `duplicate` returns `Self`, so `Widget` is not dyn compatible (not object safe): once the concrete type is erased, the compiler cannot know what `Self` is
options.D: `Vec` cannot store `Box` values; `Rc<dyn Widget>` is required

questions.explanation: To be usable as `dyn Widget`, a trait must be dyn compatible: at the book's level, its methods must not return `Self` and must not have generic type parameters. Behind `dyn Widget` the concrete type is erased, so a return type of `Self` is unknowable. `name` returning `String` is fine, traits may have many methods, and `Vec<Box<...>>` is a normal type.

questions.section: 18.2 Using Trait Objects That Allow for Values of Different Types
questions.prompt: Select ALL statements that are true about trait objects and generics in Rust.

options.A: A `Vec<Box<dyn Draw>>` can hold values of several different concrete types at the same time
options.B: A generic `Vec<T>` where `T: Draw` can mix `Button` and `SelectBox` values in one vector
options.C: Method calls on a trait object are resolved at runtime by looking the method up in a table of function pointers
options.D: Monomorphization generates a specialized, nongeneric copy of generic code for each concrete type it is used with
options.E: Dynamic dispatch enables the compiler to inline trait-object method calls more aggressively than static dispatch

questions.explanation: A: trait objects allow heterogeneous collections. C: a trait object carries a pointer to the value plus a table used to find the right method at runtime. D: that is the definition of monomorphization. B is false because `T` is substituted with exactly one concrete type, so the vector is homogeneous. E is backwards: dynamic dispatch prevents inlining because the target is unknown at compile time.

questions.prompt: In the blog example, `Post` stores `state: Option<Box<dyn State>>`, and `request_review` calls `self.state.take()`. Why is `take` needed?

options.A: `take` clones the state so the old state and the new state can coexist
options.B: `State::request_review` takes `self: Box<Self>` and consumes the old state; `take` moves the `Box` out of the field (leaving `None`) because Rust won't let you move a value out of a struct behind `&mut self`
options.C: `take` is required to free the old state's memory before the new state is allocated
options.D: `take` switches the call from dynamic dispatch to static dispatch for performance

questions.explanation: The transition methods consume the boxed state (`self: Box<Self>`) so the old state value is invalidated. Rust forbids moving a field out of a borrowed struct, so the field is `Option` and `take()` swaps in `None`, yielding ownership of the `Box`. The result of `s.request_review()` is then stored back with `Some(...)`. No cloning happens, and dispatch is still dynamic.

questions.prompt: Using the book's completed state-pattern `Post` (Draft -> PendingReview -> Published), what does this program print?

options.A: `[Hello]` then `[Hello]` then `[Hello]`
options.B: `[]` then `[]` then `[Hello]`
options.C: `[]` then `[Hello]` then `[Hello]`
options.D: It panics on the first call to `content` because the post is still a draft

questions.explanation: The `State` trait's `content` method has a default implementation returning `""`, which `Draft` and `PendingReview` use; only `Published` overrides it to return `&post.content`. So the text is invisible while the post is in `Draft` and `PendingReview`, and appears only after `approve` transitions the state to `Published`. Nothing panics; `content` on a draft just returns the empty string.

questions.prompt: Still using the book's state-pattern `Post`: `approve` on the `Draft` state returns `self` unchanged. What does this print?

options.A: `[Draft text]`
options.C: It panics because `approve` is invalid in the `Draft` state
options.D: It does not compile because `approve` may only be called after `request_review`

questions.explanation: `Draft::approve` returns `self`, so approving a draft is a silent no-op and the post stays in `Draft`, whose `content` is the default `""`. This is exactly the behavior the pattern encodes: invalid transitions do nothing at runtime rather than being rejected. The compiler cannot enforce call order here, which motivates the alternative of encoding states as types.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a genuine downside of the trait-object state pattern that the book calls out?

options.A: The states are coupled to one another: `PendingReview` itself creates `Published`, so inserting a new state (e.g., `Scheduled`) between them requires changing existing state code; there is also duplicated transition logic
options.B: It requires `unsafe` code to move the state between transitions
options.C: Each state must be defined in a separate crate to satisfy the orphan rule
options.D: Adding a new operation to `Post` requires editing every `match` on the state enum

questions.explanation: The book lists coupling between states (each state names its successor) and logic duplication (the similar `take`-delegate-restore bodies on `Post`, and states returning `self`, which can't be a default method because the trait wouldn't be dyn compatible). No `unsafe` or separate crates are involved, and there is no state enum or `match` in this implementation, which is precisely the point of the pattern.

questions.prompt: In the book's alternative that encodes states as separate types (`DraftPost`, `PendingReviewPost`, `Post`), a teammate claims: "If you forget to call `approve` and try `content()` on a `PendingReviewPost`, the program panics at runtime." What actually happens?

options.A: It panics, exactly as the teammate says
options.B: It compiles and returns an empty string, like the trait-object version
options.C: It fails to compile: `PendingReviewPost` simply has no `content` method, so the invalid use is impossible to express; transitions consume `self` and return the next type, moving the checks to compile time
options.D: It compiles, but `content` returns `None` because the state is still pending

questions.explanation: Encoding states as types makes invalid states and transitions unrepresentable: only the published `Post` type defines `content`, and `request_review`/`approve` take `self` by value, consuming the old state so it cannot be reused. The cost is that callers must rebind (`let post = post.request_review();`) and the workflow is no longer fully encapsulated inside one type, but bugs like this one become compile-time errors instead of runtime behavior.

questions.section: 19.1 All the Places Patterns Can Be Used
questions.prompt: Which of the following are places where patterns can be used in Rust? Select all that apply.

options.A: The arms of a `match` expression
options.B: `while let` conditional loops
options.C: Function parameters, e.g. `fn print_coordinates(&(x, y): &(i32, i32))`
options.D: The loop variable of a `for` loop, e.g. `for (index, value) in v.iter().enumerate()`
options.E: The operands of arithmetic operators like `+`

questions.explanation: Patterns appear in `match` arms, `if let` and `while let` expressions, `for` loop bindings, `let` statements, and function or closure parameters (which can destructure tuples directly). Operands of arithmetic operators are ordinary expressions, not pattern positions.

questions.section: 19.1 All the Places Patterns Can Be Used
questions.prompt: Consider the statement `let x = 5;`. Which statement about `let` and patterns is true?

options.A: `let` statements do not involve patterns; only `match` expressions use patterns
options.B: Every `let` statement has the form `let PATTERN = EXPRESSION;` — in `let x = 5;` the name `x` is itself an irrefutable pattern that binds the whole value
options.C: Patterns in `let` only work with tuples, like `let (a, b) = (1, 2);`
options.D: `let x = 5;` uses a pattern only if you add an explicit type annotation

questions.explanation: A plain variable name is a pattern that matches anything and binds the entire value to that name. That is why destructuring like `let (x, y, z) = (1, 2, 3);` works: it is the same `let PATTERN = EXPRESSION;` form. Function parameters are patterns in exactly the same way.

questions.section: 19.1 All the Places Patterns Can Be Used
questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: Tuesday is green day!
options.B: Using purple
options.C: Using orange
options.D: Using blue

questions.explanation: The chain mixes `if let`, `else if`, and `else if let` with plain boolean conditions. `favorite_color` is `None` (first branch skipped), `is_tuesday` is false (second skipped), and `"34".parse()` yields `Ok(34)`, so the shadowed `age` is `34`, which is greater than 30 — printing `Using purple`. Note the compiler does not check `if let` chains for exhaustiveness.

questions.section: 19.1 All the Places Patterns Can Be Used
questions.prompt: What does this loop print?

options.A: `1`, `2`, `3` (each on its own line)
options.B: `3`, `2`, `1` (each on its own line)
options.C: It loops forever because the pattern always matches
options.D: Compile error: `while let` cannot use the refutable pattern `Some(top)`

questions.explanation: `while let` runs as long as the pattern keeps matching. `pop` removes elements from the end of the vector, returning `Some(3)`, `Some(2)`, `Some(1)`, and finally `None`, which fails to match `Some(top)` and ends the loop. Refutable patterns are exactly what `while let` is for.

questions.section: 19.1 All the Places Patterns Can Be Used
questions.prompt: What is the first line this program prints?

options.A: a is at index 0
options.B: 0 is at index a
options.C: a is at index 1
options.D: Compile error: a `for` loop variable cannot destructure a tuple

questions.explanation: The position after `for` is a pattern. `enumerate` produces tuples `(index, item)`, so the pattern `(index, value)` destructures each tuple: the first item is `(0, 'a')`, printing `a is at index 0`.

questions.section: 19.2 Refutability: Whether a Pattern Might Fail to Match
questions.prompt: Patterns are either refutable (can fail to match, like `Some(x)`) or irrefutable (match anything, like `x`). Which constructs accept only irrefutable patterns?

options.A: `match` arms, `if let`, and `while let`
options.B: `let` statements, function parameters, and `for` loops
options.C: Only `match` expressions
options.D: Only closure parameters

questions.explanation: `let` statements, function parameters, and `for` loops can't do anything meaningful when a pattern fails to match, so they require irrefutable patterns. `if let`, `while let`, and `let...else` are designed to handle possible match failure, so they accept refutable patterns.

questions.section: 19.2 Refutability: Whether a Pattern Might Fail to Match
questions.prompt: This program does not compile. What is the bug?

options.A: `Some(x)` needs an explicit type annotation to compile
options.B: `let` requires an irrefutable pattern, but `Some(x)` is refutable — it doesn't cover `None` (error E0005); use `let...else` or `if let` instead
options.C: You cannot destructure an `Option` with a pattern; you must call `.unwrap()`
options.D: It compiles fine but panics at runtime because the value is `None`

questions.explanation: The compiler rejects this with `error[E0005]: refutable pattern in local binding` because `let` has nowhere to go if the value is `None`. Fix it with `let Some(x) = some_option_value else { return; };` or `if let Some(x) = some_option_value { ... }`, both of which handle the non-matching case.

questions.section: 19.2 Refutability: Whether a Pattern Might Fail to Match
questions.prompt: What happens when you compile and run this program?

options.A: Compile error: `if let` requires a refutable pattern
options.B: It compiles and prints `5`, but the compiler emits a warning that the `if let` pattern is irrefutable
options.C: It compiles and runs silently with no warning
options.D: It panics at runtime

questions.explanation: Giving `if let` (or `let...else`) a pattern that always matches, like a plain variable name `x`, is accepted but pointless — the conditional adds nothing — so the compiler warns about an irrefutable pattern. The block still runs and prints `5`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `Matched, y = 10` then `at the end: x = Some(5), y = 10`
options.B: `Matched, y = 5` then `at the end: x = Some(5), y = 10`
options.C: `Matched, y = 5` then `at the end: x = Some(5), y = 5`
options.D: `Default case, x = Some(5)` then `at the end: x = Some(5), y = 10`

questions.explanation: Named variables in patterns are new bindings. `Some(y)` in the second arm does NOT compare against the outer `y = 10`; it introduces a fresh `y` that shadows it and binds whatever is inside the `Some` — here `5`. The shadow ends with the arm, so the final line still sees the outer `y = 10`.

questions.prompt: An outer variable `y` already exists, and a `match` arm uses the pattern `Some(y)`. Which statements are true? Select all that apply.

options.A: The pattern introduces a brand-new variable `y` that shadows the outer `y` inside the arm
options.B: The new `y` binds the value found inside the `Some`, regardless of the outer `y`'s value
options.C: To test the inner value against the outer `y`, you can use a match guard such as `Some(n) if n == y`
options.D: The shadowing ends when the match arm's scope ends; the outer `y` is unchanged afterwards
options.E: `Some(y)` only matches when the value equals `Some(10)` if the outer `y` is `10`

questions.explanation: Inside patterns, a bare name always creates a new binding — it never compares against an existing variable, so E is the classic misconception. Because a guard's `if` expression is not a pattern, it can refer to the outer `y`, which is how you compare against it: `Some(n) if n == y`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: one or two
options.B: three through five
options.C: anything
options.D: Compile error: ranges are not allowed in patterns

questions.explanation: `|` is the or operator in patterns (`1 | 2` matches either literal), and `3..=5` is an inclusive range pattern that matches 3, 4, or 5. Range patterns also work with `char`, e.g. `'a'..='j'`. Since `x` is 5, the second arm matches.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: On the x axis at 0
options.B: On the y axis at 7
options.C: On neither axis: (0, 7)
options.D: Compile error: a struct pattern cannot mix shorthand fields and literal values

questions.explanation: Struct patterns can mix shorthand bindings (`x` creates a variable from field `x`) with literal tests (`y: 0` requires the field to equal 0). The first arm needs `y == 0`, which fails since `y` is 7. The second arm needs `x == 0`, which succeeds and binds `y = 7`. Arms are tried in order.

questions.prompt: What is wrong with this code?

options.A: Nothing — it compiles and prints `Some numbers: 4`
options.B: Compile error: `..` can only be used once per tuple pattern — using it on both sides of `second` is ambiguous about how many values to skip
options.C: Compile error: tuples cannot be matched with `..` at all
options.D: It compiles and prints `Some numbers: 8` because `second` grabs the middle element

questions.explanation: `..` ignores 'the rest' of the values, but Rust must be able to tell unambiguously which values it covers. `(first, .., last)` is fine because `..` appears once, but `(.., second, ..)` is rejected with an error that `..` can only be used once per tuple pattern.

questions.prompt: This program fails to compile. Why?

options.A: It actually compiles and prints `found a string` then `Some("Hello!")`
options.B: Unlike `_`, the name `_s` still binds the value — it moves the `String` out of `s`, so the later `println!("{s:?}")` uses a moved value; write `Some(_)` (which never binds) or match on `&s`
options.C: `_s` is not a valid identifier because it starts with an underscore
options.D: It compiles but panics at runtime when printing `s`

questions.explanation: An underscore-prefixed name like `_s` only silences the unused-variable warning; it is still a real binding, so ownership of the `String` moves into it and `s` becomes unusable. The bare wildcard `_` never binds at all, so `if let Some(_) = s` leaves `s` intact and the program compiles.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: yes
options.B: no
options.C: Compile error: match guards cannot be combined with `|` patterns
options.D: `yes` followed by `no`

questions.explanation: A match guard applies to the entire `|` chain: the arm behaves like `(4 | 5 | 6) if y`, not `4 | 5 | (6 if y)`. Even though `x` is 4 and matches the pattern, the guard `if y` is false, so the arm is rejected and the `_` arm prints `no`.

questions.prompt: Match guards (an extra `if` condition after a pattern, like `Some(x) if x % 2 == 0`) add expressive power. What is their downside?

options.A: A guard makes the arm's pattern irrefutable
options.B: The compiler does not take guard conditions into account when checking exhaustiveness, so a `match` relying on guards still needs arms that cover all cases without them
options.C: Guards may only refer to variables bound inside the pattern, never to outer variables
options.D: The guard runs after the arm body has already executed

questions.explanation: Guards can express conditions patterns alone cannot (including comparing against outer variables, which sidesteps the shadowing problem), but the compiler cannot reason about arbitrary `if` expressions, so guarded arms don't count toward exhaustiveness checking. The guard is evaluated after the pattern matches and before the body runs.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: Found an id in range: 5
options.B: Found an id in another range
options.C: Some other id: 5
options.D: Compile error: a binding cannot be combined with a range pattern

questions.explanation: The `@` operator both tests a value against a pattern and binds it: `id_variable @ 3..=7` matches because 5 is in the range, and captures 5 in `id_variable`. By contrast, the second arm's plain `10..=12` tests the field but binds nothing, so its body could not use the id value.

questions.prompt: Which statements about destructuring patterns are true? Select all that apply.

options.A: The struct field shorthand `Point { x, y }` creates variables named `x` and `y` from the corresponding fields
options.B: Enum variants are destructured with patterns that mirror the variant's shape, e.g. `Message::ChangeColor(r, g, b)` for a tuple-like variant and `Message::Move { x, y }` for a struct-like one
options.C: Patterns can be nested, e.g. `Message::ChangeColor(Color::Hsv(h, s, v))` matches one enum stored inside another enum's variant
options.D: `Point { x, .. }` binds `x` and ignores all remaining fields of the struct
options.E: Destructuring works for structs and enums but not for tuples nested inside them

questions.explanation: Patterns compose: structs, enums, tuples, and references can all be destructured and nested arbitrarily, such as `let ((feet, inches), Point { x, y }) = ...`, so E is false. `..` inside a struct pattern skips whatever fields are not named, and the shorthand avoids writing `x: x`.

questions.prompt: Which of the following operations require an `unsafe` block (or an `unsafe fn`)? Select all that apply.

options.A: Dereferencing a raw pointer such as `*const i32`
options.B: Calling an `unsafe` function or method
options.C: Creating a raw pointer from a reference with `&raw const num`
options.D: Reading or modifying a mutable static variable (`static mut`)
options.E: Accessing a field of a `union`

questions.explanation: The five unsafe superpowers are: dereference raw pointers, call unsafe functions, access or modify mutable statics, implement unsafe traits, and access union fields. Creating a raw pointer (C) is perfectly safe -- only dereferencing it requires `unsafe`.

questions.prompt: A colleague claims: "Inside an `unsafe` block, Rust turns off the borrow checker, so I can create two mutable references to the same value." What is true?

options.A: Correct -- `unsafe` disables all compiler safety checks inside the block
options.B: Incorrect -- `unsafe` does not turn off the borrow checker; references are still fully checked, and only the five extra operations become available
options.C: Correct, but only for references to local variables, not to heap data
options.D: Incorrect -- `unsafe` disables the borrow checker only for `'static` references

questions.explanation: `unsafe` does not disable the borrow checker or any other safety checks: if you use a reference in unsafe code, it is still checked. The keyword only grants access to the five superpowers that the compiler cannot verify for memory safety. To alias mutable data you would need raw pointers, not references.

questions.prompt: What is the result of compiling and running this program?

options.A: Prints `5`
options.B: Prints `10`
options.C: Compile error: cannot have a `*const` and a `*mut` pointer to `num` at the same time
options.D: Compiles, but dereferencing `r1` is undefined behavior because `r2` exists

questions.explanation: Creating both raw pointers is safe: raw pointers are exempt from the borrow-checking rules that forbid a simultaneous immutable and mutable borrow. Inside the `unsafe` block, `*r2 = 10` writes through the mutable raw pointer, then `*r1` reads the same memory, printing `10`. Both pointers derive from a valid local, so this is well defined.

questions.prompt: Why does this program fail to compile?

options.A: `unsafe fn` may only be defined inside an `unsafe` module
options.B: An `unsafe` function must take at least one raw-pointer parameter
options.C: Calling an `unsafe` function must happen inside an `unsafe` block (or another unsafe context), so `dangerous()` in `main` is rejected
options.D: The body of `dangerous` is empty, and empty unsafe functions are forbidden

questions.explanation: Calling an unsafe function is one of the five superpowers, so it requires an unsafe context: `unsafe { dangerous(); }`. By calling it inside `unsafe`, you assert that you have read the function's contract and are upholding its safety requirements.

questions.prompt: The standard library's `split_at_mut` returns two mutable slices into one slice. Its implementation uses raw pointers and `slice::from_raw_parts_mut` inside `unsafe`, yet the function itself is safe to call. Why is this a valid "safe abstraction"?

options.A: The borrow checker cannot see that the two returned slices never overlap; the `assert!(mid <= len)` plus the pointer arithmetic guarantee both slices are in bounds and disjoint, so no caller can trigger undefined behavior
options.B: Marking a function safe automatically makes any `unsafe` code inside it sound, as long as it compiles
options.C: `slice::from_raw_parts_mut` performs its own runtime bounds checking, so the `unsafe` block is just a formality
options.D: It is not actually valid -- a purely safe implementation with two indexing expressions would also compile and should be preferred

questions.explanation: A purely safe implementation fails to compile (E0499) because the borrow checker only sees two mutable borrows of the same slice; it cannot understand that `[..mid]` and `[mid..]` are disjoint. The unsafe implementation is sound because `assert!(mid <= len)` guarantees the raw pointers stay in bounds and the two ranges cannot overlap. Wrapping verified unsafe code in a safe API is exactly how safe abstractions work.

questions.prompt: Consider this foreign function declaration. Which statement is true?

options.A: `"C"` names the application binary interface (ABI) to use, and calling `abs` requires an unsafe context because other languages do not enforce Rust's safety guarantees
options.B: `"C"` means the function's source code must be written in C89; C++ functions need `extern "C++"` and are safe to call
options.C: Functions declared in `extern` blocks are safe to call because the linker verifies their signatures at build time
options.D: `extern` blocks can only import functions; Rust functions can never be exported for C code to call

questions.explanation: `extern "C"` declares functions using the C ABI. Calling them is unsafe because foreign code does not uphold Rust's guarantees and the compiler cannot check it. The reverse direction also exists: `#[no_mangle] pub extern "C" fn ...` exports a Rust function so C code can call it, so D is false.

questions.prompt: `Iterator` is defined with an associated type (`type Item;`) instead of a generic parameter (`trait Iterator<T>`). What key difference motivates that choice?

options.A: Associated types allow one type to implement the trait many times with different `Item` types
options.B: With an associated type, a type can implement `Iterator` only once, fixing `Item` in that single impl, so callers never need annotations to disambiguate which implementation to use
options.C: Generic parameters on traits are deprecated and will be removed in a future edition
options.D: Associated types are resolved at runtime via vtables, while generic parameters are resolved at compile time

questions.explanation: With `trait Iterator<T>`, `Counter` could implement both `Iterator<u32>` and `Iterator<String>`, forcing callers to annotate which one they mean on every `next` call. An associated type permits only one `impl Iterator for Counter`, which fixes `type Item = u32` once, so the item type is unambiguous. A is backwards, and both mechanisms are compile-time constructs.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.C: Compile error: `Add` can only be implemented with `Rhs = Self`

questions.explanation: `Add` is declared as `trait Add<Rhs = Self>`, so `Self` is only the default -- overriding it with `impl Add<Meters> for Millimeters` is exactly the customization the default type parameter enables. The impl converts meters to millimeters: `500 + 2 * 1000 = 2500`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `waving waving waving `
options.B: `captain up waving `
options.C: Compile error: `Human` cannot define `fly` when two traits already provide it
options.D: `captain up ` followed by a compile error on `person.fly()` because the call is ambiguous

questions.explanation: Because `fly` takes `self`, `Trait::fly(&person)` disambiguates by trait, printing `captain ` then `up `. A plain method call `person.fly()` prefers the inherent impl over trait methods, printing `waving `. All three coexist legally; nothing is ambiguous here.

questions.prompt: This program fails to compile at the `println!` line. What is wrong, and what is the fix?

options.A: `baby_name` has no `self` parameter, so Rust cannot tell which implementor of `Animal` is meant; use fully qualified syntax: `<Dog as Animal>::baby_name()`
options.B: A type may not have both an inherent function and a trait function with the same name; rename one of them
options.C: Associated functions in traits cannot return `String`; return `&'static str` instead
options.D: `Animal::baby_name()` works, but only after adding `use Animal;` inside `main`

questions.explanation: Associated functions without a `self` receiver give the compiler no way to infer the implementing type -- other types could also implement `Animal`. Fully qualified syntax `<Dog as Animal>::baby_name()` names both the type and the trait, and prints `puppy`. (`Dog::baby_name()` would call the inherent function and print `Spot`.)

questions.prompt: Why does this code fail to compile?

options.A: A trait cannot have a default method body that calls `to_string`
options.B: `OutlinePrint` declares `fmt::Display` as a supertrait, but `Point` does not implement `Display`, so the `impl OutlinePrint for Point` is rejected
options.C: `impl OutlinePrint for Point {}` is empty, and empty impl blocks are not allowed
options.D: Supertrait syntax requires `where Self: fmt::Display` instead of a colon

questions.explanation: `trait OutlinePrint: fmt::Display` means every implementor must also implement `Display`; that supertrait bound is what lets the default body call `self.to_string()`. Since `Point` lacks a `Display` impl, the compiler reports that the trait bound `Point: std::fmt::Display` is not satisfied. Adding `impl fmt::Display for Point` fixes it. Empty impl blocks are fine, and the colon syntax is correct.

questions.prompt: You want `Display` output for `Vec<String>`, but both `Display` and `Vec<String>` are defined outside your crate, so the orphan rule blocks `impl Display for Vec<String>`. How does the newtype pattern solve this?

options.A: `Wrapper` is a local type, so implementing the external trait `Display` on it satisfies the orphan rule; the inner vector is reached via `self.0`, and the wrapper adds no runtime overhead
options.B: The newtype tells the compiler to treat `Vec<String>` as if it were declared in the current crate, so the orphan rule no longer applies to `Vec<String>` itself
options.C: It works, but each access to the inner `Vec` goes through an extra heap indirection at runtime
options.D: `Wrapper` automatically inherits every method of `Vec<String>`, including `push` and `len`

questions.explanation: The orphan rule requires the trait or the type to be local; `Wrapper` is a local tuple struct, so the impl is allowed. Newtypes are zero-cost -- the wrapper is elided at compile time. The downside is that `Wrapper` does NOT automatically expose `Vec`'s methods (D is false); you must delegate manually or implement `Deref`.

questions.prompt: Given `type Kilometers = i32;`, a developer assumes the compiler will now reject `fn add_miles(m: i32)` being called with a `Kilometers` value, catching unit mix-ups. What is true?

options.A: Correct -- a type alias creates a distinct new type, exactly like a tuple struct
options.B: Incorrect -- a type alias is only a synonym: `Kilometers` and `i32` are the same type and fully interchangeable, so no unit confusion is caught; a newtype like `struct Kilometers(i32)` would be needed for that
options.C: Correct, but only when the alias is declared `pub`
options.D: Incorrect -- aliases do create new types, but the coercion rules silently convert between them

questions.explanation: `type` creates a synonym, not a new type. Values of type `Kilometers` are treated identically to `i32` everywhere, so mixing them passes type checking. Aliases exist to reduce repetition of long types (e.g. `type Thunk = Box<dyn Fn() + Send + 'static>`), not to add type safety. For safety you want the newtype pattern.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.B: Compile error: cannot add `i32` to `Kilometers`
options.C: Compile error: type aliases may not be declared inside a function

questions.explanation: Because `Kilometers` is an alias, `y` really has type `i32`, so `x + y` is ordinary `i32` addition and prints `x + y = 10`. Type aliases can be declared inside functions.

questions.prompt: Which statements about the never type `!` and dynamically sized types (DSTs) are true? Select all that apply.

options.A: An expression of type `!` can be coerced into any other type, which is why one `match` arm can be `continue` while another produces a `u32`
options.B: `continue`, `panic!`, and a `loop` with no `break` all have type `!`
options.C: `str` (the type itself, not `&str`) has a size known at compile time
options.D: `&str` is a "fat pointer": it stores the address of the string data plus its length
options.E: Writing `fn generic<T: ?Sized>(t: &T)` allows `T` to be a DST, but the parameter must then sit behind some pointer type such as `&T`

questions.explanation: `!` has no values, so it can never actually produce one -- the compiler lets it coerce into whatever type the context demands, which is why `Err(_) => continue` type-checks alongside `Ok(num) => num`. `str` alone is a DST (its length varies per value), so C is false: you must use it behind a pointer like `&str` or `Box<str>`. `&str` stores address + length, and `?Sized` relaxes the implicit `Sized` bound while requiring indirection.

questions.prompt: Why does this code fail to compile?

options.A: String literals have type `String`, so the annotation should be `String`
options.B: `str` is a dynamically sized type: its size is not known at compile time, so a bare local variable cannot have type `str`; use `&str`, which is a fat pointer with a known size
options.C: Two `str` variables cannot exist in the same scope because they would alias the same string table
options.D: The code compiles; `str` and `&str` are interchangeable

questions.explanation: Every value of a given type must occupy the same amount of memory, but `"Hello there!"` and "How's it going?" would need 12 and 15 bytes respectively, so `str` cannot be stored directly. The golden rule for DSTs: always put them behind a pointer -- `&str`, `Box<str>`, `Rc<str>`, etc. String literals have type `&'static str`, not `String`.

questions.prompt: Given `fn do_twice(f: fn(i32) -> i32, arg: i32) -> i32` and `fn add_one(x: i32) -> i32 { x + 1 }`, which statement about the function pointer type `fn` is true?

options.A: `fn` is a trait, so `do_twice` uses dynamic dispatch to call `f`
options.B: `fn(i32) -> i32` is a concrete type that implements all three closure traits (`Fn`, `FnMut`, `FnOnce`), so a function pointer can always be passed where a closure trait bound is expected -- but a capturing closure cannot be passed as `fn`
options.C: Closures and function pointers are the same type, so `do_twice` accepts any closure, including capturing ones
options.D: Function pointers only implement `FnOnce`, so `do_twice` could call `f` at most once

questions.explanation: `fn` (lowercase) is a concrete type, not a trait. Function pointers implement `Fn`, `FnMut`, and `FnOnce`, so APIs with generic closure bounds also accept plain functions (e.g. `map(ToString::to_string)`). The reverse is limited: only non-capturing closures coerce to `fn`. That is why accepting `impl Fn(i32) -> i32` is usually preferred unless you need `fn` for C interop.

questions.prompt: This function fails to compile. What is the problem?

options.A: The closure captures `a` from its environment, so it cannot coerce to a plain function pointer; return `impl Fn(i32) -> i32` (or `Box<dyn Fn(i32) -> i32>` when multiple call sites must share one type) instead
options.B: `move` is illegal in a return position; deleting `move` makes it compile
options.C: Closures can never be returned from functions in Rust
options.D: The return type must be spelled `Fn(i32) -> i32` with a capital F

questions.explanation: Each closure has its own anonymous type; only closures that capture nothing can coerce to a `fn` pointer, and this one captures `a`. Returning `impl Fn(i32) -> i32` works for one closure per function; if you need to return closures from several functions and store them together (e.g. in a `Vec`), each `impl Trait` is a distinct opaque type, so use `Box<dyn Fn(i32) -> i32>`. Bare `Fn(...)` is a trait and cannot be a return type by itself.

The `$( ... )*` in the body repeats `temp_vec.push($x);` once per match, so the call expands to a block that pushes 1, 2, 3 and evaluates to the vector. The sum is 6.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are real differences between macros and functions in Rust? Select all that apply.

options.A: A macro can accept a variable number of arguments (e.g. `println!` with one or many), while a function signature fixes the number and types of parameters
options.B: Macros are expanded before the compiler interprets the meaning of the code, so a macro can generate items such as a trait implementation on a given type; a function cannot, because it is called at runtime
options.C: Macros must be defined or brought into scope before they are called in a file
options.D: Functions can be defined anywhere and called anywhere in the same scope, without ordering constraints
options.E: Macro definitions are generally simpler to read and maintain than equivalent function definitions

questions.explanation: Macros are a form of metaprogramming: they expand into code at compile time, which lets them take variable argument counts and generate items like trait impls. Unlike functions, macros must be in scope before the call site in the file. The trade-off is the opposite of E: macro definitions are more complex and harder to read and maintain than functions.

questions.prompt: What are the three kinds of procedural macros in Rust?

options.A: Custom `derive` macros, attribute-like macros, and function-like macros
options.B: Declarative macros, hygienic macros, and recursive macros
options.C: Inline macros, module macros, and crate macros
options.D: `macro_rules!` macros, `#[derive]` macros, and inline assembly macros

questions.explanation: Procedural macros come in three kinds: custom derive (used as `#[derive(HelloMacro)]` on structs and enums), attribute-like (creating new attributes such as `#[route(GET, "/")]`, usable on more item kinds), and function-like (called like `sql!(SELECT * FROM posts)`). Declarative `macro_rules!` macros are a separate, non-procedural category.

questions.prompt: A crate defines a custom derive macro as shown. What happens when a user writes `#[derive(HelloMacro)] struct Pancakes;`?

options.A: At runtime, the first call to `Pancakes::hello_macro()` invokes `hello_macro_derive` reflectively to build the implementation
options.B: At compile time, the compiler passes the tokens of the `Pancakes` definition to `hello_macro_derive`; the function parses them (typically with `syn`), generates code (typically with `quote!`), and the returned `TokenStream` is added to the user's crate as the trait implementation
options.C: The compiler copies the source text of `impl_hello_macro` into the user's crate verbatim, without running any code
options.D: It fails unless `hello_macro_derive` is defined in the same crate as `struct Pancakes`, because of the orphan rule

questions.explanation: Procedural macros are functions from `TokenStream` to `TokenStream` that the compiler runs during compilation. For a derive macro, the input is the annotated item's tokens; `syn::parse` turns them into a `DeriveInput` syntax tree, and `quote!` builds the generated impl, which the compiler appends to the user's code. Derive macros must live in their own crate with `proc-macro = true` -- typically a separate crate from the user's code, so D is wrong, and nothing happens at runtime.
